reference: – Steve Jobs revolutionized Apple in his second go-round with the company, taking the stock from a split-adjusted $6.05 per share in 1996 to $376 today. But with Jobs resigning as CEO, everyone is wondering what the future holds for Apple. Some reactions: What will change? Not much, write Nathan Olivarez-Giles and Thomas Suh Lauder for the LA Times. In his role as CEO, Jobs had to answer to two groups: shareholders, and the board of directors. Now he's at the helm of that board. It was the perfect time to go, writes Brian Barrett for Gizmodo, noting that the coming release of the iPhone 5 and more affordable iPhone 4 should buoy the company and give CEO Tim Cook a chance to step into the spotlight. "If they'd waited any longer, the iPhone 5 announcement would've been fully shrouded in memories of Jobs. ... This feels calculated, in the best possible way, to happen at the best possible time. Now." Apple has lost its core, writes Rupert Goodwins for ZDNet, and only time will tell how this plays out. The iPhone and iPad can only be updated so many times, and with Jobs "there was always a 'next.'" But "Apple as innovator without Jobs is Apple unknown." Its future "depends on the new next, and the longer we have to wait to see what happens, the less likely it will happen at all."

final_summary:
It's been one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was heading the investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

One of the most pressing questions surrounding the investigation is whether Mueller can subpoena Trump. While most legal experts agree that being president does not provide blanket immunity from a subpoena, there are still significant uncertainties and potential legal challenges. The Supreme Court has ruled in the past that a sitting president can be subject to a civil lawsuit, and a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed.

Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Jeff Sessions, who would need a valid reason to do so. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Sessions and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

In a related development, Attorney General Sessions recused himself from the investigation, allowing Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to oversee it. Former Presidents Ronald Reagan, George H.W. Bush, and George W. Bush, as well as Democratic Presidents Bill Clinton and Barack Obama, have all appointed Mueller to various roles throughout his career. 

Finally, there is speculation about Trump's potential use of pardons to evade accountability. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that it would be impermissible based on historical context and the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause." Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

history.draft:
It's been one year since Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump. Mueller's appointment followed Trump's decision to fire FBI Director James Comey, who was heading the investigation into alleged ties between Russia and the Trump campaign. Mueller, a decorated war veteran and former FBI director, was granted the authority to prosecute any federal crimes he uncovered during the investigation.

Mueller's team has remained tight-lipped about its activities, except for announcing indictments and plea deals. Despite facing intense scrutiny and criticism from Trump and his allies, who label the investigation a "witch hunt," Mueller's team has secured five guilty pleas and multiple indictments. The investigation's endgame will likely involve complex legal decisions regarding whether presidents can be subpoenaed or indicted, and whether Trump can fire Mueller or use pardons to hinder the probe.

Mueller's team consists of 17 attorney staff members, with at least 12 registered Democrats, although Mueller himself is a registered Republican. His appointment was made by Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, who was nominated by Trump and previously appointed by George W. Bush. Mueller's team is bound by Justice Department policy and the Civil Service Reform Act to avoid considering political affiliations when assembling the team.

Since his appointment, Mueller has focused on investigating allegations of obstruction of justice and has brought charges against several key figures, including Paul Manafort, his business partner Rick Gates, and former Trump campaign aide George Papadopoulos. Additionally, Mueller indicted 13 Russian nationals and three Russian entities on charges related to the 2016 election. Other notable developments include the guilty pleas of Dutch lawyer Alex van der Zwaan and former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn.

One of the most pressing questions surrounding the investigation is whether Mueller can subpoena Trump. While most legal experts agree that being president does not provide blanket immunity from a subpoena, there are still significant uncertainties and potential legal challenges. The Supreme Court has ruled in the past that a sitting president can be subject to a civil lawsuit, and a similar ruling in a criminal investigation is possible, but not guaranteed.

Another critical aspect of the investigation is whether Mueller can indict Trump. There is no clear judicial precedent on this matter, but many experts believe that courts would rule against the idea of prosecuting a sitting president. The Office of Legal Counsel has issued two opinions stating that a president cannot be criminally prosecuted, citing concerns that it would undermine the executive branch's ability to function.

Trump has considered removing Mueller, but his potential dismissal falls into a gray area governed by Justice Department regulations, congressional statutes, and constitutional law. Only the attorney general can fire Mueller, currently Acting Attorney General Jeff Sessions, who would need a valid reason to do so. Alternatively, Trump could try to fire Sessions and replace him with someone willing to dismiss Mueller, or attempt to rescind the Justice Department regulations governing Mueller's appointment.

In a related development, Attorney General Sessions recused himself from the investigation, allowing Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein to oversee it. Former Presidents Ronald Reagan, George H.W. Bush, and George W. Bush, as well as Democratic Presidents Bill Clinton and Barack Obama, have all appointed Mueller to various roles throughout his career. 

Finally, there is speculation about Trump's potential use of pardons to evade accountability. While the Constitution does not explicitly prohibit a self-pardon, experts argue that it would be impermissible based on historical context and the concept of nemo judex in causa sua, or "no one should be a judge in their own cause." Pardoning presidential associates could also be ineffective, as investigations often focus on broader patterns of wrongdoing rather than individual actions.

reference: – Thursday is the one-year anniversary of the start of Robert Mueller's investigation, and President Trump is among those marking the occasion. His tweet, however, drips with sarcasm. "Congratulations America, we are now into the second year of the greatest Witch Hunt in American History ... and there is still No Collusion and No Obstruction," he writes. "The only Collusion was that done by Democrats who were unable to win an Election despite the spending of far more money!" In another tweet, he calls the investigation "disgusting, illegal and unwarranted" and said his administration has nonetheless had "the most successful" first 17 months of any White House, "by far!" Related coverage: The basics: Mueller was appointed special counsel to investigate "any links and/or coordination between the Russian government and individuals associated with the campaign of President Donald Trump," explains PolitiFact. Mueller quickly expanded his investigation into whether Trump obstructed justice, notably by firing FBI chief James Comey. The piece hits other nuts and bolts of the investigation.

final_summary:
In a significant development, the National Liberation Front of Corsica (FLNC), a militant group seeking independence from France, has announced its intention to abandon its armed struggle after nearly 40 years. According to a statement released on Wednesday, the FLNC has decided to initiate a demilitarization process and transition into conventional politics, marking a major shift in its approach to achieving Corsican independence.

This decision follows a long history of violence and terrorism on the island of Corsica, which has been plagued by bombings, robberies, and assaults since 1976. The FLNC has claimed responsibility for numerous attacks, including the murder of policemen, bombings of banks in Paris, and rocket attacks on government buildings. One notable incident occurred in 1997 when over 50 bomb explosions targeted several banks and government offices across the island.

Located in the Tyrrhenian Sea, between the coasts of France and Italy, Corsica has been under French control since the 1700s. The island's unique position in the Mediterranean makes it a distinct cultural and historical entity. The FLNC's decision to disarm comes after a series of developments, including a bill passed in April that restricts land and property purchases to individuals who have been Corsican residents for at least five years.

Gilles Millet, a journalist at Corsica magazine, confirmed the authenticity of the 14-page communiqué issued by the FLNC. The group, comprising over 100 members, cited recent progress through conventional means as a primary reason for their decision to disarm. They believe that measures such as the property-owning restrictions "trace the outlines of a political solution" and mark a shift from a phase of combat and resistance to one of constructing a true Corsican political power.

Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio, the capital city of Corsica, played a pivotal role in shaping the island's history. His legacy continues to influence the island's identity and aspirations for self-governance. The FLNC's decision to lay down its arms may signal a new era for Corsica, one where the islanders can pursue their goals through peaceful means.

reference: – There is now one less armed separatist group in Europe: After nearly 40 years and thousands of attacks, including bombings and assassinations, the National Liberation Front of Corsica has decided to call it a day, CNN reports. The banned militant group, which launched its violent campaign for independence from France in 1976, says it has begun the process of demilitarization without preconditions and will pursue its goals through political channels. The group's cause still has plenty of support on the island of 320,000 people, where at least 40% of homes are second properties owned by non-residents, the Financial Times finds. The regional assembly recently restricted property purchases to those who have been residents for at least five years, and the militants say that move and similar ones show that "we are moving from a phase of combat and resistance to a phase of the construction of a true Corsican political power."

final_summary:
Every day, higher education institutions face the challenge of understanding the mindset of incoming college students to ensure effective teaching and learning. To address this challenge, Beloit College has been releasing the annual Mindset List since 1998, providing a snapshot of the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of students entering college each year. The list's creators, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, initially designed it as a reminder to faculty to be aware of dated references, but it soon evolved into a comprehensive catalog of the rapidly changing worldview of each new generation.

The Mindset List highlights the distinct characteristics of each class, starting with the class of 2014, born in 1992. For these students, the digital world is routine, and technology is often too slow. They have grown up in a world where email is considered fast, and they rarely use snail mail. The list reveals that few in the class know how to write in cursive, and they have never experienced a world without Korean-made cars on American highways. The class of 2014 has also grown up with the internet, with many having used computers with CD-ROM drives and being familiar with the concept of instant access to information.

Fast-forward to the class of 2021, mostly born in 1999, who have always searched for Pokémon and have never read a Peanuts strip that wasn't a repeat. They have grown up in a world without dial-up modems and have always had access to emojis. The list highlights the significant changes in technology, culture, and society that have occurred over the years.

Beloit College's Mindset List has become a valuable resource for educators, policymakers, and the general public to understand the values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation. The list's authors, Tom McBride and Ron Nief, have expanded their scope to create additional resources, including a speakers' program and a study guide to their book, "The Mindset Lists of American History."

The Mindset List has also shed light on the economic virtues of millennials, who have been shaped by their economic status and have developed unique values and behaviors as a result. According to economist Deidre McCloskey, the virtues of different social groups are strongly determined by their economic status, with humility being a dominant virtue among peasants and courage being a virtue among the aristocracy.

In addition to the Mindset List, the documents highlight the significance of technology in shaping the lives of young people. The Internet Archive, founded in 1996, has been collecting and preserving internet content, including Alexa Internet's crawl data, which is donated daily and added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This archive serves as a valuable resource for researchers and historians, providing insights into the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

As the world continues to evolve, the Mindset List remains an essential tool for understanding the complexities of each new generation. By examining the cultural touchstones that shape the lives of incoming college students, educators and policymakers can develop strategies to effectively engage and educate this diverse group of individuals. The list's creators continue to provide valuable insights into the changing values, attitudes, and experiences of each new generation, ensuring that education remains relevant and effective in the face of rapid technological and societal advancements.

reference: – US News & World Report is ready to shock us all with its Best Colleges 2011 ranking, which places Harvard smack dab on top. Again. But—news alert!—Princeton has tumbled from tying the venerable Crimson to No. 2. That list, which you can see here, doesn't wow us quite so much as does another annual list released today: Beloit College's Mindset List, which gives us a snapshot of the realities and events shaping the lives of this year's freshman class, born in 1992. Some highlights: The majority don't know how to write in cursive. They have never had trouble understanding what a "caramel macchiato" is. Clint Eastwood is some guy who directs movies. Fergie is a member of the Black Eyed Peas, not the British Royal Family. Czechoslovakia has never existed. The Post Office has always been basically broke. Click here for the complete 75-item list (Buffy and Barney and Beanie Babies, oh my!).

final_summary:
A CNN report from Islamabad, Pakistan, revealed that Railway Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour offered a $100,000 reward for the killing of the person behind an anti-Islam movie that has sparked outrage across the Muslim world. Speaking at a press conference on Saturday, Bilour clarified that he was making the offer as an individual, not as a government representative. When asked about the implications of his actions, Bilour stated that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative.

Bilour even invited the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, which drew strong criticism from within his own party, the Awami National Party. Spokesman Sen. Zahid Khan distanced the party from Bilour's comments, emphasizing its commitment to non-violence and opposition to militancy and extremism. Khan expressed concern over Bilour's statements, highlighting the party's long-standing efforts to combat these issues.

On Sunday, Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf's spokesperson condemned Bilour's actions, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. The US government also weighed in, with a state department official criticizing Bilour's decision as "inflammatory and inappropriate." The official noted that the prime minister's office had disassociated itself from the comments. While acknowledging the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," the US government emphasized that it did not justify violence and called upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

Protests broke out across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. To mitigate the situation, CNN reported that the US Embassy in Pakistan aired advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. The US also advised its citizens to exercise caution when traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. The global fallout also extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, leading to the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in around 20 countries across the Muslim world.

When questioned about the potential implications of his actions, Bilour clarified that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative.

Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf swiftly denounced Bilour's actions through his spokesperson, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. The State Department emphasized that while the US acknowledged the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," it did not justify violence and called upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

As tensions continued to escalate, protests broke out across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. The US Embassy in Pakistan took steps to mitigate the situation by airing advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. Additionally, the US advised its citizens to avoid traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. The global fallout also extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, prompting the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in approximately 20 countries across the Muslim world.

history.draft:
A CNN report from Islamabad, Pakistan, revealed that Railway Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour offered a $100,000 reward for the killing of the person behind an anti-Islam movie that has sparked outrage across the Muslim world. Speaking at a press conference on Saturday, Bilour clarified that he was making the offer as an individual, not as a government representative. When asked about the implications of his actions, Bilour stated that he considered himself a Muslim first and foremost, before being a government representative.

Bilour even invited the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, which drew strong criticism from within his own party, the Awami National Party. Spokesman Sen. Zahid Khan distanced the party from Bilour's comments, emphasizing its commitment to non-violence and opposition to militancy and extremism. Khan expressed concern over Bilour's statements, highlighting the party's long-standing efforts to combat these issues.

On Sunday, Prime Minister Raja Pervez Ashraf's spokesperson condemned Bilour's actions, stating that the premier would address the matter with the leader of the Awami National Party. The US government also weighed in, with a state department official criticizing Bilour's decision as "inflammatory and inappropriate." The official noted that the prime minister's office had disassociated itself from the comments. While acknowledging the film's content as "offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible," the US government emphasized that it did not justify violence and called upon responsible leaders to condemn such acts.

Protests broke out across various countries, including Pakistan, Nigeria, Greece, and Turkey, resulting in dozens of deaths and injuries. To mitigate the situation, CNN reported that the US Embassy in Pakistan aired advertisements featuring President Barack Obama and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton denouncing the film. The US also advised its citizens to exercise caution when traveling to Pakistan due to safety concerns. The global fallout also extended to France, where the satirical magazine Charlie Hebdo faced backlash for publishing caricatures of the Prophet Muhammad, leading to the closure of French embassies and diplomatic missions in around 20 countries across the Muslim world.

reference: – A US official has called it "inflammatory and inappropriate" for a Pakistani minister to offer a $100,000 bounty for killing the filmmaker of Innocence of Muslims. Railways Minister Ghulam Ahmad Bilour, who made the offer, said he would pay the bounty out of his own pocket as protests continued yesterday against the anti-Islamic film. Bilour, who called himself a "Muslim first and a government representative second," said he has invited the Taliban and al-Qaeda to carry out the assassination, reports CNN. Pakistani government officials have similarly blasted the offer. A US State Department official told the BBC yesterday: "The president and secretary of state have both said the video at the core of this is offensive, disgusting, and reprehensible—but that is no justification for violence, and it is important for responsible leaders to stand up and speak out against violence." In another threat, this one aimed at the French publication Charlie Hebdo for printing cartoons of the prophet Mohammed, an 18-year-old man who threatened the magazine's editors on Facebook has been charged with terrorism-related activity.

final_summary:
A recent internet trend known as the "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge has caused significant damage to a Houston resident's vehicle. According to KPRC-TV, a group of teenagers attempted to replicate a viral video by jumping onto the hood of a white BMW belonging to James McHugh, resulting in substantial damage estimated at $4,000. This incident occurred on a Friday evening when a group of seven young men were caught on McHugh's home security camera walking up to his car on Plaza del Sol Park.

The "Put 'Em in a Coffin" challenge originated in May after a member of the rap group ThotBoyz posted a video of himself performing a backward dive onto a car hood, which quickly gained over three million views on Vine. Since then, numerous copycat videos have surfaced on YouTube and other social media platforms, featuring individuals jumping onto various objects such as cars, store displays, trash bins, and even each other.

Last Friday, James McHugh's BMW and his wife's car suffered damage when a group of teenagers jumped onto the hoods, leaving a crater in the hood of his car. McHugh estimates the damage will cost him around $4,000. He expressed frustration over the incident, stating that it is infuriating to see his hard-earned money wasted for just 10 seconds of laughter.

reference: – The latest prank that all the kids are into is a little more destructive than the toilet paper of yesteryear: Called "Put 'em in a coffin" and based on a Vine video from May that's gone viral, it involves throwing yourself back-first onto the tops of cars (and grocery displays, and classroom desks, and one another, etc.). And for at least one Houston man and his wife, the damage is into the thousands. Last Friday, James McHugh, 28, found both his BMW and his wife's car seriously dented across the hoods, reports KPRC-TV, and a home security camera caught grainy footage of seven young men approaching his property. He says it'll cost about $4,000 for the repairs. "You work hard, and then to have that much money washed away for 10 seconds of laughter—it's very frustrating," McHugh says. People have been posting videos on YouTube, Vine, and other social media sites since rapper VonMar of the group ThotBoyz posted the original back in May, reports the Houston Chronicle. Since then, his video has been viewed more than 3 million times on Vine. As for McHugh, there's no word yet on whether his own security footage will help identify the vandals. (This high school prank resulted in 62 arrests.)

final_summary: In the United States, researchers at Imperial College London have made a groundbreaking discovery that sheds light on the mysterious link between dietary fibre and weight loss. According to their study, published in Nature Communications, a molecule called acetate, produced by the fermentation of fibre in the gut, plays a crucial role in suppressing appetite and promoting weight loss. The team, led by biochemist Jimmy Bell, conducted experiments on mice in the United Kingdom, feeding them a high-fat diet supplemented with inulin, a type of dietary fibre found in chicory and sugar beets, sourced from Charles River in the UK. The results showed that the mice consuming inulin ate less and gained less weight compared to those on a high-fat diet alone, which was also provided by Charles River in the UK. Further investigation revealed that the inulin triggered the production of acetate, which accumulated in the hypothalamus, a region of the brain responsible for regulating appetite, located in the UK. Using advanced imaging techniques, including positron emission tomography (PET) scans, the researchers tracked the movement of acetate from the gut to the brain, demonstrating that it played a direct role in suppressing hunger. When acetate was injected into the bloodstream, colon, or brain, it significantly reduced food intake in mice. Bell and his team propose that the widespread adoption of processed foods, low in fibre, has contributed to the rising obesity epidemic in the UK. In ancient times, humans consumed far more fibre-rich foods, which supported a healthier balance between gut bacteria and brain function. Today, the average Western diet in the UK contains only about 15 grams of fibre per day, compared to the 100 grams consumed by our ancestors. To combat this issue, the researchers are exploring ways to harness the power of acetate for therapeutic applications in the UK. They are collaborating with companies to develop novel formulations that can deliver acetate in a sustained-release format, mimicking the natural process of fibre digestion, which involves the use of 0.9% saline solution. This could potentially lead to the creation of an "acetate pill" that helps regulate appetite and supports weight management. The study's findings have sparked excitement among experts in the UK, with some cautioning that further research is needed to fully understand the complexities of the gut-brain axis. However, the implications of this breakthrough are profound, suggesting that a simple, fibre-rich diet may hold the key to addressing the global obesity crisis in the UK. In related news, a team of scientists at the University of Alberta in Canada has raised concerns about the oversimplification of the research, highlighting the intricate mechanisms governing neurotransmitter release in the hypothalamus. Nevertheless, the study's lead author, Jimmy Bell, remains optimistic that the discovery will inspire innovative solutions to tackle the growing problem of obesity in the UK. Meanwhile, researchers at various institutions, including Agilent Technologies in the US, Sigma in the US, and Siemens in Germany, are working together to advance our understanding of the gut-brain connection. Their efforts aim to unlock the secrets of acetate's role in appetite regulation and explore potential applications in the development of new treatments for obesity. In the US, researchers at Charles River are conducting parallel studies on the effects of acetate on appetite suppression, while in Canada, scientists are investigating the impact of fibre on gut health. Meanwhile, in Spain, researchers are examining the metabolic pathways involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In France, experts are analyzing the molecular mechanisms underlying acetate's action on the hypothalamus, while in the UK, researchers at Imperial College London continue to refine their understanding of the acetate-fibre connection. The collaboration among these teams highlights the international scope of the research, as scientists strive to unravel the mysteries of the gut-brain axis and uncover new avenues for treating obesity. Using cutting-edge tools like Bruker's nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, researchers are gaining insights into the biochemical processes involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In the US, researchers at Cell Signalling are developing novel assays to detect and quantify acetate levels in biological samples. As the scientific community continues to investigate the intricacies of the gut-brain axis, researchers are turning to statistical analysis software like Stata to identify patterns and correlations in the data. By combining these advances in technology and methodology, scientists hope to develop effective strategies for preventing and treating obesity, ultimately improving public health outcomes worldwide.

According to their study, published in Nature Communications, a molecule called acetate, produced by the fermentation of fibre in the gut, plays a crucial role in suppressing appetite and promoting weight loss. The team, led by biochemist Jimmy Bell, conducted experiments on mice in the United Kingdom, feeding them a high-fat diet supplemented with inulin, a type of dietary fibre found in chicory and sugar beets, sourced from Charles River in the UK. The results showed that the mice consuming inulin ate less and gained less weight compared to those on a high-fat diet alone, which was also provided by Charles River in the UK. Further investigation revealed that the inulin triggered the production of acetate, which accumulated in the hypothalamus, a region of the brain responsible for regulating appetite, located in the UK. Using advanced imaging techniques, including positron emission tomography (PET) scans, the researchers tracked the movement of acetate from the gut to the brain, demonstrating that it played a direct role in suppressing hunger. When acetate was injected into the bloodstream, colon, or brain, it significantly reduced food intake in mice. Bell and his team propose that the widespread adoption of processed foods, low in fibre, has contributed to the rising obesity epidemic in the UK. In ancient times, humans consumed far more fibre-rich foods, which supported a healthier balance between gut bacteria and brain function. Today, the average Western diet in the UK contains only about 15 grams of fibre per day, compared to the 100 grams consumed by our ancestors. To combat this issue, the researchers are exploring ways to harness the power of acetate for therapeutic applications in the UK. They are collaborating with companies to develop novel formulations that can deliver acetate in a sustained-release format, mimicking the natural process of fibre digestion, which involves the use of 0.9% saline solution. This could potentially lead to the creation of an "acetate pill" that helps regulate appetite and supports weight management. The study's findings have sparked excitement among experts in the UK, with some cautioning that further research is needed to fully understand the complexities of the gut-brain axis. However, the implications of this breakthrough are profound, suggesting that a simple, fibre-rich diet may hold the key to addressing the global obesity crisis in the UK. In related news, a team of scientists at the University of Alberta in Canada has raised concerns about the oversimplification of the research, highlighting the intricate mechanisms governing neurotransmitter release in the hypothalamus. Nevertheless, the study's lead author, Jimmy Bell, remains optimistic that the discovery will inspire innovative solutions to tackle the growing problem of obesity in the UK. Meanwhile, researchers at various institutions, including Agilent Technologies in the US, Sigma in the US, and Siemens in Germany, are working together to advance our understanding of the gut-brain connection. Their efforts aim to unlock the secrets of acetate's role in appetite regulation and explore potential applications in the development of new treatments for obesity. In the US, researchers at Charles River are conducting parallel studies on the effects of acetate on appetite suppression, while in Canada, scientists are investigating the impact of fibre on gut health. Meanwhile, in Spain, researchers are examining the metabolic pathways involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In France, experts are analyzing the molecular mechanisms underlying acetate's action on the hypothalamus, while in the UK, researchers at Imperial College London continue to refine their understanding of the acetate-fibre connection. The collaboration among these teams highlights the international scope of the research, as scientists strive to unravel the mysteries of the gut-brain axis and uncover new avenues for treating obesity. Using cutting-edge tools like Bruker's nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, researchers are gaining insights into the biochemical processes involved in acetate production and its effects on the brain. In the US, researchers at Cell Signalling are developing novel assays to detect and quantify acetate levels in biological samples. As the scientific community continues to investigate the intricacies of the gut-brain axis, researchers are turning to statistical analysis software like Stata to identify patterns and correlations in the data. By combining these advances in technology and methodology, scientists hope to develop effective strategies for preventing and treating obesity, ultimately improving public health outcomes worldwide.

reference: – Experts have long suggested that fiber keeps us feeling full because it takes a long time to digest, but new research challenges that notion—and may point the way to a new anti-hunger pill. The key is a short-chain fatty acid called acetate, which emerges as fiber is digested. Acetate then makes its way to the brain's hypothalamus, where it builds up and triggers chemical reactions that tell us we're full, Time reports. Researchers reached this conclusion by scanning the progress of a dietary fiber called inulin through the bodies of mice. Those mice who ate fatty diets rich in inulin gained less weight than mice who didn't eat inulin. But one electrophysiologist not related to the study points out that the mice were fed dietary fiber levels of about 11%. He tells Nature that at such a level, "the room would be full of mouse farts"—and perhaps the mice were eating less because they felt gassy. The researchers plan to next test the idea in humans. So could we eventually see an acetate-based diet pill? It's possible, the Telegraph suggests: "The major challenge is to develop an approach that will deliver the amount of acetate needed to suppress appetite but in a form that is acceptable and safe for humans," a researcher says.

final_summary:
HOPE, Maine — On a warm afternoon on June 3, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer, who later confirmed that the raccoon had indeed tested positive for rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. By Alex Acquisto, BDN staff writer, published June 14, 2017, updated June 22, 2017. 

Earlier this month, a Wiscasset man was bitten on both hands in Topsham by what was believed to be a rabid fox. As of June 7, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state that year, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

history.draft:
On a warm afternoon in late May or early June, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer. Subsequent testing confirmed that the raccoon had indeed contracted rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. As of June 7, 2017, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state that year, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

history.draft:
HOPE, Maine — On a warm afternoon on June 3, Rachel Borch, a resident of Hope, Maine, went for a jog on a familiar wooded trail near her home. Unbeknownst to her, the tranquility of the surroundings was disrupted by a rabid raccoon that suddenly appeared before her, baring its tiny teeth. According to Borch, the raccoon "bounded" towards her, prompting her to rip out her headphones and drop her phone on the ground. With adrenaline coursing through her veins, Borch realized the raccoon's erratic behavior indicated it was likely suffering from rabies.

As the raccoon lunged at her, Borch found herself trapped on the narrow footpath, unable to outrun the animal. In a desperate bid to defend herself, she decided to use her bare hands to fend off the attack. However, the raccoon sunk its teeth into her thumb, refusing to let go despite Borch's attempts to shake it off. The situation escalated further as the raccoon's paws scratched her arms and legs wildly, causing Borch to scream and cry out in terror.

In a moment of quick thinking, Borch spotted a nearby puddle of water on the path and seized the opportunity to subdue the raccoon. She dragged the animal into the puddle, using her thumb still lodged in its mouth, and held its head underwater until it eventually stopped struggling. After what felt like an eternity, the raccoon's movements ceased, and Borch managed to pull her thumb free from its grasp.

Shaken and hyperventilating, Borch fled the scene, leaving behind her soaked shoes and the lifeless raccoon. Her father, Brad, retrieved the animal and placed it in a dog food bag, handing it over to Heidi Blood, the local animal control officer, who later confirmed that the raccoon had indeed tested positive for rabies.

According to Blood, the presence of one infected animal in the area suggests that there may be others in the vicinity, highlighting the importance of vigilance and caution when interacting with wildlife. Blood emphasized that animals infected with rabies can exhibit unusual behavior, such as acting delirious or disoriented, and warned people and their pets to steer clear of potentially infected animals, even if they appear deceased.

Rachel Borch, who has since received multiple vaccinations and antibiotic treatments for her injuries, recounted the harrowing experience, stating that she had never imagined encountering a situation like this before. Her encounter serves as a stark reminder of the risks associated with rabies, a viral disease that affects the brain and spinal cord and can prove fatal if left untreated. By Alex Acquisto, BDN staff writer, published June 14, 2017, updated June 22, 2017. 

Earlier this month, a Wiscasset man was bitten on both hands in Topsham by what was believed to be a rabid fox. As of June 7, the Maine CDC reported 20 animals, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks, that had tested positive for rabies in the state that year, compared to 64 cases in 2016.

reference: – Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. Borch, 21, knew immediately that something was wrong with the raccoon that was charging toward her. "Imagine the Tasmanian devil," she tells the Bangor Daily News of the "terrifying" June 3 incident. Yanking out her headphones, she began "dancing" around the animal on the narrow path, but she tells the Herald she knew that, one way or another, the animal was going to bite her. She figured her hands would be the best spot, so she offered them up. The raccoon chomped down on her thumb and stayed there, scratching at her legs and arms as she screamed. Now on her knees, she spotted her phone lying submerged in a mud puddle, and had an idea: drown it. "With my thumb in its mouth, I just pushed its head down into the muck," she tells the News. When the raccoon finally stopped moving, she yanked out her finger and raced the three-quarters of a mile home. Two days later, the state confirmed the animal was infected with rabies. Borch got rabies shots, and an animal control officer adds an unpleasant footnote: The infected raccoon may not be alone. "Not to scare people," Heidi Blood tells the News, but "when there’s one, there’s typically another." (Normally nocturnal like raccoons, a crazed beaver attacked a woman paddle-boarder.)

final_summary:
In the week ending July 23, Google's new social networking service, Google+, experienced a decline in U.S. visitors, with total visits falling about 3 percent to 1.79 million compared to the prior seven days. This decrease follows a significant surge in traffic, where Google+ visits rose by 283 percent in the week ended July 16. Additionally, the average time spent on the site dropped by 10 percent to 5 minutes and 15 seconds during the same period.

The slowdown in growth raises concerns about the platform's momentum, particularly given its rapid expansion in the preceding weeks. Despite Google co-founder Larry Page announcing that Google+ had surpassed 10 million users within three weeks of its launch, the service faces challenges in retaining users and keeping them engaged. According to Experian Hitwise, which tracks web traffic, excluding mobile users and third-party application traffic, the platform's ability to sustain interest and attract repeat visitors will be crucial to its long-term success.

A closer examination of user behavior reveals that many individuals, including power users, are experiencing a decline in engagement with Google+. One blogger noted that their usage has decreased significantly, loading the site only twice a day due to the lack of fresh content and the prevalence of self-promotional posts. Similar sentiments were echoed by others, suggesting that the initial excitement surrounding Google+'s launch has worn off, leaving users with a sense of stagnation.

The introduction of features such as Hangouts appears to be a bright spot for Google+, offering a unique aspect that sets it apart from other social media platforms. However, other features like Sparks have faced criticism for their complexity and lack of clear purpose. Circles, initially praised for their simplicity, have also encountered difficulties in terms of maintenance and usage, with some users finding it challenging to effectively utilize them.

Meanwhile, referral traffic from Google+ has begun to drop, indicating a potential issue with the platform's ability to drive external engagement. This decline in referrals may be attributed, in part, to Google's handling of branding and content, which has led to frustration among users and contributors. Today, Google Inc. fell $15.30, or 2.5 percent, to $607.22 on the Nasdaq Stock Market. With Google's reputation on the line, the company must address these concerns and adapt to changing user behaviors to ensure the continued success of Google+.

reference: – Less than a month after its big launch, Google+ has hit its first minus: Usage declined 3% to 1.79 million visits last week, reports Bloomberg. What's more, time spent on the new social media site dropped 10%, falling to 5 minutes to 15 seconds. It's important to note Experian Hitwise's numbers include neither international nor mobile web traffic, and Google+ is technically still in "limited beta," writes MG Siegler of Techcrunch. But when your prime competition has 750 million users, any setback can be daunting. Since making its debut June 28, Google+ quickly rose to 20 million users in just three weeks. "There was a sense of excitement: Could this really be the next big social network?" writes Siegler. But "after initially checking it several times a day, I now load Google+ about twice a day, mainly to see if I’m missing anything. I rarely find that I am. I +1 a few things here and there, maybe leave a comment. But overall, the content feels fairly stale. The new car smell is wearing off. And it’s time for reality." And that reality? "User retention is a bitch," says Siegler.

final_summary:
Roger Watson, a resident of Berkley, Michigan, was drafted on October 6, 1966, and met a lifelong friend, Dave Brown, during their basic Army training at Fort Hood, Texas. The two men, both from northwest Detroit, shared a bond that transcended their military service, becoming close companions despite being separated for nearly five decades. However, their paths diverged after they completed their initial training and returned home before deploying to Vietnam.

Dave Brown had heard that Roger Watson was wounded early in his tour but never received confirmation about his fate. Meanwhile, Roger Watson settled down in Berkley, got married, raised a family, and often wondered about his missing friend. It wasn't until five years ago that Roger Watson befriended a neighbor, Peggy Brown, who unknowingly shared a connection to his past. As they exchanged emails and shared pictures of birds, Peggy noticed the Purple Heart logo on Roger's license plate and the 9th Infantry insignia on his car, which sparked a chain of events that led to their astonishing reunion.

Peggy Brown, Dave's wife, had been friendly with Roger Watson over the fence, bonding over their shared interest in birds. On a particular day, she sent an email to Roger Watson, mentioning her husband's service in the 9th Infantry during the Vietnam War, which prompted a flurry of emails exchanging stories and memories. The exchange revealed that Roger Watson had indeed been wounded in action on March 8, 1968, losing a kidney, three feet of small intestine, and suffering liver damage.

The news brought immense joy to Dave Brown, who had spent 40 years wondering about his friend's fate. The two men finally reunited in their backyards in Berkley, where they had unknowingly lived within 150 feet of each other for 18 years. Their reunion was nothing short of remarkable, with both men expressing amazement and gratitude for the unexpected turn of events. As they reminisced about their time in the Army and Vietnam, they discovered that their lives had taken similar paths, with both becoming husbands, fathers, and grandfathers. The encounter reignited a deep connection, and they vowed to spend more time together, sharing stories and creating new memories.

reference: – A Detroit man who long pondered the fate of his old Army buddy found him in the oddest of places: living practically next door for the last 18 years. Dave Brown heard that his basic training buddy Roger Watson had been wounded in Vietnam, which was true—Watson lost a kidney and suffered other organ damage after being shot early in his tour in 1968, the Detroit Free Press reports. Brown even checked the Vietnam Veterans Memorial to see whether his fellow combat engineer demolition specialist, who trained with him at Ft. Hood in Texas, had died. Then Brown's wife, Peggy, befriended a kitty-corner neighbor over the back fence, chatting with him about their mutual interest in birds. She was also intrigued by the Purple Heart license plate on his car, and 9th Infantry Vietnam Veterans cap in his car's rear window. So she asked about them in an email. Soon, the exclamation points were flying: "Small world!!!!!!" and "Wow!!!!" are among the words in their email exchange as they figured out who was who. A backyard meeting was quickly arranged, and Brown "came running out of the house, over to the corner, had to beat back the brush, we just shook hands and couldn’t believe it," Watson tells WXYZ. "It was just an incredible feeling, just amazing." Reclaiming their friendship after nearly half a century, they plan to "share a beer every now and then" and see "a lot more of each other," says Watson. "Grow very old and watch our grandchildren grow up," adds Dave. "It's just great to see him alive. It makes me happy." (Read about a guy apparently outed as "fake" Army veteran.)

final_summary:
In the wake of the devastating Orlando massacre on Sunday, Senator Marco Rubio (R., Fla.) found himself engaged in a candid conversation with long-time friend and fellow Floridian, Lieutenant Governor Carlos Lopez-Cantera, about their respective futures in politics. The two men, who have been friends for 20 years, met at the scene of the tragedy, where they witnessed the aftermath of the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history. According to Lopez-Cantera, he urged Rubio to reconsider running for his Senate seat, citing the gravity of the situation and the significance of the upcoming election. This conversation marked a pivotal moment for Rubio, who had previously shown hesitation to run for re-election despite pressure from Senate leaders and donors.

Lopez-Cantera's words struck a chord with Rubio, who had been weighing his options and considering a potential exit from the Senate. The lieutenant governor emphasized that his decision to bring up the topic was not driven by personal ambition, but rather a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "This is bigger than me," Lopez-Cantera said. "It's about our country and this election." With the filing deadline for the Senate race looming on June 24, Rubio's decision will have significant implications for the Republican Party's chances of retaining control of the Senate.

The conversation between Rubio and Lopez-Cantera has sparked intense speculation about the senator's future, with many believing that he will ultimately choose not to run for re-election. Longtime friends of Rubio suggest that the allure of a higher-paying job and the opportunity to spend more time with his family will likely sway his decision. However, others argue that Rubio's commitment to public service and his desire to continue making a difference in the Senate will keep him in the running.

As the debate continues, Lopez-Cantera remains resolute in his own bid for the Senate seat, emphasizing that his decision to discuss the matter with Rubio was not a ploy to secure a deal or gain an advantage. "There is no deal," he insisted. "There is no plan." Despite the rumors and speculation surrounding the two friends, Lopez-Cantera maintains that his goal is to serve the people of Florida and the nation, not to advance his own career.

On Wednesday, Rubio told reporters that he would take time to consider his decision, stating that he would head home later that week to spend time with his family. Meanwhile, the Republican primary for the Senate seat remains crowded, with several candidates vying for the nomination, including U.S. Representative David Jolly, who is set to announce his plans on Friday, and defense contractor Todd Wilcox, who has indicated that he will stay in the race regardless of Rubio's decision. Lopez-Cantera's conversation with Rubio has also drawn attention from other Republican candidates, such as U.S. Representative Ron DeSantis, who may consider running for re-election in the 6th Congressional District if Rubio runs.

Miami-Dade County Commissioner Steve Bovo, a friend of both men, noted that Lopez-Cantera's discussion with Rubio was motivated by a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "Carlos is not only thinking about their friendship but the country," Bovo said. The outcome of the election will have far-reaching consequences for the balance of power in the Senate, and Rubio's choice will undoubtedly play a crucial role in determining the fate of the Republican Party's efforts to retain control of the chamber.

In the wake of the devastating Orlando massacre on Sunday, Senator Marco Rubio (R., Fla.) found himself engaged in a candid conversation with long-time friend and fellow Floridian, Lieutenant Governor Carlos Lopez-Cantera, about their respective futures in politics. The two men, who have been friends for 20 years, met at the scene of the tragedy, where they witnessed the aftermath of the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history. According to Lopez-Cantera, he urged Rubio to reconsider running for his Senate seat, citing the gravity of the situation and the significance of the upcoming election. This conversation marked a turning point for Rubio, who had previously shown reluctance to run for re-election despite pressure from Senate leaders and donors.

Lopez-Cantera's words struck a chord with Rubio, who had been weighing his options and considering a potential exit from the Senate. The lieutenant governor emphasized that his decision to bring up the topic was not driven by personal ambition, but rather a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "This is bigger than me," Lopez-Cantera said. "And this isn't about me. It's about our country and this election." With the filing deadline for the Senate race looming on June 24, Rubio's decision will have significant implications for the Republican Party's chances of retaining control of the Senate.

The conversation between Rubio and Lopez-Cantera has sparked intense speculation about the senator's future, with many believing that he will ultimately choose not to run for re-election. Longtime friends of Rubio suggest that the allure of a higher-paying job and the opportunity to spend more time with his family will likely sway his decision. However, others argue that Rubio's commitment to public service and his desire to continue making a difference in the Senate will keep him in the running.

As the debate continues, Lopez-Cantera remains resolute in his own bid for the Senate seat, emphasizing that his decision to discuss the matter with Rubio was not a ploy to secure a deal or gain an advantage. "There is no deal. There is no plan," he insisted. Despite the rumors and speculation surrounding the two friends, Lopez-Cantera maintains that his goal is to serve the people of Florida and the nation, not to advance his own career.

Meanwhile, the Republican primary for the Senate seat remains crowded, with several candidates vying for the nomination. U.S. Representative David Jolly is set to announce his plans on Friday, while defense contractor Todd Wilcox has indicated that he will stay in the race regardless of Rubio's decision. The outcome of the election will have far-reaching consequences for the balance of power in the Senate, and Rubio's choice will undoubtedly play a crucial role in determining the fate of the Republican Party's efforts to retain control of the chamber.

history.draft:
In the wake of the devastating Orlando massacre on Sunday, Senator Marco Rubio (R., Fla.) found himself engaged in a candid conversation with long-time friend and fellow Floridian, Lieutenant Governor Carlos Lopez-Cantera, about their respective futures in politics. The two men, who have been friends for 20 years, met at the scene of the tragedy, where they witnessed the aftermath of the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history. According to Lopez-Cantera, he urged Rubio to reconsider running for his Senate seat, citing the gravity of the situation and the significance of the upcoming election. This conversation marked a pivotal moment for Rubio, who had previously shown hesitation to run for re-election despite pressure from Senate leaders and donors.

Lopez-Cantera's words struck a chord with Rubio, who had been weighing his options and considering a potential exit from the Senate. The lieutenant governor emphasized that his decision to bring up the topic was not driven by personal ambition, but rather a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "This is bigger than me," Lopez-Cantera said. "It's about our country and this election." With the filing deadline for the Senate race looming on June 24, Rubio's decision will have significant implications for the Republican Party's chances of retaining control of the Senate.

The conversation between Rubio and Lopez-Cantera has sparked intense speculation about the senator's future, with many believing that he will ultimately choose not to run for re-election. Longtime friends of Rubio suggest that the allure of a higher-paying job and the opportunity to spend more time with his family will likely sway his decision. However, others argue that Rubio's commitment to public service and his desire to continue making a difference in the Senate will keep him in the running.

As the debate continues, Lopez-Cantera remains resolute in his own bid for the Senate seat, emphasizing that his decision to discuss the matter with Rubio was not a ploy to secure a deal or gain an advantage. "There is no deal," he insisted. "There is no plan." Despite the rumors and speculation surrounding the two friends, Lopez-Cantera maintains that his goal is to serve the people of Florida and the nation, not to advance his own career.

On Wednesday, Rubio told reporters that he would take time to consider his decision, stating that he would head home later that week to spend time with his family. Meanwhile, the Republican primary for the Senate seat remains crowded, with several candidates vying for the nomination, including U.S. Representative David Jolly, who is set to announce his plans on Friday, and defense contractor Todd Wilcox, who has indicated that he will stay in the race regardless of Rubio's decision. Lopez-Cantera's conversation with Rubio has also drawn attention from other Republican candidates, such as U.S. Representative Ron DeSantis, who may consider running for re-election in the 6th Congressional District if Rubio runs.

Miami-Dade County Commissioner Steve Bovo, a friend of both men, noted that Lopez-Cantera's discussion with Rubio was motivated by a sense of duty to the country and the importance of the election. "Carlos is not only thinking about their friendship but the country," Bovo said. The outcome of the election will have far-reaching consequences for the balance of power in the Senate, and Rubio's choice will undoubtedly play a crucial role in determining the fate of the Republican Party's efforts to retain control of the chamber.

reference: – The main obstacle keeping Marco Rubio from seeking re-election to his Senate seat just disappeared. The prospect of Rubio doing so gained traction in the wake of the Orlando shooting, but he continued to insist that he wouldn't run against his friend already in the race, Carlos Lopez-Cantera. Now, however, Lopez-Cantera himself is publicly giving Rubio the all-clear, reports Politico. He says he and Rubio discussed it in recent days, with Lopez-Cantera encouraging him to consider changing his mind. “I don’t want you to feel like you have to say that because of outside pressure,” he quotes Rubio as responding. The incumbent told reporters on Wednesday that he'd go home this week to spend time with his family and consider his next steps. Rumors are swirling that the pair have some sort of plan—perhaps for Rubio to win the seat over a Democrat (Republicans believe he has the best shot), then resign and allow Lopez-Cantera to run before the 2020 presidential campaign. But Lopez-Cantera says that's not the case. "Until I mentioned this, he was really committed to spending time with his kids, being able to be home for more than 10 days without having to go somewhere," he says. As of now, "nothing has changed,” he adds. "I'm still running. Marco isn't." Rubio's decision isn’t expected until the weekend at the earliest, per the Wall Street Journal; he has until June 24 to file for re-election. If he does so, Lopez-Cantera won't run, says Politico.

final_summary:
Late Wednesday, Twitter announced plans to overhaul its process for verifying accounts, resulting in the removal of the coveted blue checkmark from several high-profile far-right users. Among those affected was Tim "Baked Alaska" Gionet, a former BuzzFeed staffer turned alt-right figure, who was permanently banned from the platform. In response to the loss of his verification, Gionet resorted to livestreaming his meltdown at an In-N-Out Burger, where he randomly screamed at innocent bystanders.

Laura Loomer, a self-proclaimed independent journalist and former employee of Project Veritas, a right-wing sting operation led by James O'Keefe, took the loss of her verification particularly hard. Loomer, who gained notoriety for disrupting a New York City performance of "Julius Caesar" to provoke a reaction, suggested that the situation was eerily reminiscent of the beginning of a film about the Holocaust. When a verified Twitter user pointed out that the comparison was unfounded, Loomer doubled down on her stance.

Interestingly, other notable far-right figures, including neo-Nazis Richard Spencer and Jason Kessler, who organized the deadly white supremacist rally in Charlottesville, retained their verification status despite being stripped of it. This development has led some to question whether Twitter's efforts to address harassment and abuse on the platform are merely superficial, aimed at avoiding the appearance of endorsing extremist ideologies without actually preventing them from using the service.

Loomer's reaction to losing her verification was not limited to Twitter. On Thursday, she sparked controversy when she posted a series of anti-Muslim tweets following a terrorist attack in New York. Her comments included calls for a new ride-sharing service that did not employ Muslim drivers, after it was discovered that the suspect in the attack, Sayfullo Saipov, was a former Uber driver. Loomer's tweets also featured a photo of two women wearing hijabs near the scene of the attack, accompanied by a comment implying that they might be celebrating the incident.

The backlash against Loomer was swift and intense, with many critics accusing her of stoking racism in the aftermath of a tragedy. Celebrities such as Gigi Hadid and Alyssa Milano condemned Loomer's comments, with Hadid labeling her a "f***ing moron" and Milano reporting her to Twitter for hate speech. As a result, Uber and Lyft subsequently banned Loomer from using their services. Despite the criticism, Loomer remained defiant, announcing that she would take legal action against the ride-hailing companies.

reference: – A few Twitter users lost the coveted blue tick on Wednesday after Twitter released new guidelines regarding verified accounts. The move came after the company was attacked for granting verified status to Jason Kessler, who put together the white nationalist rally that rocked Charlottesville in August (and issued this "vile" tweet about victim Heather Heyer). In a series of tweets, Twitter explained that "verification was meant to authenticate identity & voice ... [but] verification has long been perceived as an endorsement. We gave verified accounts visual prominence on the service which deepened this perception. We should have addressed this earlier but did not prioritize the work as we should have." General verification requests had been put on ice as of Nov. 9 pending a review of the program, and the Guardian reports that Twitter's new policy specifies that accounts that promote hate, support hate groups, or harass users will see verification revoked. Among those to immediately lose the check, per the Washington Post, were Kessler, white nationalist Richard Spencer, and far-right activist Laura Loomer (known for rushing the stage during Julius Caesar and being banned from Uber and Lyft after anti-Muslim tweets). Loomer's response to her notice of verification removal including tweeting "Translation: I'm a conservative" and, per Splinter, comparing her situation to that of Jews during the Holocaust.

final_summary:
Celebrated author Alice Walker, known for her Pulitzer Prize-winning novel The Color Purple, has once again found herself embroiled in controversy for her association with David Icke, a British author and public speaker known for his conspiracy theories and anti-Semitic views. In an interview with The New York Times Book Review, Walker listed Icke's book "And the Truth Shall Set You Free" as one of the four books on her nightstand, sparking widespread criticism and accusations of anti-Semitism.

Icke's book is described as an "unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract" that promotes a range of anti-Semitic tropes, including the idea that a shadowy cabal controls the world and that this cabal is Jewish. The book draws heavily on the infamous anti-Semitic pamphlet, The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, and features quotes and assertions that are deeply hostile to Jews and the Talmud. Icke's views on Jews are not limited to this book, however - he has a long history of making anti-Semitic statements, including calling the Talmud "among the most appallingly racist documents on the planet."

Walker's association with Icke is not new, and she has previously praised his work and ideas in interviews and online posts. In 2013, she wrote a glowing review of Icke's book "Human Race Get Off Your Knees," describing it as an "amazing book" that allowed her to understand the "root of the incredible evil that has engulfed our planet." She has also shared Icke's content on social media, including a video interview with Alex Jones, and promoted one of his lectures to her readers on YouTube. 

Despite her long history of promoting Icke's work, Walker has never been held accountable by elite cultural critics, and her association with Icke has been largely ignored by publications like The New York Times. However, the paper's failure to challenge Walker's recommendation of Icke's book has sparked outrage, with many accusing the newspaper of perpetuating anti-Semitism in Britain.

Yair Rosenberg, a senior writer at Tablet, criticized The New York Times for failing to provide context or qualification for Walker's recommendation, noting that the paper's policy is to allow subjects to speak for themselves without editorial intervention. However, Rosenberg argued that this approach is inadequate when dealing with anti-Semitic content, and that the paper has a responsibility to challenge and contextualize such views.

Walker's defenders have sought to deflect criticism by pointing to her support for the Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions (BDS) movement against Israel, arguing that her anti-Israel views are being unfairly conflated with anti-Semitism. However, this argument is not convincing, as many anti-Zionists are also anti-Semites, and Walker's views on Jews and the Talmud are clearly anti-Semitic.

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) has long monitored Icke's activities and has condemned his anti-Semitic rhetoric. The Anti-Defamation League has stated that Icke's views are not just anti-Semitic but also contribute to a broader culture of hate and intolerance.

The controversy surrounding Walker and Icke highlights the need for greater scrutiny and accountability from cultural institutions like The New York Times. By failing to challenge and contextualize anti-Semitic views, these institutions risk perpetuating hatred and intolerance. As Rosenberg noted, "Not publishing anti-Semitic book recommendations unchallenged in the New York Times would be a good place to start."
anchors: The New York Times Book Review

reference: – The New York Times is standing by its Q&A with Pulitzer-winning author Alice Walker despite complaints about her comments on a book by an accused anti-Semite. The 74-year-old author of The Color Purple told the newspaper's By the Book column that was she was currently reading David Icke's And the Truth Shall Set You Free and called it "a curious person's dream come true." At Tablet, Yair Rosenberg writes that the book referencing The Protocols of the Elders of Zion, a text accusing Jewish people of a plot for global domination, "is an unhinged anti-Semitic conspiracy tract written by one of Britain's most notorious anti-Semites." The Washington Post highlights this quote: "I strongly believe that a small Jewish clique which has contempt for the mass of Jewish people worked with non-Jews to create the First World War, the Russian Revolution, and the Second World War." A Times rep says the column is a "portrait of a public person through the lens of books; it is not a list of recommendations from our editors," who might "dislike, disdain or even abhor" the books mentioned, per the Guardian. But Amy Russo at HuffPo notes the paper failed to provide context in an editor's note, "thereby allowing [Icke's] name to be promoted unchecked." Rosenberg argues the column only ensures "racism is disseminated to more people." Walker also said this of the book: "In Icke’s books there is the whole of existence, on this planet and several others, to think about." Her comments are "especially corrosive because the anti-Jewish conspiracies she uplifted and adopted are part of the same white supremacist power structure she so deftly fought through her written work in the past," adds Jewish filmmaker Rebecca Pierce. (Walker once blocked an Israeli publisher from printing her book.)

final_summary:
According to a recent report by the BBC, officials at the Alipore zoo in Kolkata, formerly known as Calcutta, are facing a unique challenge with a 10-year-old white tiger named Vishal. For the past six months, zoo officials have attempted to mate Vishal with an eight-year-old tigress named Rupa, but he has shown little interest in doing so. The efforts to encourage him began last year, with zoo officials administering various treatments, including vitamins A and E, and libido enhancers, in an attempt to boost Vishal's libido. However, none of these measures have yielded results.

Dayanarayan Banerjee, a senior veterinary officer at the zoo, attributes Vishal's behavior to his shyness, likely caused by growing up in captivity without interacting with the opposite sex. This theory is supported by experts from the forest and wildlife department, who believe that Vishal's demeanor is not indicative of a lack of virility, but rather a result of his limited socialization.

The breeding program at Alipore zoo is crucial for maintaining a healthy population of big cats, as many of the current residents are aging and nearing the end of their reproductive prime. The last successful breeding of a tiger cub occurred more than a decade ago, and officials are eager to revive the program. Despite the challenges posed by Vishal's reluctance, zoo authorities remain optimistic and are exploring alternative options, including introducing him to a younger tigress named Rani, who is described as "young and full of vigor." Banerjee expressed concerns that Vishal may be too old to mate effectively, citing that the ideal age range for tiger mating is typically around five to six years old, with seven to eight-year-olds also being viable candidates.
