history.draft:
Researchers C. A. Thorstenson and A. D. Pazda led a study published in the journal Psychological Science to investigate the relationship between emotions and color perception. The study aimed to explore whether emotions can influence color perception, building on the idea that emotions can affect low-level visual processes, which are essential for higher-order vision. 

Past research has suggested that emotions can influence color perception, and the current study aimed to provide further insight into this relationship. The study involved two experiments, conducted by C. A. Thorstenson and A. D. Pazda, with the supervision of A. J. Elliot. In the first experiment, 129 college students were randomly assigned to watch either a sad film clip, an amusing clip, or a neutral screen saver. The sad clip featured a poignant scene from Disney's The Lion King, while the amusing clip consisted of a stand-up comedy routine. The neutral condition involved watching a blank desktop screensaver. After viewing the clips, participants were tasked with identifying desaturated colors, specifically red, yellow, green, and blue patches that had been muted to gray.

The results of the first experiment revealed that participants who watched the sad clip struggled to distinguish between shades along the blue-yellow color axis, whereas those who watched the amusing or neutral clips performed accurately. Notably, the impairment in color perception was specific to the blue-yellow axis, suggesting that sadness may have a unique impact on the processing of these colors. This finding is consistent with previous research indicating that dopamine, a neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, plays a crucial role in color perception.

To confirm the results, the researchers conducted a second experiment, which yielded similar findings. Participants who watched the sad clip were less accurate in perceiving color along the blue-yellow axis compared to those who watched the neutral clip. However, their performance was equivalent to the neutral group along the red-green axis. The researchers speculate that dopamine deficiency might influence perception of blue-yellow colors, given that past research has shown that perception of these colors is affected in clinical disorders involving dopamine.

The study's findings have implications for our understanding of how emotions shape our subjective experience of the world. The results suggest that our perception of reality is not solely determined by objective stimuli but is also influenced by our internal emotional state. This concept, known as the "top-down" effect, proposes that higher-level cognition can modulate perception, rather than merely reflecting it. While some researchers argue that the evidence for top-down effects is still limited, the study contributes to the growing body of research exploring the complex interplay between emotions, cognition, and perception.

The study's lead author, C. A. Thorstenson, emphasized that the findings have broader implications for our understanding of the human experience. He stated, "Our results show that mood and emotion can affect how we see the world around us." The study's conclusions are supported by previous research demonstrating that exposure to certain colors can influence cognitive performance, facial attractiveness, and food intake. As Thorstenson noted, "Data are beginning to emerge supporting the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them."

The study's significance extends beyond the realm of basic science, offering insights into the intricate relationships between emotions, cognition, and perception. By shedding light on the complex interactions between the brain's neural networks, the study provides a deeper understanding of how our experiences shape our perception of reality. As Thorstenson aptly put it, "How we feel can really influence how we see the world around us."

reference: – Got the blues? You probably aren't seeing blues clearly. That's the takeaway from a new study that finds how a person views the color blue may actually depend on mood, reports Medical Daily. Not all colors were affected in the same way. Researchers at the University of Rochester asked 129 college students to watch either a stand-up comedy clip or the scene from The Lion King in which Mufasa dies, then recorded the participants' moods, per the Washington Post. Next, participants took a color accuracy test in which they had to look at 48 color patches that had been desaturated to look nearly gray, then identify whether each was red, yellow, green, or blue. People who were feeling down in the dumps performed worse than those who were amused. In other words, the data supports "the conventional wisdom that people's emotions influence how colorful the world looks to them," the researchers say. However, the sad group performed poorly only when quizzed about colors on what researchers call the blue-yellow axis. They could identify colors on the red-green axis just as well as others—perhaps due to the evolutionary need to see red as a sign of anger, reports Time. The results are "a reminder that our experience of the world is not as immediate and objective as we’d like to believe," observes the Post. Researchers suggest a sad mood messes up the ability of the neuron transmitter dopamine to process information about blue-yellow colors. (This woman sees 100 times more colors than you.)

final_summary:
On October 19, 2018, CNN's Chief White House Correspondent Jim Acosta tweeted from President Donald Trump's rally in Missoula, Montana, expressing frustration over the loud music being played by the Trump campaign, which made it nearly impossible for him to conduct live TV reports. He wondered if the high volume posed a security concern for local law enforcement, who may also be unable to hear due to the excessive noise.

Justin Caporale, a Republican consultant and former aide to First Lady Melania Trump, responded to Acosta's tweet with a mocking remark, "Dear Diary…", implying that Acosta was whining like a child writing in a diary. This seemingly innocuous jab sparked a heated exchange between the two, with Acosta sending Caporale a direct message containing a profanity-laced insult, "F**k you." However, unbeknownst to Acosta, Caporale had shared their conversation with Daily Caller reporter Peter Hasson, who obtained screenshots of the exchange and publicly revealed them on social media.

The incident drew attention from others, including conservative commentator Matt's Idea Shop, who created a humorous meme referencing the exchange. In response to the backlash, Acosta issued an apology to Caporale, claiming he had mistaken him for an "old friend from the campaign days" and expressed regret for offending him. The apology came after the initial exchange, and it remains unclear why Acosta chose to block Caporale shortly after sending the insulting message.

reference: – One of the reporters who tangles publicly with President Trump the most is himself in a bit of hot water over a tweet. Jim Acosta of CNN has apologized after tweeting "f--- you" (except he spelled it out) to a critic, reports Mediaite. Acosta also maintains that he mistook the person as an old friend, but not everyone is buying that. It all started Thursday night when Acosta tweeted a complaint that the Trump campaign turned up the music so loudly at a Trump rally that it was difficult to work—the implication being that it was deliberate. At that point, a former aide for Melania Trump, Justin Caporale, mocked Acosta with a tweet reading, "Dear Diary ..." That's when Acosta sent his controversial response—he did so in a private direct message, but Caporale then made it public. Acosta also blocked Caporale. As online criticism grew, and Caporale demanded an apology, Acosta responded: "Hey buddy I thought you were an old friend from the campaign days. I’m so sorry. Hope I didn’t offend you. Have a good night and take care." As the Wrap notes, the apology seems a bit odd given that Acosta also blocked Caporale, raising the question of why he would block a friend. Neither Acosta nor CNN has commented further. (Acosta also once tangled memorably with White House aide Stephen Miller.)

final_summary:
Schlitterbahn Waterparks and Resorts, a Texas-based company operating a waterpark in Kansas City, Kansas, announced on Tuesday that the 168-foot-tall Verrückt waterslide, where a 10-year-old boy named Caleb Schwab died in a tragic accident, would be permanently decommissioned and removed from the tower once the investigation into the tragedy is concluded and permission is granted by the court. Caleb, the son of Kansas state Representative Scott Schwab, was decapitated while riding the slide, which was billed as the world's tallest waterslide, featuring multi-person rafts making a 17-story drop at speeds of up to 70 mph.

The incident occurred on August 7, 2016, and two other riders in the same raft suffered facial injuries. Since then, the ride has been closed, and Schlitterbahn has cooperated with investigators and the victims' families and attorneys, stating that the safety of their staff and guests is their top priority. In a statement, the company expressed their heartbreak over the tragedy and acknowledged that they had never experienced such a devastating event in their 50 years of providing an environment for families and friends to gather.

Over the past five decades, Schlitterbahn has established itself as a beloved destination for families and friends to create lasting memories. However, the Verrückt tragedy has brought attention to the need for stricter regulations on amusement park rides. In Kansas, private inspectors are currently allowed to conduct annual checks, whereas state inspections are not mandatory. A document released by the state Department of Labor revealed that all of Schlitterbahn's rides passed private inspections in June.

A Schlitterbahn spokesperson told 41 Action News that the slide "will never run again – not at any park, anywhere," and that the park is prepared to dismantle the slide and conveyor belt as soon as permitted by the court. The investigation into the tragedy is ongoing, with a homicide detective still examining the ride's components, including rafts, netting, and metal frames. The case remains open, although criminal charges are unlikely.

Personal injury attorney Matt Meyerkord noted that independent investigations like the one underway for the Schlitterbahn case require extensive examination of the ride's mechanics and engineering. The Schwab family's attorney, Michael C. Rader, stated that he has been working with Schlitterbahn and their attorneys to permanently decommission the slide and ensure its eventual demolition, pending the conclusion of all investigations and potential litigation. As the investigation continues, the possibility of a lawsuit or settlement remains uncertain.

In Kansas, there are limits on personal injury and wrongful death cases, with caps of $300,000 for non-economic damages and $250,000 for wrongful death cases. If the case proceeds to court, it may be moved to Texas due to Schlitterbahn being a Texas-based company. An independent investigation is also underway, involving attorneys and experts for the Schwab family and the two female passengers who were injured in the incident.

reference: – The 10-year-old son of a Kansas lawmaker died on the world's tallest water slide this summer, and that ride is now closed for good. Caleb Schwab, the son of state Rep. Scott Schwab, suffered a "fatal neck injury" Aug. 7 on the 168-foot Verruckt slide at the Schlitterbahn Kansas City water park; the slide has been closed as investigators look into Caleb's death, the Kansas City Star reports. Once the probe is done and the park gets the legal go-ahead, the slide will be "decommissioned," with the ride permanently shuttered and the slide taken down from its tower, per a Schlitterbahn statement released Tuesday. "In our opinion, it is the only proper course of action following this tragedy," the park says, adding it remains "heartbroken" over Caleb's death. It's not clear whether the Schwab family will sue or settle with the park, or if criminal charges will be filed, though the Kansas City PD tells KSHB charges aren't likely. In Kansas, permanent amusement park rides have to undergo yearly inspections, but they can be done by private firms rather than the state, the AP reports. In Schlitterbahn's case, all of its rides received a thumbs-up in June after they received private inspections. But some people who had ridden the slide, operational since 2014, spoke out after Caleb's death and noted they'd had uneasy experiences on the ride, including shoulder harnesses coming loose and rafts flying into the air when they shouldn't. The park notes in its release that it will eventually announce what attraction will be built in Verruckt's place.

final_summary:
The American Association of Physics Teachers' publication, the American Journal of Physics, recently featured a study challenging the long-held notion that it would take approximately 42 minutes to fall through the center of the Earth, a concept known as the "gravity tunnel problem." This widely-accepted answer has been taught to physics students for decades. However, a recent study suggests that the actual time may be shorter – around 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

The study, conducted by Alexander Klotz, a graduate student in physics at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, aimed to create a more realistic model of Earth's internal structure by incorporating seismic data. Unlike the traditional assumption of a uniform density, Klotz's model revealed a less dense crust and mantle, and a more dense core. By applying this new model to the gravity tunnel problem, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is significantly reduced.

David Jackson, a physicist at Dickinson College in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and editor of the American Journal of Physics, praised Klotz's work, noting that it offers a fresh perspective on a classic problem. "This is the kind of paper we love," Jackson said. "It not only adds more detail about the structure of Earth but also explains why you can replace one overly simple assumption with another equally crude one and still get a much more accurate answer." Jackson added that the classic problem needs to remain, but Klotz's contribution provides a valuable addition to the classic problem.

Klotz's findings suggest that the traditional method of calculating the time it would take to fall through the Earth assumes a constant force of gravity, which is not representative of the actual conditions. In reality, the force of gravity varies depending on the distance from the center of the Earth. By accounting for this variation, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is closer to 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

Interestingly, Klotz discovered that if he assumed a constant gravitational field throughout the duration of the trip, the math showed that it would indeed take approximately 38 minutes to fall through the Earth. This finding highlights the complexity of the problem and the importance of considering the nuances of Earth's internal structure. Isaac Newton's law of gravity underlies the gravity tunnel problem, which demonstrates both the principles of gravity and cyclical motion. The study's results underscore the need for a more accurate understanding of these fundamental concepts, particularly in light of the complexities of Earth's internal structure.
anchors: The American Association Of Physics Teachers

history.draft:
The American Association of Physics Teachers' publication, the American Journal of Physics, recently featured a study challenging the long-held notion that it would take approximately 42 minutes to fall through the center of the Earth, a concept known as the "gravity tunnel problem." This widely-accepted answer has been taught to physics students for decades. However, a recent study suggests that the actual time may be shorter – around 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

The study, conducted by Alexander Klotz, a graduate student in physics at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, aimed to create a more realistic model of Earth's internal structure by incorporating seismic data. Unlike the traditional assumption of a uniform density, Klotz's model revealed a less dense crust and mantle, and a more dense core. By applying this new model to the gravity tunnel problem, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is significantly reduced.

David Jackson, a physicist at Dickinson College in Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and editor of the American Journal of Physics, praised Klotz's work, noting that it offers a fresh perspective on a classic problem. "This is the kind of paper we love," Jackson said. "It not only adds more detail about the structure of Earth but also explains why you can replace one overly simple assumption with another equally crude one and still get a much more accurate answer." Jackson added that the classic problem needs to remain, but Klotz's contribution provides a valuable addition to the classic problem.

Klotz's findings suggest that the traditional method of calculating the time it would take to fall through the Earth assumes a constant force of gravity, which is not representative of the actual conditions. In reality, the force of gravity varies depending on the distance from the center of the Earth. By accounting for this variation, Klotz found that the time it would take to fall through the center of the Earth is closer to 38 minutes and 11 seconds.

Interestingly, Klotz discovered that if he assumed a constant gravitational field throughout the duration of the trip, the math showed that it would indeed take approximately 38 minutes to fall through the Earth. This finding highlights the complexity of the problem and the importance of considering the nuances of Earth's internal structure. Isaac Newton's law of gravity underlies the gravity tunnel problem, which demonstrates both the principles of gravity and cyclical motion. The study's results underscore the need for a more accurate understanding of these fundamental concepts, particularly in light of the complexities of Earth's internal structure.

reference: – It's a common hypothetical in physics classrooms: How long would it take to fall through a tunnel from one side of the Earth to the other? The new answer: 38 minutes and 11 seconds, reports Science. The result is courtesy of McGill University graduate student Alexander Klotz, whose calculations take four minutes off the previously accepted answer and appear in the American Journal of Physics. The reason for the shorter time? Unlike previous calculations, Klotz's take into account that the Earth's layers have different densities, and those differences would affect the hypothetical falling man, notes Phys.org. "The way the Earth is structured, the gravity increases slightly as you go deeper towards the dense core, to about 110% of its surface value, before getting weaker as you move through the core, reaching zero at the center,” says Klotz, as quoted in Forbes. (If it's all a little too hypothetical, try physics-inspired pasta instead.)

final_summary:
On Thursday, Sept. 22, 2011, in Orlando, Florida, Republican presidential hopefuls Mitt Romney and Rick Perry clashed in a heated debate, sponsored by Fox News and Google, over various issues, including immigration, healthcare, and Social Security. The debate, which was part of a series of three debates in as many weeks, saw the two front-runners engaging in a series of tense exchanges, with Romney accusing Perry of flip-flopping on several key issues.

Perry, who has been leading in national opinion polls, faced intense scrutiny over his stance on immigration, particularly his support for a Texas law that provides in-state tuition rates to children of undocumented immigrants. Romney criticized Perry's policy, stating that it creates a "magnet" for illegal immigrants seeking educational benefits. Perry, however, defended his position, arguing that it is unfair to punish children who entered the country through no fault of their own. When Romney pressed him on the issue, Perry shot back, saying, "I don't think they have a heart."

The debate also centered around Social Security, with Romney accusing Perry of advocating for the abolition of the program, citing a passage from Perry's book "Fed Up!" Perry denied the accusation, claiming that he never suggested such a thing. Romney then mocked Perry, saying, "You better find that Rick Perry and get him to stop saying that." Perry retaliated by accusing Romney of flip-flopping on his views on healthcare, pointing out that Romney had initially supported the healthcare reform bill he implemented in Massachusetts, but later removed the language from subsequent editions of his book.

Romney and Perry also exchanged blows over their respective records on gun rights and healthcare. Perry claimed that Romney had changed his stance on gun ownership, while Romney accused Perry of supporting a healthcare plan that would require individuals to purchase insurance or face a fine. The two candidates' aggressive posturing dominated the debate, leaving other contenders, including Herman Cain, Newt Gingrich, and Jon Huntsman, struggling to gain attention.

The debate, moderated by Fox News, highlighted the increasingly contentious nature of the Republican primary contest. With Perry leading in the polls and Romney closing the gap, the stakes are high for both candidates. The debate showcased their contrasting styles, with Romney presenting himself as a steady and consistent leader, while Perry came across as more passionate and emotive. Despite the intensity of the exchanges, neither candidate emerged clearly victorious, leaving the outcome of the primary contest still uncertain.

reference: – Republicans got back into debate mode tonight, with Rick Perry and Mitt Romney trading a series of sharp and sometimes sarcastic barbs at one another over Social Security, immigration, and other issues, reports AP. No fewer than nine candidates were on the stage, thanks to the invite extended to former New Mexico Gov. Gary Johnson. (He got perhaps the biggest applause line of the night with this line: "My neighbor's two dogs have created more shovel-ready projects for this country" than Obama.) But Perry and Romney were the main focus, and their back-and-forth on Social Security typified things: Perry said his position on it isn't as radical as his foes say, adding it was "not the first time that Mitt's been wrong on some issues before," notes the New York Times. Romney responded: "There's a Rick Perry out there that's saying the federal government shouldn't be in the pension business. You better find that Rick Perry and get him to stop saying that." Perry then accused Romney of changing views of his Massachusetts health care law in different versions of his book, and later widened the flip-flopping charges to a host of other issues. (Romney used the phrase "nice try" at different times to deflect Perry criticisms.) Immigration also emerged as a main issue, with Romney, Michele Bachmann, and Rick Santorum criticizing the Texas policy of giving in-state tuition benefits to immigrants' kids, notes Politico. Bachmann called such programs "magnets," but Perry said of his critics, "I don't think you have a heart." Catch up with live-blogging and details on the other candidates here.

final_summary:
Dawn Wells, best known for her iconic role as Mary Ann Summers on the classic television show "Gilligan's Island," is facing a dire financial situation after suffering a series of setbacks. In 2008, Wells experienced the devastating effects of the banking crash, resulting in the loss of her life savings. This financial blow was further complicated by a life-threatening surgery that left her hospitalized for two months and requiring extensive recovery time. 

After the banking crash, Wells faced another unexpected challenge when she broke her knee this year, adding to her existing health struggles. Her current predicament has led to mounting medical expenses and penalties imposed by the IRS, which are compounding daily. To alleviate these burdens, a GoFundMe campaign was initiated by her friend, Dugg Kirkpatrick, aiming to raise $194,000 to cover her outstanding debts and medical costs. 

As of the latest update, the fundraising effort has garnered nearly $120,000 in just a short period, with contributions pouring in from supporters on social media platforms such as Facebook. Kirkpatrick, a hairstylist who has worked with Wells in the past, has been actively promoting the campaign and advocating for her well-being. He revealed that Wells has been struggling to find suitable housing, having lost her home, and is currently seeking to relocate to a smaller assisted living facility catering to individuals from the film and television industry. However, her application was initially rejected due to her outstanding debt. Kirkpatrick is now working towards getting her moved into the new facility in Glendale, California, sometime soon. 

God bless those who have contributed to this cause, and please consider helping our American icon, Dawn Wells, by sharing this post. Please help me save Dawn Wells. Thank you in advance.

reference: – Dawn Wells is not doing so well, according to the friend who launched a GoFundMe crowdfunding campaign for the Gilligan's Island actress. Wells, 79, who played Mary Ann on the show, lost almost everything in the 2008 financial crisis, the friend tells TMZ. This year, while already recovering from a major surgery and complications resulting from it, she broke her knee. She now needs to move to an assisted living facility, but it won't take her because she's nearly $200,000 in debt, the friend says. "Dawn needs $194,000 to alleviate penalties by the IRS (which are compounded daily) and to pay her hospital costs," the GoFundMe campaign says, though it's seeking to raise just $180,000. In a campaign update, the friend quotes Wells as saying, "I just don’t know what happened! I thought I would be just fine, but apparently I’m not. I’ve found myself with no home, husband, or kids. I thank god every day that I have friends and fans who care or this whole thing would be too overwhelming." The campaign asks the public to help "YOUR favorite castaway," noting that she is "a woman who gave so many people Joy over the years." As of this writing, it has raised more than $46,000. People notes that the campaign is run by someone calling himself Dugg Kirkpatrick, and points to a Twitter photo seeming to indicate he is a hairstylist who has worked with Wells.

final_summary:
Representative Robert E. Andrews (D-N.J.) announced his resignation from Congress on Tuesday, citing a 20-year tenure marked by an unprecedented lack of legislative success. During his time in office, Andrews introduced an astonishing 646 bills, yet only four of them successfully passed the House of Representatives, with none making it to the president's desk due to failing to clear the Senate hurdle. This record makes Andrews the least successful lawmaker of the past two decades, with a batting average that would be considered abysmal in any other context.

However, Andrews disputes this characterization, arguing that the traditional notion of lawmaking – where a bill is introduced, debated, and voted upon – no longer applies on Capitol Hill. According to him, the current system involves massive omnibus bills packed with unrelated provisions, which often succeed despite having little to do with each other. Andrews claims that his true measure of success lies in the numerous ideas he has embedded within these larger bills, which have ultimately become law.

Andrews estimates that approximately 110 of his ideas have been incorporated into other legislation, although this figure remains difficult to verify. His legislative output has been staggering, with no other representative introducing more bills since his arrival in Congress. However, his colleagues have fared better, with Carolyn B. Maloney (D-N.Y.) passing nine bills, Don Young (R-Alaska) securing 76, Charles B. Rangel (D-N.Y.) achieving 38, and Ron Paul (R-Texas) managing one minor bill.

A closer examination of Andrews' bills reveals a diverse range of topics, including support for Taiwan, commemorative coins honoring Korean immigrants, and duty reductions on various chemicals. While these efforts appear to have failed, Andrews insists that his approach is effective in getting his ideas onto the legislative agenda. He attributes his success to introducing bills that serve as building blocks for future legislation, often referred to as "moving vehicles." These bills frequently gain traction due to impending deadlines or other external factors, allowing Andrews to insert his proposals into the mix.

Joshua Tauberer, whose site govtrack.us allows users to track and compare vast amounts of data on Congress, notes that only one of the two companion bills will actually become law, but the work to get either enacted may have been shared by the two sponsors equally. Andrews cites examples of his success, such as changes to the military health-care program Tricare and rules about insurance coverage of orthotic devices. Some other ideas have changed government policy without being passed in any form, such as a decision by mortgage firms Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to require prompt decisions on short sales.

The Internet Archive, a digital repository dedicated to preserving web content, has been archiving pages as they are created and linking to previous versions to prevent broken links. A photograph of Andrews, alongside fellow Representative Tim Bishop (D-N.Y.), taken on February 11, 2011, serves as a poignant reminder of the changing landscape of Congress. As Andrews departs, his legacy remains a subject of debate, with some questioning whether his unorthodox approach to lawmaking has yielded tangible results.

His legislative output has been staggering, with no other representative introducing more bills since his arrival in Congress. However, his colleagues have fared better, with Carolyn B. Maloney (D-N.Y.) passing nine bills, Don Young (R-Alaska) securing 76, Charles B. Rangel (D-N.Y.) achieving 38, and Ron Paul (R-Texas) managing one minor bill.

A closer examination of Andrews' bills reveals a diverse range of topics, including support for Taiwan, commemorative coins honoring Korean immigrants, and duty reductions on various chemicals. While these efforts appear to have failed, Andrews insists that his approach is effective in getting his ideas onto the legislative agenda. He attributes his success to introducing bills that serve as building blocks for future legislation, often referred to as "moving vehicles." These bills frequently gain traction due to impending deadlines or other external factors, allowing Andrews to insert his proposals into the mix.

Joshua Tauberer, whose site govtrack.us allows users to track and compare vast amounts of data on Congress, notes that only one of the two companion bills will actually become law, but the work to get either enacted may have been shared by the two sponsors equally. Andrews cites examples of his success, such as changes to the military health-care program Tricare and rules about insurance coverage of orthotic devices. Some other ideas have changed government policy without being passed in any form, such as a decision by mortgage firms Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to require prompt decisions on short sales.

The Internet Archive, a digital repository dedicated to preserving web content, has been archiving pages as they are created and linking to previous versions to prevent broken links. A photograph of Andrews, alongside fellow Representative Tim Bishop (D-N.Y.), taken on February 11, 2011, serves as a poignant reminder of the changing landscape of Congress. As Andrews departs, his legacy remains a subject of debate, with some questioning whether his unorthodox approach to lawmaking has yielded tangible results.

reference: – New Jersey Democrat Rob Andrews made a surprise announcement today that he'll be giving up his House seat as of Feb. 18 to join a Philadelphia law firm, reports Philly.com. And while it's big news in Jersey, Andrews isn't exactly a household name nationally. Still, the Washington Post observes that he's got a legislative track record remarkable in its own way: Over 23 years, not one of the 646 pieces of legislation Andrews authored has become law. All the more impressive is that no representative has introduced more bills than him in that span. Sure, most bills fail in Congress, but "by those numbers, Andrews would be America’s least successful lawmaker of the past two decades," writes David Fahrenthold. Andrews, described as a "semi-liberal back-bencher," defends himself by saying that modern lawmaking these days is done through massive pieces of legislation, not through individual bills. By his own reckoning, Andrews estimates that 110 of his ideas have become law in that fashion. If he wants to eliminate the goose egg, however, maybe he can rename a post office in his final two weeks?

final_summary:
Thursday morning, a press representative for director Michael Bay stated that the filmmaker was "fine" and "not hurt" after an incident in Hong Kong, where he was making "Transformers 4." Later that day, the representative clarified that it was "erroneous" to report that Bay was injured in the attack, saying instead that he had fended off a man wielding an air-conditioning unit as a weapon.

Reuters reported that two Hong Kong men, described as brothers, were arrested after approaching Bay at the "Transformers 4" set and demanding money, approximately $13,000. The men attacked Bay, and the older brother also assaulted three police officers who intervened. Both men were charged with suspicion of assault, and the younger brother was also charged with suspicion of blackmail.

According to a statement from Gabriela Gutentag, a press representative for Bay, the incident occurred on October 17, 2013, during the first day of production in Hong Kong, marking the start of a new phase of filming after four and a half months of shooting in various locations across the United States. The production company encountered a man, allegedly under the influence of a narcotic substance, who had earlier accosted several crew members, attempting to extort them for thousands of dollars.

The man, accompanied by two others, rushed onto the set in Quarry Bay and swung the air-conditioning unit directly at Bay's head, but the director ducked and wrestled the device away, preventing potential harm. The security team then intervened, and the police, who also scuffled with the assailant and two of his companions, arrested the three men. No one on the cast or crew was injured, and filming resumed immediately.

Michael Bay described the incident in a post on his website, comparing the assailant to a character from Brad Pitt's film "World War Z," who lifted several men off the ground and attempted to bite them. The assailant even bit into a guard's Nike shoe, which fortunately had an air bubble that popped, saving the toe. Bay expressed gratitude towards the Hong Kong authorities and the crew for their professionalism, stating that they had a great day of shooting despite the incident.

On October 17, 2013, during the filming of "Transformers 4" in Hong Kong, a chaotic scene unfolded when a man, allegedly under the influence of a narcotic substance, approached the set and attempted to intimidate the crew. According to multiple accounts, including those from Michael Bay's representatives and the director himself, the individual, described as a "zombie-like" figure, wielded an air-conditioning unit as a weapon and demanded money from the crew. 

The man, reportedly accompanied by two others, had previously accosted several crew members, attempting to extort them for thousands of dollars, specifically mentioning a sum of about $13,000. When confronted by Michael Bay, the situation escalated, and the man lunged at the director with the air-conditioning unit. However, Bay dodged the attack and managed to wrestle the device away, preventing potential harm.

As the situation spiralled out of control, the security team intervened, and the police arrived, eventually arresting four individuals, including the man wielding the air-conditioning unit and two of his companions, for assaulting the officers. The incident occurred on the first day of production in Hong Kong, which marked the beginning of a new phase of filming after four and a half months of shooting in various locations across the United States, including Arizona.

In a statement, Gabriela Gutentag, a press representative for Michael Bay, clarified that the director was not injured in the altercation, contrary to initial reports suggesting otherwise. Instead, Bay described the incident in a post on his website, comparing the assailant to a character from Brad Pitt's film "World War Z," who lifted several men off the ground and attempted to bite them. The incident even saw the assailant biting into a guard's Nike shoe, which fortunately had an air bubble that popped, saving the toe.

Despite the commotion, filming resumed shortly after, with Bay expressing gratitude towards the Hong Kong authorities and the crew for their professionalism. The incident drew attention from international media outlets, including Reuters and BBC News, which reported on the unusual demands made by the group and the subsequent arrest of the suspects.

He actually bit into one of the guards Nike shoe, insane," Bay writes. "Then it took 15 Hong Kong cops in riot gear to deal with these punks. In all, four guys were arrested for assaulting the officers."

final_summary:
In a recent court hearing held on Monday, Halle Berry, the Oscar-winning actress, took her ex-partner Gabriel Aubry, the father of their 6-year-old daughter Nahla, to court over a disagreement regarding the child's appearance. According to reports from TMZ and E! News, Berry alleged that Aubry had been straightening and highlighting Nahla's naturally curly hair while in his care, attempting to make her appear less African American. This move sparked a heated argument between the two, with Berry accusing Aubry of altering Nahla's appearance.

The incident occurred during a custody battle between the two, who have been embroiled in a contentious shared parenting arrangement since their breakup in 2010, two years after dating. Berry, who is of mixed heritage, has previously stated that she considers Nahla to be black and wants her to identify as such. However, Aubry, who is white, has been accused of trying to alter Nahla's appearance to fit his own preferences.

A judge ultimately intervened, ruling that neither parent can alter Nahla's hair color or texture, and instead ordered that it be allowed to grow back naturally. This decision came after Berry's lawyer, Steve Kolodny, appeared in court on her behalf, while Aubry also attended the hearing on Monday.

This is not the only issue that has arisen in the couple's custody battle. In 2012, Berry sought to reduce her child-support payments to Aubry from $16,000 per month to a lower amount, citing concerns that he has been living off the payments without contributing financially. Berry claimed that Aubry has been unemployed and has been taking advantage of the system by relying solely on her financial support.

The couple's relationship has been tumultuous, with a history of physical altercations and public disputes. In 2012, Aubry was arrested after a fight with Berry's then-fiancé, Olivier Martinez, and in 2013, Berry attempted to reduce her child-support payments to motivate Aubry to find employment.

In a 2011 interview with Ebony magazine, Berry expressed her desire for Nahla to choose her own racial identity, stating that she considered herself black and believed in the "one-drop rule," a concept that deems individuals with any amount of black ancestry to be black. Berry acknowledged the challenges of navigating multiple racial identities, saying that she had struggled to balance her black and white heritage throughout her life.

The ongoing custody battle between Berry and Aubry continues to raise questions about the complexities of co-parenting and the importance of respecting each other's differences in a blended family.

reference: – Halle Berry and Gabriel Aubry have long stood as models of how not to treat the person with whom you're co-parenting a child (see: brawl, citizen's arrest, insane child support), but they've taken their bickering over daughter Nahla to a new low: As TMZ reports, Berry hauled Aubry back into court on Monday, and the subject of contention was Nahla's hair, which Berry claims Aubry has been straightening and lightening in an apparent attempt to make the 6-year-old look less African American. (Aubry is white and Berry is biracial, notes E! Online). The judge ruled that neither parent can alter Nahla's hair. Berry identifies both herself and her daughter as black, notes the LA Times, saying in a 2011 interview that "I feel like she's black. I'm black and I'm her mother." Berry last month sought to reduce her child support payments to Aubry, adds the Times; she currently pays $16,000 a month and reportedly wants it cut to less than $4,000 to prompt the unemployed model to get a job.

final_summary:
Palau, a Pacific island state located in Oceania, is shifting its tourism strategy to focus on attracting high-end travelers and reducing the number of visitors. According to President Tommy Remengesau, the government aims to rebrand the country as a luxury destination by introducing a new law that would restrict the construction of hotels to only five-star establishments. This move is part of a broader effort to address concerns about the environmental impact of mass tourism, including damage to coral reefs and disturbance of wildlife caused by an influx of visitors.

Remengesau emphasized the importance of "quality rather than quantity" tourism, citing the need to develop the islands in a way that respects both the natural environment and the local population. To achieve this goal, the government is offering tax breaks and exemptions to high-end investors who can provide their own infrastructure, such as water treatment systems and power backups, and take care of other necessities like building roads.

The decision to limit tourism growth follows a significant increase in visitor numbers in recent years, with a notable surge in Chinese tour groups. In response, the government halved the number of charter flights from China in 2015, resulting in a decrease in overall visitor numbers but an increase in average spending per tourist. Remengesau believes this approach has confirmed the effectiveness of targeting high-end travelers, who contribute more to the local economy through their higher spending.

Palau relies heavily on tourism, accounting for 85% of its GDP, which is generated largely from image-rich destinations like its pristine beaches, world-class scuba diving, and forest-covered volcanic islands. However, the influx of visitors has put pressure on the country's resources, leading to concerns about overcrowding, reef damage, and wildlife disturbance. 

President Remengesau wants to encourage high-end hotels that "would come in with their own designed water treatment system [and] power back-ups. If there are any other infrastructures that they have to do, like opening a road, the investor would do that themselves." The hotels would be rewarded by the government for their assistance with "the necessary tax breaks and exemptions."

Palau is renowned for its idyllic shores, coral reefs, and desert islands, as captured by images from iStock/Norimoto. With 500 islands to choose from, Palau offers numerous opportunities for luxury resort development, although the exact locations for the new five-star hotels have yet to be determined. The government's aim is to promote sustainable and high-end tourism, striking a balance between economic benefits and environmental protection. As reported by Radio New Zealand International and Marianas Variety, the initiative shows promise, but questions remain about whether the hotels will be built in harmony with the natural environment, preserving the unique beauty of the islands that make Palau a sought-after destination.

reference: – The Pacific island nation of Palau boasts some stunning sights—but they don't want just anybody coming to check them out. The country's president, Tommy Remengesau, is planning to introduce legislation restricting hotel development to five-star chains, a move that would effectively make Palau a "luxury only" nation, the Telegraph reports. Remengesau says mass tourism is damaging the environment of Palau, which is made up of around 250 islands, and he wants to pursue a "quality not quantity" strategy. "There's a right way to do things and there's a wrong way to do things," says the president, per the BBC. And when it comes to development, he adds, "the best spots around the world have blended in with the environment." Radio New Zealand notes that the proposal comes after massive growth in tourism over the last couple of years, most of which involved package tours from mainland China. Remengesau says tourism spending actually went up after steps were taken to reduce the number of charter flights from China. (A tourism slogan in Australia is pretty darn explicit.)

final_summary:
On Tuesday, Russian hackers who breached the State Department's network last year used that foothold to gain access to the White House's unclassified computer system, allowing them to view sensitive information about President Obama's schedule. This breach occurred despite the White House's initial downplaying of its severity, with officials describing it as affecting only an unclassified system. However, the hackers gained access to real-time, non-public details of the president's schedule, which is considered highly sensitive and prized by foreign intelligence agencies.

The intrusion began when hackers sent a phishing email from a State Department account, infecting a White House computer with malware. The State Department has been battling its own highly sophisticated cyber intrusion for months, although it remains unclear which parts of its network remain breached. Private security experts had suspected a connection between the State Department and White House breaches, given Russia's history of infiltrating U.S. critical infrastructure, including oil and gas pipelines, wind turbines, and nuclear power plants.

White House officials confirmed in October that its unclassified system had been breached, with computers and systems not damaged but some services disrupted as cybersecurity experts worked to limit hacker activity. By late February, the vast majority of systems were back online, according to White House Press Secretary Josh Earnest. However, the breach highlights the risks associated with the White House's reliance on unclassified systems, particularly those handling sensitive information like the president's schedule.

Ben Rhodes, President Obama's Deputy National Security Adviser, acknowledged the potential vulnerability of the unclassified system, stating that the White House uses a separate system for classified information to protect sensitive national security-related items from being obtained by hackers. He emphasized that the White House takes regular actions to prevent vulnerabilities and enhance security on the unclassified system.

The breach is part of a larger pattern of Russian cyberattacks targeting the U.S., with Director of National Intelligence James Clapper warning in January that the Russian cyber threat is more severe than previously assessed. The attack on the State Department's system, which began before Clinton's departure as Secretary of State, has left some officials questioning the safety of her private email server, which critics argue may have been even less secure than the State Department's system.

The White House hack was first reported by CNN, with officials confirming that the breach occurred last year, during a period of heightened tensions between the U.S. and Russia over Ukraine. The hack was described as a demonstration of Russia's willingness to target the heart of American power, with CBS News senior national security analyst Juan Zarate noting that Russia, along with China and other nation-states, possesses advanced cyber tools capable of causing significant harm.

US officials have expressed concern over the breach, with Senator Susan Collins calling the revelations "troubling" and highlighting the need for improved cybersecurity measures. The incident has led to a renewed focus on the importance of protecting sensitive information and preventing future breaches, particularly in light of the ongoing cyber threats posed by Russia and other nations.

reference: – Russian hackers' apparent infiltration of the White House computer network in October wasn't exactly confidence-inspiring, even though it was the unclassified portion of the system that was breached. Now US officials say some of the unclassified information that made its way onto hackers' screens was related to President Obama's itineraries—not classified knowledge, but not exactly public knowledge, notes CNN. The Hill points out that although the press has access to "a general outline" of Obama's daily routine, there are still phone calls and meetings that aren't made public, and that foreign spies would apparently love to know about. Obama's exact location at any given point during the day is also often kept on the down-low, and certain parts of the White House are off-limits to the press, the Hill adds. Besides the White House scheduling and press offices, others who use the hacked system include the general counsel's office and the budget and legislative liaison offices, ABC News reports. The hackers are believed to have accessed the White House network by first hacking State Department files, according to investigators, which leads to the fear that hackers have gotten back into the State Department system. One official tells CNN that the hackers have "owned" the network "for months." "There's always vulnerability," Deputy National Security Adviser Ben Rhodes noted at a press conference, per CBS News. "The fact is that's why we have a classified system because there's less risk in the classified system, and that is secure." (The FBI is going after one Russian hacker in particular.)

final_summary:
President Donald Trump announced on Thursday that the administration would cease making payments to insurers under the Affordable Care Act (ACA) program, a move that could significantly destabilize the healthcare market. The decision, which affects an estimated $7 billion in payments this year, comes as the 2018 open enrollment period is set to begin soon. According to the White House, the Department of Health and Human Services has determined that there is no appropriation for the cost-sharing reduction payments, citing guidance from the Justice Department.

This move is seen as the most aggressive attempt yet by Trump to dismantle the ACA, a law that has expanded private insurance to millions of people through the creation of marketplaces, also known as exchanges, where individuals can purchase plans often subsidized by the government. The law requires that plans offered on the exchanges include essential benefits, such as hospital care, maternity care, and mental health services, and prohibits insurers from denying coverage to people with pre-existing medical conditions.

Healthcare experts warn that the cessation of payments could lead to higher costs for those remaining in ACA plans, as healthier individuals may opt for cheaper, short-term plans that are exempt from ACA requirements. These short-term plans, which do not adhere to the same standards as ACA-compliant plans, could attract large numbers of healthy individuals, leaving behind a riskier pool of patients and driving up premiums.

Trump's decision has sparked widespread criticism from Democrats, with Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi labeling it "spiteful sabotage" that will harm working families and the middle class. New York Attorney General Eric Schneiderman has vowed to sue the administration to prevent the action, while some insurers may also pursue litigation to recover the lost funds.

The move has also raised concerns among Republican lawmakers, with Representative Ileana Ros-Lehtinen expressing her opposition to the decision, stating that it will result in more uninsured individuals in her district. Despite the backlash, the White House maintains that the decision is necessary to address the law's flaws and that Congress should take responsibility for fixing the issue.

However, Trump has hinted that he may reconsider the decision if a bipartisan agreement on healthcare reform is reached. According to sources, he has privately told at least one lawmaker that the payments may continue if a deal is struck. The fate of the subsidies remains uncertain, but the consequences of the administration's decision are clear: a potentially volatile healthcare market and increased financial burdens on those relying on the ACA for coverage.
anchors: The Department Of Health And Human Services

reference: – In a move that analysts say could be a devastating blow to ObamaCare, President Trump is ending one of the health care bill's crucial subsidies. White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders confirmed Thursday night that the federal government will stop "cost-sharing reduction" payments to health insurers, which help millions of people afford insurance they get through ObamaCare's exchanges, Politico reports. Sanders called the payments a "bailout of insurance companies" and "another example of how the previous administration abused taxpayer dollars and skirted the law to prop up a broken system." Related coverage: The impact: The Washington Post likens the decision to "throwing a bomb into the marketplaces" created by ObamaCare. Insurers have said this is the strongest step Trump could take to undermine the law, because rising costs would force them to bail without the promise of getting reimbursed. The alternative could be to remain, but to raise premiums to levels most couldn't afford. The New York Times also calls the subsidies "essential" to the Affordable Care Act. Trump's possible offer: "The Democrats ObamaCare is imploding," the president tweeted Friday morning. "Massive subsidy payments to their pet insurance companies has stopped. Dems should call me to fix!" The last part might be the most telling. The Wall Street Journal reports that Trump has told at least one lawmaker that he's open to preserving the payments if a bipartisan deal is struck on health care. All eyes on this: Trump is referring to a deal being negotiated by GOP Sen. Lamar Alexander and Democratic Sen. Patty Murray. Under the broad strokes, the subsidies would be continued, a win for Democrats, but states would get more flexibility to avoid ObamaCare rules, a win for Republicans, per the Journal. Big deadline: Open enrollment for the ObamaCare exchanges begins in less than three weeks, raising pressure for a quick resolution. Background: A federal court declared the subsidies illegal last year, though the decision is being appealed. The subsidies add up to around $7 billion this year, a figure expected to steadily rise in coming years. The attorneys general of California and New York vowed Thursday night that they would challenge the latest move in court, while Nancy Pelosi and Chuck Schumer called it "a spiteful act of vast, pointless sabotage leveled at working families and the middle class in every corner of America." Second move: Earlier Thursday, Trump signed an executive order that's expected to undermine ObamaCare in a different way.

final_summary: On Friday, Disney announced the cast for the next installment in the Star Wars saga, including the late Carrie Fisher reprising her iconic role as Princess Leia Organa. Director J.J. Abrams revealed that Fisher will be featured in the film using previously unreleased footage shot for "The Force Awakens," stating that they "desperately loved Carrie Fisher" and were determined not to recast or create a computer-generated character. Abrams expressed gratitude towards Fisher's daughter, Billie Lourd, for giving her support and blessing for the decision to utilize the unseen footage from "Episode VII." This move comes after Disney initially stated that there were no plans to digitally recreate Fisher's performance following her passing in 2016. However, Abrams explained that they found a way to honor Fisher's legacy and role as Leia in the Skywalker saga through the use of the existing footage. The film's production is set to begin soon, with Abrams returning as director alongside co-writer Chris Terrio. Other returning cast members include Daisy Ridley, Adam Driver, John Boyega, Oscar Isaac, Domhnall Gleeson, Kelly Marie Tran, Joonas Suotamo, Mark Hamill, Anthony Daniels, and Billy Dee Williams, along with newcomers Naomi Ackie and Richard E. Grant. Lucasfilm president Kathleen Kennedy had previously indicated that Fisher's death would halt her involvement with the picture, but Abrams and his team were able to find a solution. The film is expected to hit theaters later this year, with John Williams set to compose the score once again.

Abrams expressed gratitude towards Fisher's daughter, Billie Lourd, for giving her support and blessing for the decision to utilize the unseen footage from "Episode VII." This move comes after Disney initially stated in 2016 that there were no plans to digitally recreate Fisher's performance following her passing at the age of 60 due to a cardiac event while traveling from London to Los Angeles. Despite this initial stance, Abrams explained that they found a way to honor Fisher's legacy and role as Leia in "Episode IX" through the use of the existing footage. The film's production is set to begin on August 1, with Abrams returning as director alongside co-writer Chris Terrio. Other returning cast members include Daisy Ridley, Adam Driver, John Boyega, Oscar Isaac, Lupita Nyong'o, Domhnall Gleeson, Kelly Marie Tran, Joonas Suotamo, Mark Hamill, Anthony Daniels, and Billy Dee Williams, along with newcomers Naomi Ackie and Richard E. Grant. The film is scheduled to release on December 20, 2019, with John Williams set to compose the score once again.

reference: – Excellent news for fans of Princess Leia. Or in this case, General Leia. The late Carrie Fisher will appear in the upcoming Star Wars film thanks to unused footage, reports People. Director JJ Abrams announced the move Friday, and he said it had the blessing of Fisher's daughter, Billie Lourd. Star Wars: Episode IX, which caps the latest trilogy in the series, is due in theaters late next year, reports CNN. "We desperately loved Carrie Fisher," said Abrams. "Finding a truly satisfying conclusion to the Skywalker saga without her eluded us," (and) "we were never going to recast, or use a CG character." Fisher died in 2016 at age 60 of cardiac arrest. (See more stories about Fisher here.)

final_summary:
On a routine patrol in South Pasadena, California, a rookie police officer named Chris Perez, who had been on the force for only a year in Southern California, received a distressing call about a possible jumper on the York Bridge. As he arrived at the scene, a 12-year-old girl was perched on a 6-inch ledge, teetering on the edge of an 80-foot drop. Perez approached the situation with caution, knowing that a single misstep could prove fatal.

Instead of rushing towards the girl, Perez walked calmly towards her, establishing a connection before initiating a conversation. The girl revealed that she had fled a group home in Los Angeles due to severe emotional distress, citing poor treatment and confinement. Perez employed a cautious and empathetic approach, engaging in a series of conversations with the girl to build trust and establish a rapport.

Through careful questioning, Perez learned that the girl was scared and needed help, and he devised a plan to safely rescue her. He asked permission to move closer and eventually managed to coax her onto the sidewalk, where he offered her water and comforted her as she broke down in tears. When the girl complained of an ankle injury, Perez promptly called for medical assistance.

The incident occurred on a Tuesday evening, and the girl, who had been living in a group home in Los Angeles, was taken into protective custody for further evaluation. According to police officials, the girl is safe thanks to Perez's calm and professional actions. In an interview with CBS News correspondent Carter Evans, Perez recounted the harrowing experience, highlighting the importance of patience and empathy in crisis situations.

Perez's heroic actions were witnessed by bystanders, including those who initially alerted authorities to the situation. His parents, both of whom are also law enforcement officers, expressed pride in their son's selfless act. While Perez remains humble about his role in saving the girl's life, he acknowledged the significance of his actions and the impact it has had on his family.

Just an hour into his shift, Perez was told about the young girl on the bridge. She told him she wanted to be reunited with her only family member, an aunt. Perez is reluctant to call himself a hero and uncomfortable that others are calling him one – but he is glad his parents noticed. Hearing it from his parents is one thing, he says, because he knows he did right by them.

Perez employed a gentle and empathetic approach, engaging in a series of conversations with the girl to build trust and establish a rapport.

reference: – Faced with a 12-year-old girl hanging by an arm from an 80-foot-high bridge, threatening to end her young life, a rookie cop started talking, reports CBS Los Angeles. Asking the girl if he could come closer, Officer Chris Perez says the child "was just crying and holding on;" she told Perez she had fled a group home in LA, and that she needed help. "It was just back-and-forth banter with me and her," Perez says. "'Can I come closer to you? Can I talk you closer? Can I pull you over the ledge so we can talk in a safer environment?' It was that back-to-back conversation with her that I felt was going so smoothly that I felt comfortable enough to lean over and get her." Per CBS, Perez "leaned over the railing, grabbed her in a bear hug, and helped her over the railing to safety." Police officials credit Perez's "calm, professional actions" with saving the girl, while Perez himself seems appalled that someone so young could find herself so desperate. "I can’t relate, that, oh my Lord, this young individual, this juvenile, is on the other side [on the bridge] what could possibly be going wrong in your life to make you want to do something like this?" But after the rescue, "she was sad and just started breaking down even more," Perez tells CBS News. The girl, who wanted to reconnect with an aunt, her only family, was put in police custody.

final_summary:
According to a recent survey conducted by ForbesWoman and the National Endowment for Financial Education (NEFE), nearly one-third of Americans who share their finances with their spouses admit to lying to them about money, while another third claim to have been deceived. This staggering statistic comes from an online poll, commissioned by ForbesWoman and NEFE and conducted by Harris Interactive, which surveyed 2,019 U.S. adults from December 17 to 21. The results reveal that financial infidelity is a widespread issue affecting many relationships.

Among those who admitted to financial dishonesty, the majority involve hiding small transactions such as cash, minor purchases, and bills, but a significant number of individuals also conceal major purchases, maintain secret bank accounts, and falsify information about their debt or earnings. NEFE's Chief Executive, Ted Beck, emphasizes that "a third of the population admits to not being honest with their spouse," which can lead to severe consequences, including arguments, reduced trust, and even divorce. Specifically, 67% of couples impacted by financial infidelity reported that the deception led to an argument, while 16% said it resulted in a divorce and 11% stated it led to a separation.

Psychotherapist Tina Tessina notes that financial deceit can be just as damaging as other forms of infidelity, causing a total loss of trust, feelings of betrayal, and relationship destruction. Seattle resident Theo Pauline Nestor experienced this firsthand when she discovered her husband's secret gambling habit and accumulated debt, which ultimately led to their divorce after 13 years of marriage.

The survey found that over half of financial cheaters hide cash (58%) or minor purchases (54%), with 30% admitting to concealing bills, 16% hiding major purchases, 15% maintaining secret bank accounts, 11% lying about their debts, and 11% misrepresenting their earnings. Matrimonial and family law attorney Nancy Chemtob, who specializes in cases involving financial infidelity, observes that having more wealth does not necessarily mean more honesty, as individuals with higher incomes often engage in more complex schemes to deceive their partners.

Chemtob cites examples of clients who have been victims of financial infidelity, including a woman who fabricated her entire financial history during courtship and another who lied about receiving child support. Family therapist Carleton Kendrick attributes financial dishonesty to various motivations, such as pragmatism, control, guilt, and fear, and notes that societal norms and upbringing can influence individual attitudes towards money in relationships.

Interestingly, the survey reveals that men and women are equally likely to engage in financial infidelity, although women are more likely to report discovering their partner's deceit. However, men tend to perceive their partners as hiding minor purchases more frequently than women, whereas women are more likely to accuse their partners of lying about their earnings and debt. NEFE's Beck recommends discussing finances openly and honestly, ideally before marriage, and suggests using tax season as a non-threatening opportunity to address financial concerns.
anchors: The National Endowment For Financial Education

reference: – Infidelity isn't always the stuff of illicit affairs. A new survey shows that 31% of Americans who combine their finances with a spouse have lied about money to said spouse. "Financial infidelity may be the new normal," writes Jenna Goudreau at her Forbes blog. The most common misdeeds were hiding cash (58%), hiding minor purchases (54%), and hiding a bill (30%). But a decent number admitted to hiding major purchases (16%), keeping a secret bank account (15%), and lying about debt or earnings (11%). “Betrayal regarding money can be just as painful and damaging as other kinds of cheating," says a psychotherapist. It, too, can lead to "total loss of trust, feelings of betrayal and destruction of the relationship." For those feeling a little suspicious, Forbes has a list of 10 red flags (defensiveness, insistence on handling bills alone, etc.) to look for.

final_summary:
How many times in your life – heck, how many times just this month, or this year – have you hovered over that little ticky box without bothering to click the TOS link first? Or scrolled straight to the bottom of a pop-up window with 17 pages of boring legalese in it, just to continue installation? If your answer is anything other than "all the times," you are in a very, very small minority.

A new study from researchers at two universities has confirmed what most of us already anecdotally know: nobody's actually reading the fine print, even if they should. According to a recent study, a staggering 98% of people fail to read the fine print when signing up for online services. The study involved creating a fictitious social networking site called NameDrop, whose terms of service and privacy policy were modeled after those of LinkedIn. However, the researchers added several "gotcha" clauses that would normally raise red flags, including one stating that users would have to share their data with security agencies in the United States and abroad, potentially impacting their eligibility for various aspects of life, and another requiring users to assign their firstborn child to the site, with the agreement becoming enforceable until the year 2050 if the user did not yet have children.

The study found that out of 543 participants, only 10-11 noticed the existence of these clauses, while the rest either skipped the fine print altogether or quickly scanned it without fully understanding its contents. On average, participants who chose to read the terms of service spent just 51 seconds doing so, while those who read the privacy policy averaged 73 seconds. These speeds are far below the estimated time it would take to thoroughly comprehend the lengthy documents, which consisted of approximately 8000 and 4000 words, respectively.

This phenomenon is not unique to younger generations; a similar experiment conducted in the UK in 2014 found the same results, where users unknowingly signed away their firstborn in exchange for access to a free WiFi hotspot. In 2010, a UK-based retailer discovered that customers were willing to sign over their rights without hesitation. Other studies have shown that fewer than one in five internet users actually reads the terms of service, and even those who do often don't bother to process the information.

The study's findings highlight the importance of critically evaluating online agreements before clicking "accept." As long as companies disclose their intentions, they can essentially do whatever they want with users' accounts and personal information. The researchers attribute this lack of attention to the complexity and length of the policies, which are often poorly formatted and filled with legalese. While there are tools available to aid comprehension, there is still a long way to go in making online policies more accessible and understandable. A study published on Ars Technica in April 2016, last revised in August 2018, and written in June 2018, further emphasizes the issue, suggesting that information overload is a significant negative predictor of reading terms of service upon signup.

reference: – If Facebook or Apple ever turns truly evil, we might be in big trouble, apparently. A study published last week found that almost nobody reads online terms of service agreements—and even fewer understand them. Ars Technica reports 543 college students signed up for a fake social networking site called NameDrop, believing they were helping with a "pre-launch evaluation." They were actually agreeing to hand over their first-born child to NameDrop—enforceable through 2050—as well as have their data and information shared with the NSA, foreign security agencies, and all manner of third parties, according to Consumerist. But, of course, only 2% of participants realized they were agreeing to any of that. The study found 399 participants didn't read any of the 12,000 words comprising NameDrop's terms of service and privacy policy. The other 144 spent an average of 73 seconds reading the privacy policy and 51 seconds reading the terms of service. Based on average reading speed, it should have taken 30 minutes and 16 minutes respectively. In the end, 98% of participants didn't notice any of the onerous clauses in NameDrops's terms. The study concluded that people "view policies as nuisance" and that "I agree to these terms and conditions" is the "biggest lie on the internet." (Amazon will void its terms of service if the zombies come.)

final_summary:
In a tense standoff over the U.S. debt ceiling, Speaker John Boehner has reiterated his stance that President Barack Obama must accept entitlement cuts without tax increases in exchange for a debt-ceiling increase. This hardline approach comes ahead of crucial talks at the White House between Obama and congressional leaders aimed at averting a U.S. default. Boehner's comments follow Obama's praise for the Speaker's willingness to explore a "grand bargain" involving significant spending reductions on popular programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and Social Security in return for as much as $1 trillion in new revenue. However, Boehner abruptly ended their one-on-one discussions on Saturday after facing criticism from conservative lawmakers and activists, including House Majority Leader Eric Cantor.

Boehner maintained that the disagreement between him and Obama lies in policy, stating that the president wants to raise taxes on job creators while he believes the focus should be on reducing entitlements. He emphasized that the House cannot pass a bill increasing taxes on job creators and instead proposed a debt-limit increase tied to spending cuts exceeding the hike in the debt limit, along with measures to restrain future spending. Additionally, Boehner advocated for the passage of a balanced-budget amendment, set to be debated in the House next week.

Boehner disputed claims that he had discussed tax increases during his private talks with Obama, asserting that his goal was to simplify and lower tax rates, which would stimulate economic growth and generate additional revenue for the federal government. However, opposition from Cantor and Senate Republicans led Boehner to retreat on such an agreement. In a separate statement, Boehner acknowledged that he shares Obama's concern about raising the U.S. borrowing limit to prevent a government default but remains adamant that House Republicans will not support any deal involving tax hikes.

Boehner expressed his preference for significant tax reform, suggesting that increased revenues could be achieved without raising taxes. He criticized the White House for its perceived lack of commitment to reforming major social benefit programs, echoing his earlier assertion that Obama insists on raising taxes. The Gop leader emphasized that the House cannot pass a bill that raises taxes on job creators. The U.S. government faces an August deadline to reach a deal that combines spending cuts and a debt-ceiling increase.

history.draft:
In a tense standoff over the US debt ceiling, Speaker John Boehner has reiterated his stance that President Barack Obama must accept entitlement cuts without tax increases in exchange for a debt-ceiling increase. This hardline approach comes ahead of crucial talks at the White House between Obama and congressional leaders aimed at avoiding a US default. Boehner's comments follow Obama's praise for the Speaker's willingness to explore a "grand bargain" involving significant spending reductions on popular programs like Medicare, Medicaid, and Social Security in return for up to $1 trillion in new revenue. However, Boehner abruptly ended their one-on-one discussions on Saturday after facing backlash from conservative lawmakers and activists, including House Majority Leader Eric Cantor.

Boehner maintained that the disagreement between him and Obama lies in policy, not personality, stating that the president wants to raise taxes on job creators while he believes the focus should be on reducing entitlements. He emphasized that the House cannot pass a bill increasing taxes on job creators and instead proposed a debt-limit increase tied to spending cuts exceeding the hike in the debt limit, along with measures to restrain future spending. Additionally, Boehner advocated for the passage of a balanced-budget amendment, set to be debated in the House next week.

Boehner disputed claims that he had discussed tax increases during his private talks with Obama, asserting that his goal was to simplify and lower tax rates, which would stimulate economic growth and generate additional revenue for the federal government. However, opposition from Cantor and Senate Republicans led Boehner to abandon this approach. In a separate statement, Boehner acknowledged that he shares Obama's concern about raising the US borrowing limit to prevent a government default but remains adamant that House Republicans will not support any deal involving tax hikes.

Boehner expressed his preference for significant tax reform, suggesting that increased revenues could be achieved without raising taxes. He criticized the White House for its perceived lack of commitment to reforming major social benefit programs, echoing his earlier assertion that Obama insists on raising taxes. With the August 2 deadline looming, Obama and congressional leaders continue to work towards a deal that combines spending cuts and a debt-ceiling increase.
