reference: – John Boehner stuck to his guns in a press conference today, offering precious little hope that a debt ceiling deal was anywhere close to getting done. He thanked Barack Obama for praising his "good-faith effort," but said it mattered little. “Our disagreements are not personal,” he said, according to Politico. “They never have been. The gulf between the two parties now is about policy.” “This boil downs to two things: The president insists on raising taxes, and they’re just not serious enough [about] fundamental entitlement reform,” Boehner said. “It takes two to tango, and they’re just not there yet.” Boehner said he did fundamentally agree that the debt limit needed to rise, the AP reports. But he said the bill could only pass the House if it included an even larger amount of spending cuts.

final_summary:
In the wake of the surprise Season 3 premiere of Rick and Morty, fans have been clamoring for the revival of McDonald's Szechuan dipping sauce, which was released in 1998 to promote the Disney movie Mulan. This nostalgia-fueled frenzy has led to a thriving market for rare and discontinued food products, with enthusiasts willing to pay exorbitant prices for a taste of their childhood. A prime example of this phenomenon is the sale of a 20-year-old packet of the coveted sauce on eBay for a staggering $14,700.

Brian Florence, the curator of Sodafinder.com, a website dedicated to cataloging and selling hard-to-find soft drinks, has capitalized on this trend. With a collection of over 400 12-packs of canned soft drinks, Florence travels extensively throughout the country to hunt down unique regional varieties, often using his findings to fuel his online business. His customers, mostly nostalgic adults seeking to recapture their childhood memories, are willing to pay premium prices for rare sodas like Wink, Buffalo Rock Ginger Ale, and Coca-Cola made with real sugar.

Patrick Meynders, an Amazon seller from Alberta, Canada, has also tapped into this market, specializing in discontinued snacks like Dunkaroos, which he imports from Europe to meet the demands of his customers. Similarly, Josh Nichols, an eBay vendor from Texas, has built a business around selling Dr. Pepper, particularly the Dublin Dr. Pepper variant made with real cane sugar, which he purchases directly from the manufacturer. Another notable entrepreneur is Pam Lloyd-Camp, who runs a robust eBay candy shop called junk-it-junction, selling old-timey favorites like giant Jawbreakers, candy cigarettes, and Sugar Daddies.

The appeal of these products lies in their ability to evoke nostalgia and transport consumers back to a simpler, more carefree era. Darrel D. Muehling, a marketing expert, notes that nostalgia is a powerful tool in advertising, allowing brands to tap into consumers' fond memories and create a sense of longing. However, this nostalgia can be a double-edged sword, as evidenced by the mixed reactions to Meynders' Dunkaroos, with some customers praising the taste while others express disappointment due to changes in formulation.

As the demand for these products continues to grow, entrepreneurs like Florence and Meynders must navigate the complex landscape of sourcing, pricing, and distribution. Florence has developed a system for setting prices based on his costs, including transportation and food expenses, while also adjusting for demand. Meanwhile, Meynders relies on his relationships with suppliers and his ability to adapt to changing market conditions.

Jerry, Rick's hapless counterpart, would likely approach this situation differently, perhaps using Jerry's credit card to acquire the sauce. The revival of McDonald's Szechuan dipping sauce remains a topic of debate, with the company's spokesperson hinting that they may reconsider bringing back the product in response to customer demand. Until then, fans will continue to scour eBay and other online platforms, hoping to stumble upon a rare packet of the elusive sauce.

reference: – This is either a big fan of the cartoon Rick and Morty, a hopeful collector, or a very brave and nostalgic soul: A packet of 1998 McDonald's Szechuan sauce has sold for $14,700 on eBay, reports Time. The strange background: McDonald's put out the dipping sauce in that year as a publicity tie-in for the Disney movie Mulan, and it received a giant nostalgic push when the character Rick from the popular Adult Swim show pined for it in a recent episode. That led to a viral campaign for McDonald's to bring it back, complete with multiple Change.org petitions. The company has hinted it might do so, and hopeful fans have noted that a remake of Mulan is in the works for next year. But the eBay sale is the strangest component yet. "I just bought a really old car, while cleaning it I found a packet of this sauce," the listing stated. "After watching the recent episode of Rick and Morty I went online to see if it was worth anything. Turns out it was. Also this comes with a packet of wasabi as well." A post at Eater notes that what it calls "nostalgic food" is popular on eBay, "but this is an unusually high price tag." No word yet on the identity of the buyer, or plans for the sauce, but the Daily Dot is issuing a challenge: The character Rick would definitely eat the stuff, "so how about it, new owner of rare McDonald’s Mulan dipping sauce. Are you squanchy enough to be a Rick?" (A brother recently drove his sister to McDonald's, which is notable only because they have a combined age of 11.)

final_summary:
Multiple high-profile celebrity couples have announced their separations in recent months. Singer Mariah Carey and actor Nick Cannon have reportedly been living apart since May, with sources indicating that their separation has been ongoing for months. Divorce lawyers have been negotiating a property settlement and custody agreement. Although the exact reason behind their split remains unclear, insiders suggest it may be linked to a public appearance made by Nick Cannon on Big Boy's radio show in March, where he discussed his personal life, including naming five celebrities he had slept with. This incident allegedly led to a breakdown in their relationship.

Ronnie Magro and Sammi Giancola, a couple from the reality TV show Jersey Shore, have officially ended their five-year on-again, off-again romance. In an interview, Magro stated that the couple grew apart and stopped spending quality time together, ultimately deciding it was best to go their separate ways. Magro has since become involved in caring for his friend Jenni "JWoww" Farley's newborn daughter, Meilani.

Actress Meg Ryan and musician John Mellencamp have ended their relationship after over three years together. The couple cited the strain of their long-distance lifestyle as the reason for their split, with Ryan residing in New York and Mellencamp based in Indiana. Mellencamp has expressed his preference for a quieter life in Indiana, away from the fast pace of city life and constant media attention. Ryan has focused on her directorial debut, Ithaca, a WWII drama.

"It was the long distance that ultimately was the cause," says one source; Ryan, 52, lives in New York while Mellencamp, 62, lives in Indiana. Rodriguez, 36, and Efron, 26, reportedly had a big fight in Ibiza that prompted the split. In slightly more D-list news, Jersey Shore couple Ronnie Magro and Sammi "Sweetheart" Giancola are done for good after an on-again, off-again five-year relationship, Magro tells People. "We were drifting apart and not really spending any time together," he says. "She's a great girl, but we were growing distant." And, though there's no official word on this yet, multiple outlets are reporting that Mariah Carey and Nick Cannon are all but divorced. Lawyers have been negotiating property and custody agreements for months, according to TMZ, and the couple has been living separately since May. According to Page Six's sources, Carey, 45, thinks Cannon, 33, might have cheated.

final_summary:
Dave Mirra, a legendary figure in the world of BMX, tragically passed away on February 4 at the age of 41. His untimely death was met with shock and sadness from the BMX community and beyond. Recent revelations have shed light on the underlying cause of his demise, revealing that Mirra suffered from Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative disease linked to repeated head trauma. According to neuropathologist Lili-Naz Hazrati of the University of Toronto, Mirra's brain showed signs of CTE, a condition previously associated with football and hockey players. This diagnosis marks a significant milestone, making Mirra the first action sports athlete to be officially diagnosed with CTE. Hazrati's findings were corroborated by multiple neuropathologists from both the US and Canada, further solidifying the conclusion.

Mirra's CTE diagnosis raises concerns about the long-term effects of repeated head trauma in action sports. Throughout his illustrious career, Mirra accumulated numerous concussions, including a particularly severe incident at age 19 when he was struck by a car while walking outside a nightclub. Despite suffering a fractured skull and a blood clot in his brain, Mirra remarkably recovered and continued to pursue his passion for BMX. However, his determination and resilience may have ultimately contributed to his downfall. Mirra's wife, Lauren, revealed in an interview with ESPN The Magazine that her husband began exhibiting noticeable personality changes in his final weeks, struggling with mood swings, memory loss, and depression. These symptoms are characteristic of advanced stages of CTE, a disease that can only be diagnosed post-mortem.

Lauren's account highlights the devastating impact of CTE on individuals and their loved ones. She emphasized the importance of raising awareness about the disease, stating, "This is the beginning of bringing awareness. It would be amazing if this is something we can detect in life one day. If we can detect it, prevent it, stop it, let's do all of the above." As the BMX community mourns the loss of a beloved icon, Mirra's legacy serves as a poignant reminder of the risks associated with action sports. His story underscores the need for greater understanding and support for athletes who suffer from CTE and its consequences.

In the wake of his passing, Mirra's family has expressed gratitude for the outpouring of support and has vowed to continue advocating for CTE awareness and research. In recognition of his contributions to the sport, Mirra will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame in June and will be honored at the X Games with a new event bearing his name, the Dave Mirra BMX Park Best Trick competition. While these tributes serve as a testament to Mirra's enduring impact, they also serve as a poignant reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of prioritizing athlete safety and well-being.

In recognition of his contributions to the sport, Mirra will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame in June and will be honored at the X Games with a new event bearing his name, the Dave Mirra BMX Park Best Trick competition. While these tributes serve as a testament to Mirra's enduring impact, they also serve as a poignant reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of prioritizing athlete safety and well-being.

history.draft:
Dave Mirra, a legendary figure in the world of BMX, tragically passed away on February 4 at the age of 41. His untimely death was met with shock and sadness from the BMX community and beyond. Recent revelations have shed light on the underlying cause of his demise, revealing that Mirra suffered from Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative disease linked to repeated head trauma. According to neuropathologist Lili-Naz Hazrati of the University of Toronto, Mirra's brain showed signs of CTE, a condition previously associated with football and hockey players. This diagnosis marks a significant milestone, making Mirra the first action sports athlete to be officially diagnosed with CTE. Hazrati's findings were corroborated by multiple neuropathologists from both the US and Canada, further solidifying the conclusion.

Mirra's CTE diagnosis raises concerns about the long-term effects of repeated head trauma in action sports. Throughout his illustrious career, Mirra accumulated numerous concussions, including a particularly severe incident at age 19 when he was struck by a car while walking outside a nightclub. Despite suffering a fractured skull and a blood clot in his brain, Mirra remarkably recovered and continued to pursue his passion for BMX. However, his determination and resilience may have ultimately contributed to his downfall. Mirra's wife, Lauren, revealed in an interview with ESPN The Magazine that her husband began exhibiting noticeable personality changes in his final weeks, struggling with mood swings, memory loss, and depression. These symptoms are characteristic of advanced stages of CTE, a disease that can only be diagnosed post-mortem.

Lauren's account highlights the devastating impact of CTE on individuals and their loved ones. She emphasized the importance of raising awareness about the disease, stating, "This is the beginning of bringing awareness. It would be amazing if this is something we can detect in life one day. If we can detect it, prevent it, stop it, let's do all of the above." As the BMX community mourns the loss of a beloved icon, Mirra's legacy serves as a poignant reminder of the risks associated with action sports. His story underscores the need for greater understanding and support for athletes who suffer from CTE and its consequences.

In the wake of his passing, Mirra's family has expressed gratitude for the outpouring of support and has vowed to continue advocating for CTE awareness and research. In recognition of his contributions to the sport, Mirra will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame in June and will be honored at the X Games with a new event bearing his name, the Dave Mirra BMX Park Best Trick competition. While these tributes serve as a testament to Mirra's enduring impact, they also serve as a poignant reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of prioritizing athlete safety and well-being.

reference: – Dave Mirra, the legendary BMX star who killed himself in February, had chronic traumatic encephalopathy—a disease more commonly seen in football players and boxers, ESPN reports. He's believed to be the first action sports athlete to be diagnosed with it. "The key is brain injury," says the neuropathologist who diagnosed Mirra. "Regardless of how you get it, through BMX or hockey, you are at risk for this." Mirra suffered numerous concussions throughout his BMX career. He once described a 2006 crash to the Washington Post by saying, “I basically fell 16 feet straight to my head.” “This is a young man that had a pretty rugged sports career and took a lot of injuries," USA Today quotes a friend as saying. Mirra's diagnosis of CTE was confirmed by multiple neuropathologists, though it's unclear what stage the disease was in. Scans of Mirra's brain showed the same tau protein deposits as those in the brains of former football players. CTE, which is only able to be diagnosed after death, can cause depression, dementia, and memory loss. Mirra's wife Lauren says Mirra underwent major mood and personality changes in the year before he killed himself at 41. “We ask for your continued support in honoring Dave’s legacy and for your patience as we plan to create a platform for CTE awareness and research," reads a statement from Lauren, per USA Today. Mirra, who "was the face of his sport," will be inducted into the National BMX Hall of Fame next month. (Also on Tuesday, Bubba Smith was announced as the 90th former NFL player to be diagnosed with CTE.)

final_summary:
A federal wrongful death lawsuit filed by the parents of Megan Rondini, a University of Alabama student who died by suicide in 2016, alleges that the university and local law enforcement mishandled her case after she reported being raped by T.J. Bunn Jr., a local man from a well-connected and influential family in the community. According to the lawsuit, Megan met Bunn at a popular bar in Tuscaloosa, where she had gone out with friends, and was later taken to his home, where she was allegedly sexually assaulted, possibly after being drugged. 

BuzzFeed News reported on the case, revealing that the police investigation was marred by allegations of bias towards Bunn and a lack of attention given to Megan's account of events. Investigators questioned Megan about her actions during the alleged assault, including taking a handgun and $3 for cab fare from Bunn's home, rather than focusing on gathering evidence to support her claim of rape. The lawsuit claims that Megan was treated as a suspect rather than a victim, and that her status as a survivor of a sex crime was disregarded. Despite testing positive for a sexually transmitted disease that she likely contracted from Bunn, Megan's allegations were not taken seriously by the authorities. The investigation was further compromised by the failure to test her rape kit, interview witnesses, and record all interviews conducted.

The University of Alabama's Title IX coordinator, Beth Howard, and a therapist from the Women and Gender Resource Center, Cara Blakes, were also named in the lawsuit for their alleged mishandling of Megan's case. However, the university claimed that it had provided Megan with support and resources, including meeting her at the hospital, providing information about services available on campus, and referring her to a new therapist after the initial one recused herself due to a conflict of interest. However, the lawsuit disputes this, stating that Megan was denied counseling services and that the university showed hostility towards her.

Megan eventually dropped the case, left the university, and returned to her hometown of Austin, Texas, where she struggled with depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Her parents claim that the trauma she experienced from the rape and the inadequate response from law enforcement and the university ultimately led to her decision to take her own life. The lawsuit seeks an unspecified amount of damages and aims to bring attention to the need for improved handling of sexual assault cases by law enforcement and universities. The University of Alabama has since announced plans to establish two sexual assault programs to care for victims, and its staff is undergoing training in the SANE (Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner) course curriculum.

reference: – Former University of Alabama student Megan Rondini would still be alive if not for the "mishandling" of her alleged rape, her parents say. In a wrongful death lawsuit filed Sunday, Rondini's parents accuse the university, school officials, sheriff's deputies, and Rondini's alleged rapist of enabling her suicide in February 2016. Rondini had been a 20-year-old student at the University of Alabama in July 2015 when she alleged she was raped by local businessman TJ Bunn Jr. But authorities "wrongfully acted or failed to act in response," the lawsuit alleges, per Buzzfeed. It adds deputies—who found the case didn't meet Alabama's legal definition for rape—didn't test Rondini's rape kit or interview witnesses, an "intentional" behavior that was due to Rondini's gender. Rondini—who allegedly stole $3 and a gun from Bunn—was "treated as a crime suspect," not a victim, the suit continues, per the AP, also alleging a school counselor refused to see Rondini unless she took anxiety medication. Rondini later dropped out of school, moved back to Texas, and suffered "extreme depression, anxiety, PTSD, fear, panic attacks, decline of cognitive functions and general well-being … all of which directly led to Megan's loss of life," the suit states. Per AL.com, two days before her suicide, she sent a text reading, "I wonder what I could've accomplished if one man didn't completely rip everything away from me." Bunn, who claimed the sex was consensual, was never charged with a crime. His lawyer tells AL.com that the lawsuit is "baseless" and "simply false."

final_summary:
A mass shooting occurred at a Waffle House restaurant in Nashville, Tennessee, on Sunday, April 22, 2018, resulting in the deaths of four people and injuring four others. The suspect, Travis Reinking, 29, allegedly opened fire in the parking lot and then stormed the restaurant, targeting patrons and employees. According to reports, Reinking was nearly naked, wearing only a green jacket and brandishing an assault-style rifle during the attack. A quick-thinking customer, James Shaw, wrestled the gun away from Reinking, preventing further loss of life.

An intensive manhunt ensued, with authorities describing Reinking as potentially armed and possibly hiding in a wooded area near an apartment complex where he resided. Police warned residents of the surrounding neighborhood to exercise caution, advising them to check on their neighbors and report any suspicious activity. Schools in the area were placed on lockdown, with students allowed to move freely within the buildings but denied entry to external visitors.

As the investigation unfolded, disturbing reports emerged about Reinking's troubled past, including incidents in his hometown of Morton, Illinois. In May 2016, Reinking claimed that pop star Taylor Swift was stalking him and hacking his phone, prompting a visit from Tazewell County deputies. In July 2017, Reinking was arrested by the U.S. Secret Service after attempting to breach a restricted area near the White House, stating he wanted to meet President Donald Trump. Following the arrest, Reinking's state firearms card was revoked, and four guns, including an AR-15, were confiscated. His father, Jeffrey Reinking, took possession of the guns under the condition that he would keep them secure and out of his son's reach.

It remains unclear whether Reinking regained access to the firearms. Nashville Police Chief Steve Anderson expressed concerns that Reinking may have mental health issues, citing previous encounters between law enforcement and the suspect. The victims of the Waffle House shooting included Taurean Sanderlin, 29, a restaurant employee; Joe Perez, 20, a patron; DeEbony Groves, 21, a Belmont University student; and Akilah Dasilva, 23, a music video producer and student at Middle Tennessee State University. Four others sustained injuries, including James Shaw, who disarmed Reinking, and his girlfriend, Tia Waggoner, who underwent surgery for her wounds.

The FBI, along with local law enforcement agencies, including the Tennessee Bureau of Investigation, joined the investigation, which spanned across multiple jurisdictions, including Tazewell County, Illinois, where Reinking's past behavior raised red flags. Sheriff Robert Huston in Tazewell County, Illinois, stated that deputies allowed Reinking's father to take possession of the guns on the promise that he would "keep the weapons secure and out of the possession of Travis." As the search for Reinking continued, authorities emphasized the importance of community vigilance, urging residents to report any sightings or information that might lead to the suspect's capture. Associated Press writers John Raby in Charleston, West Virginia; Ed White in Detroit; and Justin Pritchard in Los Angeles contributed to this report.

The 29-year-old, accused of killing four people in a rampage early Sunday, was last seen later in the day in a wooded area near his apartment complex, wearing only pants, say police, who have gone door-to-door in the area and warned residents to be on alert. According to police reports, relatives in Reinking's Illinois hometown say that since 2014, he has been having delusions that Taylor Swift is stalking him and dozens of unknown culprits are hacking into his phone and computer, the New York Daily News reports. According to a July 2016 police report from Peoria, Reinking told officers he had confronted the singer in a Dairy Queen parking lot. In his version of events, "Taylor climbed up the side of a building and Travis followed. However, when he reached the rooftop, Taylor was gone." In other incidents the next year, after he moved to Nashville, Reinking allegedly threatened a man with a semi-automatic rifle and barged into a community pool and jumped in wearing a pink housecoat. Police say Reinking's weapons, including the AR-15 used to kill four people at the Waffle House, were seized after he was arrested outside the White House last year, the Tennessean reports. The weapons were given to Reinking's father after he promised to keep them away from his son. Police believe the father later gave the four firearms back to his son. (A Waffle House customer managed to wrest the gun away from Reinking.)

final_summary:
According to The Associated Press, as Dzhokhar Tsarnaev prepares to be formally sentenced to death for his role in the 2013 Boston Marathon bombings, one question lingers: will he break his silence? Hours ahead of the sentencing hearing, scheduled for Wednesday, defense lawyers have remained tight-lipped about whether Tsarnaev, now 21, plans to address the court before the death sentence is officially imposed.

During the trial, the public only caught glimpses of Tsarnaev through indirect means, such as through Sister Helen Prejean, a death penalty opponent who met with him five times and reported that he expressed genuine remorse for the carnage that left three dead and over 200 injured. However, prosecutors countered with video footage showing Tsarnaev giving the middle finger to a security camera in a holding cell just months after the bombing.

Legal experts believe that speaking up might not serve Tsarnaev well, citing the precedent set by Timothy McVeigh, who was sentenced to death and later executed for the Oklahoma City bombing. Stephen Jones, who represented McVeigh, advises against making a statement unless it sounds authentic and natural, warning that anything else might come across as insincere. McVeigh himself chose to speak up during his sentencing hearing, quoting a dissenting opinion from Justice Louis Brandeis in the 1928 Supreme Court case Olmstead v. United States, stating, "Our Government is the potent, the omnipresent teacher. For good or for ill, it teaches the whole people by its example."

Other convicted terrorists have used their time to deliver diatribes, such as Zacarias Moussaui, who cursed America and praised Osama bin Laden during his sentencing for his role in the 9/11 attacks, and Abu Hamza, who proclaimed his innocence and complained about prison conditions. However, these examples highlight the risks of speaking up, as it can be perceived as too little, too late.

David Hoose, a Massachusetts defense lawyer, believes that Tsarnaev should keep quiet, arguing that whatever he says will be seen as insufficient given the gravity of the crimes. On the other hand, New York Law School professor Robert Blecker suggests that expressing remorse could potentially aid Tsarnaev in his future appeals, particularly if he seeks a commutation of his death sentence from the president.

More than 20 victims of the Boston Marathon bombings, including family members of those who died and individuals who suffered injuries, are expected to testify during the sentencing hearing. Celeste Corcoran, a survivor of the attack, has stated that she and her daughter will not attend the hearing, deeming it unnecessary and emotionally taxing. As the nation awaits Tsarnaev's decision, many wonder whether he will choose to speak up or remain silent, a choice that could have significant implications for his future.

reference: – A nun says Dzhokhar Tsarnaev expressed remorse, while prosecutors played video of him giving the finger to a security camera. But today, secondhand narratives and hand gestures may give way to words straight out of the Boston Marathon bomber's mouth during his sentencing, NBC News reports. Although defense lawyers are mum, the will-he-or-won't-he question has set off a debate over whether it's a smart idea for him to vocalize, say, his motives or apologies to victims' families, as the Los Angeles Times suggests. The lawyer who defended Timothy McVeigh, for one, thinks Tsarnaev shouldn't talk. "I would be very surprised if he speaks," he tells NBC. "Generally, I don't advise clients to make a statement unless they are articulate and it ... doesn't sound like it was written by lawyers." Adds another defense attorney, "I don't think there's anything to be gained by it at this point," he says. "Whatever would be said … would be viewed as too little, too late." But a law school prof tells CBS that Tsarnaev could take a long view: While an apology won't affect his sentence, it could give him leverage if he seeks to have his death sentence commuted by the president. Conversely, he could use his sentencing as a platform, like Zacarias Moussaoui, who issued taunts at his 2006 sentencing for conspiring on the 9/11 attacks: "God curse America. And God save Osama bin Laden. You will never get him." Who will likely be speaking today: bombing victims and their families, who will have the chance to give impact statements, the Times notes. (Tsarnaev didn't testify at his trial, but he did cry during one person's testimony.)

final_summary:
The Rich Earth Institute, a pioneering organization based in Brattleboro, Vermont, has been at the forefront of a groundbreaking initiative to harness the potential of human urine as a natural fertilizer. Founded by Kim Nace and her partner Abe Noe-Hays, the institute has been collecting and processing urine from volunteers since 2012, with the goal of replacing chemical fertilizers in agriculture. Initially, the team collected 600 gallons of urine from friends and neighbors, followed by approximately 3,000 gallons from 170 human volunteers in 2013. Their ambitious aim is to double the amount to around 6,000 gallons this year, equivalent to filling a third of an average American swimming pool.

The concept of using urine as a fertilizer is not new, having been explored in various parts of the world. In fact, studies have shown that urine contains essential plant nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, which are typically extracted from the earth or air through mining and industrial processes. By collecting and processing urine, Rich Earth seeks to divert these valuable resources from waterways and redirect them to farms, thereby reducing the environmental impact of traditional fertilizers.

One of the earliest adopters of this innovative approach was Jay Bailey, owner of Fair Wind Farms, who participated in the initial trial in 2012. He applied a urine solution to his hay fields using a horse-drawn applicator and observed a significant increase in productivity, with urine-treated fields yielding twice as much as those without fertilizers. Encouraged by the results, the USDA awarded Rich Earth a $10,000 grant to expand the trials at Fair Wind in 2013.

The benefits of using urine as a fertilizer extend beyond its environmental advantages. According to a study conducted by the Stockholm Water Company in the late 1990s, one Northern European adult produces enough plant nutrients in their urine to meet the food requirements for another person. Similar experiments have been conducted in China and Mexico, demonstrating the efficacy of urine-based fertilizers. In fact, researchers at Tribhuvan University in Kathmandu, Nepal, have been studying the use of human urine as a fertilizer for decades, with promising results.

However, concerns surrounding the use of human urine as a fertilizer persist. One major issue is the presence of pharmaceutical residues in urine, which can pose health risks if not properly addressed. To mitigate this problem, Rich Earth has developed strategies for sanitizing urine, including solar pasteurization and long-term storage in a warm greenhouse. Additionally, the organization is collaborating with the University of Michigan and SUNY Buffalo to investigate the extent of pharmaceutical contamination in urine.

Another challenge lies in changing public perception and cultural attitudes towards using urine as a fertilizer. While some communities, like those in Nepal, have been utilizing human urine for centuries, others may be hesitant to adopt this practice due to cultural or social stigma. Moreover, the effectiveness of urine-based fertilizers relies on proper combination with compost, as urine alone lacks sufficient nutrients for sustained plant growth.

Despite these challenges, the Rich Earth Institute remains committed to exploring the potential of human urine as a sustainable alternative to chemical fertilizers. With ongoing research and development, this innovative approach may soon become a viable solution for environmentally conscious farmers worldwide.

reference: – Nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium are among plants' key needs—and your urine contains all of them. That's why Vermont's Rich Earth Institute is studying the use of human urine as fertilizer, Modern Farmer reports. There's already plenty of evidence that it works. Yahoo cites one study, earlier reported by National Geographic, in which a researcher found that a blend of urine and compost resulted in taller sweet pepper plants with more peppers. A Swedish study, meanwhile, sent urine from housing projects to a farm. Researchers found that one adult's pee provides enough plant nutrients for 50% to 100% of another person's diet, Modern Farmer notes. A farmer working with the Rich Earth Institute also found urine to be highly effective in a 2012 field test, results that helped the group get a $10,000 USDA grant. Last year, the institute collected some 3,000 gallons of pee from 170 people. "When people realize that they produce something every day that can be helpful to the environment and the earth, it’s a very wonderful feeling," says its administrative director. But hurdles remain: For instance, human urine can contain remnants of medications.

final_summary:
A recent study published in The Lancet Psychology has provided new insights into the phenomenon of hearing voices, challenging the conventional wisdom that it is solely a symptom of mental illness. Led by Durham University researcher Angela Woods, the study analyzed data from 153 individuals who reported hearing voices, revealing a diverse array of experiences that contradict the common stereotypes often depicted in media. Contrary to popular portrayals, many participants described their voices as internal characters with distinct personalities, engaging in conversations rather than simply issuing commands. 

In fact, as many as 15 percent of those surveyed had not been diagnosed with any psychological disorder, indicating that hearing voices may be more widespread and varied than previously assumed. According to Woods, the study underscores the importance of adopting a multifaceted approach to mental health research, recognizing the intricacies of human experiences that cannot be reduced to simplistic diagnoses. This perspective shift is essential for developing a more comprehensive understanding of the complexities surrounding hearing voices.

Illustrations by John T Takai accompany the article, highlighting the nuances of this phenomenon. Meanwhile, another study published in Frontiers in Psychology delves into the role of dreams in shaping religious beliefs and conceptions of the divine. Neurologists Patrick Mcnamara and Kelly Bulkeley argue that humans possess an innate inclination to attribute agency and intelligence to enigmatic events, including those experienced in dreams. They propose that dreams offer a unique environment where people can interact with supernatural entities, leveraging the brain's capacity to generate "mental stimulations of alternate realities" and attribute mental states to others.

This concept is exemplified in the biblical account of Jacob, who reportedly received a theophany while resting in the desert of Canaan. However, modern cognitive scientists would interpret this occurrence as a product of Jacob's brain, rather than a direct communication from God. Mcnamara and Bulkeley suggest that humans are genetically predisposed to create god concepts during dreaming, drawing parallels between the neural mechanisms governing sleep and the attribution of agency to supernatural entities.

As reported by Ross Pomeroy, this study sheds light on the intricate relationships between the human brain, perception, and spirituality. Interestingly, the biblical figure of Jacob's ancestors, Abraham and Isaac, also feature prominently in the narrative of the Old Testament. By examining the complexities of hearing voices and the role of dreams in shaping religious beliefs, researchers aim to gain a deeper understanding of the human experience and its multifaceted nature.

history.draft:
A recent study published in The Lancet Psychology has provided new insights into the phenomenon of hearing voices, challenging the conventional wisdom that it is solely a symptom of mental illness. Led by Durham University researcher Angela Woods, the study analyzed data from 153 individuals who reported hearing voices, revealing a diverse array of experiences that contradict the common stereotypes often depicted in media. Contrary to popular portrayals, many participants described their voices as internal characters with distinct personalities, engaging in conversations rather than simply issuing commands. 

In fact, as many as 15 percent of those surveyed had not been diagnosed with any psychological disorder, indicating that hearing voices may be more widespread and varied than previously assumed. According to Woods, the study underscores the importance of adopting a multifaceted approach to mental health research, recognizing the intricacies of human experiences that cannot be reduced to simplistic diagnoses. This perspective shift is essential for developing a more comprehensive understanding of the complexities surrounding hearing voices.

Illustrations by John T Takai accompany the article, highlighting the nuances of this phenomenon. Meanwhile, another study published in Frontiers in Psychology delves into the role of dreams in shaping religious beliefs and conceptions of the divine. Neurologists Patrick Mcnamara and Kelly Bulkeley argue that humans possess an innate inclination to attribute agency and intelligence to enigmatic events, including those experienced in dreams. They propose that dreams offer a unique environment where people can interact with supernatural entities, leveraging the brain's capacity to generate "mental stimulations of alternate realities" and attribute mental states to others.

This concept is exemplified in the biblical account of Jacob, who reportedly received a theophany while resting in the desert of Canaan. However, modern cognitive scientists would interpret this occurrence as a product of Jacob's brain, rather than a direct communication from God. Mcnamara and Bulkeley suggest that humans are genetically predisposed to create god concepts during dreaming, drawing parallels between the neural mechanisms governing sleep and the attribution of agency to supernatural entities.

As reported by Ross Pomeroy, this study sheds light on the intricate relationships between the human brain, perception, and spirituality. Interestingly, the biblical figure of Jacob's ancestors, Abraham and Isaac, also feature prominently in the narrative of the Old Testament. By examining the complexities of hearing voices and the role of dreams in shaping religious beliefs, researchers aim to gain a deeper understanding of the human experience and its multifaceted nature.

reference: – Religions throughout the world—and throughout history—have put dreams at the center of their belief systems, and researchers say there's a good reason for that. We do much of our intense dreaming during REM sleep, when the highly active brain acts almost like it's awake, writes Ross Pomeroy at Real Clear Science. All that activity can lead to dreamed situations that feel very real—in some cases, even religious. In a new paper in Frontiers in Psychology, a neurologist and theological scholar argue that dreams are well-suited to create experiences in which we believe we've spoken to a deity of some kind. That is in part due to the feeling of an alternative reality we get in dreams, the researchers say. What's more, in dreams, we often ascribe emotions and thoughts to those we believe we're encountering. We give them an "exaggerated degree of agency" because our dreaming brains have trouble understanding our thoughts as our own, the researchers write. The researchers also note that unclear boundaries between REM sleep and wakefulness can be linked to schizophrenia—but at Gizmodo, Annalee Newitz notes that the feeling of "hearing voices" may not necessarily mean a person is dealing with mental illness. A recent survey found that 15% of respondents who say they've heard voices have no diagnosis of disorder, and the "hearing" involved isn't necessarily auditory; the voices may not feel as though they're external at all, researchers say. (Researchers last year described schizophrenia as not one, but eight different illnesses.)

final_summary:
A devastating plane crash occurred on November 28, 2016, near Medellin, Colombia, resulting in the deaths of 71 people, including most of the Brazilian soccer team Chapecoense, which was headed to the Copa Sudamericana final. The chartered plane, operated by LaMia Airlines, was carrying 72 passengers and nine crew members when it crashed into a mountainside. Among the survivors were six individuals, including three members of the Chapecoense soccer team: goalkeeper Jackson Follmann, who underwent amputation of his right leg; defender Helio Neto, who suffered severe trauma to his skull, thorax, and lungs; and fellow defender Alan Ruschel, who required spine surgery.

According to reports from the scene, the pilot of the plane radioed the control tower at Medellin's airport, stating that he was experiencing electrical difficulties and low fuel levels. Co-pilot Juan Sebastian Upegui, who was flying a nearby plane, recalled hearing the pilot's distress calls, including a "Mayday" alert, before the radio transmission ceased. The cause of the crash remains under investigation, with teams from Brazil, Colombia, Bolivia, and the UK contributing to the inquiry.

The victims included 21 players, eight members of the coaching staff, 20 journalists, and 22 other passengers, mostly from Chapecoense. The team, from the small city of Chapeco in southern Brazil, had risen from the fourth division to the top tier since 2009 and was set to compete in the Copa Sudamericana final against Atletico Nacional of Medellin. The team's vice-president, Ivan Tozzo, described the tragedy as a "celebration turned into a nightmare."

In response to the disaster, Brazil declared three days of mourning, and global soccer stars such as Lionel Messi and Pele offered condolences. The city of Chapeco, where the team was based in the state of Santa Catarina, was plunged into grief, with schools closing, businesses shutting down, and residents displaying black and green ribbons in tribute to the fallen players. The team's stadium, Conda, was transformed into a makeshift memorial, with fans gathering to pay their respects.

Argentina's San Lorenzo, which Chapecoense defeated in the semifinals, expressed its condolences to the team and its supporters. The Associated Press reported on the tragedy, and Reuters provided updates from the scene. The Colombian team, Atletico Nacional, announced that they would donate the Copa Sudamericana trophy to Chapecoense in honor of the deceased players. The club's governor, Raimundo Colombo, expressed his deep regret and solidarity with the families of the victims, describing the team as "making history" by reaching the final of an international competition. As the investigation continues, the focus remains on supporting the survivors and the families of those who lost their lives in the tragic accident.

reference: – A chartered plane with a Brazilian first division soccer team on board crashed in a mountainous area near Medellin while on its way to the finals of a regional tournament, killing 76 people, Colombian officials say. Five people survived, including three players, reports Reuters. Aviation authorities say the British Aerospace 146 short-haul plane, operated by a charter airline named LaMia, declared an emergency at 10pm because of an electrical failure, the AP reports. The aircraft, which had departed from Santa Cruz, Bolivia, was transporting the Chapecoense soccer team to Medellin's Jose Maria Cordova International Airport. The team started the journey in Sao Paulo. A Reuters photographer described the tail section as having been severed from the plane and totally destroyed. The team, from the small city of Chapeco, was in the middle of a fairy-tale season. It joined Brazil's first division in 2014 for the first time since the 1970s and made it last week to the Copa Sudamericana finals after defeating two of Argentina's fiercest squads, San Lorenzo and Independiente, as well as Colombia's Junior. "What was supposed to be a celebration has turned into a tragedy," Medellin Mayor Federico Gutierrez said from the search-and-rescue command center. "May God accompany our athletes, officials, journalists, and other guests traveling with our delegation," the club said in a brief statement on its Facebook page. The Guardian reports Brazilian President Michel Temer has declared three days of mourning.

final_summary:
On a fateful night, Officer Lameen Barnes documented an incident involving Mr. Dixon in an official report, stating that he attempted to throw an unknown liquid substance at officers, prompting a search for contraband in his cell. However, eyewitness accounts from three individuals contradict this account, revealing a disturbing sequence of events. According to these witnesses, including two who wished to remain anonymous due to fear of retaliation from officers and their employer, Corizon, which provides healthcare services to the city's jails, Mr. Dixon was wheeled into an examination area without surveillance footage, where he frantically yelled for help, fearing for his life. Approximately six correction officers surrounded him, with one guard repeatedly punching him in the head.

This incident occurred amidst a broader crisis within New York City's jail system, where Commissioner Joseph Ponte, who took office in March, implemented a policy to confine up to 47 mentally ill inmates in 23-hour isolation without prior approval from mental health professionals. This decision was made in an effort to reduce violence, particularly among inmates who owed time in solitary confinement. However, the New York City Board of Correction disputed this approach, citing concerns over the health risks posed to vulnerable inmates, especially those taking psychotropic medications, who were transferred from climate-controlled observation units to sweltering solitary cells.

The board noted that nearly 40 percent of the approximately 11,500 daily inmates in city jails have a diagnosed mental health issue, with about a third suffering from severe conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. In light of this, the transfer of inmates like Mr. Dixon raises questions about the treatment and care of mentally ill detainees. Another inmate, Mr. Lane, who had also splashed guards with a liquid, corroborated the accounts of abuse, recalling being punched repeatedly by correction officers.

Commissioner Ponte defended his actions, stating that the measure was necessary to address the surge in violence on Rikers Island, where 21 slashings occurred within a six-week period and a total of 87 incidents were reported in the previous fiscal year. The Associated Press reported that this number represents a significant increase from the 68 total incidents in the previous fiscal year and 55 in fiscal 2012. Despite this, the board emphasized the need for adherence to established protocols to ensure the safety and well-being of all inmates, particularly those with mental health needs. As part of his efforts to address the crisis, Commissioner Ponte revealed that there are approximately 1,000 inmates who owe time in solitary confinement but are currently in general population due to lack of space. These inmates are considered high-risk for harming themselves and others, and separating them is seen as a way to stem the violence on Rikers Island.

A federal judge has ordered that inmates taking psychotropic medications should not be placed in uncooled cells, as exposure to heat can be fatal. Additionally, some inmates were denied medication and experienced seizures after being transferred to solitary confinement. One inmate reportedly suffered a seizure. Commissioner Ponte acknowledged that the situation is dire, saying, "What we don't have, and have not had for probably more than five years now, is a plan that's impacted the level of violence on Rikers Island."
anchors: The New York City Board Of Correction

reference: – Guards at Rikers Island don't just neglect the mentally ill or let them bake to death, they also routinely brutally beat them, often without suffering any repercussions, a New York Times investigation has discovered. The Times uncovered a secret report compiled by a city department this year that found that 129 Rikers inmates suffered "serious injuries" in altercations with staff members over an 11-month span last year; 77% of those inmates had a mental health diagnosis. In most cases, the inmates were beaten while handcuffed. The Times investigated the incidents individually, and offer some disturbing details: When guards found Jose Bautista trying to hang himself, they saved him, but then allegedly threw him to the ground and punched him with such force that they perforated his bowel. It was one of five beatings noted in the report that followed a suicide attempt. Bautista needed emergency surgery, but guards took nine hours to drive him to the hospital—which is only 15 minutes away. When Andre Lane threw water or urine at guards, they allegedly handcuffed him to a gurney, wheeled him to a clinic that wasn't monitored by security cameras, and beat him so violently that the walls and cabinets were covered with his blood. After Brian Mack complained that guards were stealing inmates' food, a guard captain allegedly hit him in the eye with his radio, while another officer punched him in the jaw. He came away with a broken eye socket and jaw. Correction officials said he'd been in a fight with another inmate, but investigators noted no such fight was logged in prison records. None of the guards involved in any of the cases have faced criminal charges—or even administrative ones. Rikers has been under pressure to reform its treatment of mentally ill inmates—in January, for example, it announced it would stop putting the "seriously mentally ill" in solitary, though the Times notes that classification applies to a small segment of the population. Last week an oversight board said the newly-appointed city jail commissioner had in June illegally sent as many as 47 mentally ill Rikers inmates to solitary without consulting clinicians, the AP reports.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, a new study by the U.S. Department of Education revealed a disturbing trend - the number of sex crimes reported on U.S. college campuses soared by 50% over the course of a decade. According to the report, which looked at assault data up to 2011, 3,300 forcible sex offenses were reported on campuses across the U.S., up from 2,200 a decade earlier. This increase may not necessarily mean that more assaults are occurring, but rather that more students are feeling empowered to come forward and report incidents.

Experts attribute this rise in reports to increased awareness and education on college campuses about what constitutes sexual assault, as well as a growing willingness among students to speak out. A separate study by the U.S. Department of Education found that the number of reported forcible sex crimes on campus increased by 52% between 2001 and 2011, with 2.2 reports per 10,000 full-time students in 2011. This trend is echoed in other studies, including an NPR analysis that found a 49% increase in "forcible rapes" reported at four-year colleges between 2008 and 2012.

Despite the challenges, some institutions are making strides in addressing the issue. The University of Michigan, for instance, has implemented various initiatives to prevent sexual assault, including bystander education programs and training sessions for first-year students on recognizing and responding to potential threats. However, the university has faced criticism for its handling of sexual misconduct allegations, including a high-profile case involving former football player Brendan Gibbons, who was eventually expelled after a lengthy investigation.

Student activists have demanded greater transparency and accountability from the administration, leading to changes in policies and procedures. The White House has also responded to the issue by releasing guidelines urging universities to take proactive measures to prevent sexual assault and create safe environments for victims to come forward. With the implementation of the Campus SaVE Act, all schools nationwide will be required to adhere to stricter standards for assault prevention education and investigations.

The prevalence of sexual assault remains a significant concern, with the Rape Abuse & Incest National Network estimating that 60% of incidents go unreported. A National Institute of Justice study found that 14% of college women reported experiencing unwanted sexual contact while incapacitated, highlighting the importance of education and awareness in preventing such incidents. Photographer Erin captured images of students protesting the University of Michigan's handling of the Gibbons case, while photographer Joe Shapiro documented the university's efforts to address the issue through training programs for first-year students.

history.draft:
According to a recent analysis by NPR's Investigative Unit, the number of "forcible rapes" reported at four-year colleges increased by 49% between 2008 and 2012, reaching a total of 3,300 reported cases in 2011, as stated in a U.S. Department of Education report. While this rise in reports indicates a persistent and ugly problem of sexual assault on college campuses, experts view it as a positive trend, suggesting that more students are feeling empowered to come forward and report the underreported crime. 

Daniel Carter, director of the 32 National Campus Safety Initiative, attributes the increase in reports to improved support systems and resources available to victims, allowing them to seek help and heal. He emphasizes that schools still have a long way to go in addressing the issue, but acknowledges progress made by institutions like the University of Michigan, which has implemented various initiatives to prevent sexual assault, including bystander education programs and training sessions for first-year students on recognizing and responding to potential threats.

However, the University of Michigan has faced criticism for its handling of sexual misconduct allegations, including a high-profile case involving former football player Brendan Gibbons, who was eventually expelled after a lengthy investigation. Student activists have demanded greater transparency and accountability from the administration, leading to changes in policies and procedures.

A separate study by the U.S. Department of Education found that the number of reported forcible sex crimes on campus increased by 52% between 2001 and 2011, with 2.2 reports per 10,000 full-time students in 2011. Experts suggest that this rise may be due to increased awareness and education on college campuses about what constitutes sexual assault, as well as a growing willingness among students to report incidents.

The White House has responded to the issue by releasing guidelines urging universities to take proactive measures to prevent sexual assault and create safe environments for victims to come forward. With the implementation of the Campus SaVE Act, all schools nationwide will be required to adhere to stricter standards for assault prevention education and investigations by fall 2015.

Joe Shapiro, an NPR reporter, notes that while the reported increase in sexual assaults may seem alarming, it could actually indicate a shift towards greater openness and trust between students and authorities. According to a National Institute of Justice study, 14% of college women reported experiencing unwanted sexual contact while incapacitated, highlighting the importance of education and awareness in preventing such incidents.

history.draft:
On Tuesday, a new study by the U.S. Department of Education revealed a disturbing trend - the number of sex crimes reported on U.S. college campuses soared by 50% over the course of a decade. According to the report, which looked at assault data up to 2011, 3,300 forcible sex offenses were reported on campuses across the U.S., up from 2,200 a decade earlier. This increase may not necessarily mean that more assaults are occurring, but rather that more students are feeling empowered to come forward and report incidents.

Experts attribute this rise in reports to increased awareness and education on college campuses about what constitutes sexual assault, as well as a growing willingness among students to speak out. A separate study by the U.S. Department of Education found that the number of reported forcible sex crimes on campus increased by 52% between 2001 and 2011, with 2.2 reports per 10,000 full-time students in 2011. This trend is echoed in other studies, including an NPR analysis that found a 49% increase in "forcible rapes" reported at four-year colleges between 2008 and 2012.

Despite the challenges, some institutions are making strides in addressing the issue. The University of Michigan, for instance, has implemented various initiatives to prevent sexual assault, including bystander education programs and training sessions for first-year students on recognizing and responding to potential threats. However, the university has faced criticism for its handling of sexual misconduct allegations, including a high-profile case involving former football player Brendan Gibbons, who was eventually expelled after a lengthy investigation.

Student activists have demanded greater transparency and accountability from the administration, leading to changes in policies and procedures. The White House has also responded to the issue by releasing guidelines urging universities to take proactive measures to prevent sexual assault and create safe environments for victims to come forward. With the implementation of the Campus SaVE Act, all schools nationwide will be required to adhere to stricter standards for assault prevention education and investigations.

The prevalence of sexual assault remains a significant concern, with the Rape Abuse & Incest National Network estimating that 60% of incidents go unreported. A National Institute of Justice study found that 14% of college women reported experiencing unwanted sexual contact while incapacitated, highlighting the importance of education and awareness in preventing such incidents. Photographer Erin captured images of students protesting the University of Michigan's handling of the Gibbons case, while photographer Joe Shapiro documented the university's efforts to address the issue through training programs for first-year students.

reference: – There might be a reason that college sexual assaults make the news more often these days. A new Department of Education report has found that reports of "forcible" campus sexual assaults shot up 50% between 2001 and 2011, jumping from 2,200 to 3,300, Time reports. At the same time, every other category of college crime decreased. The numbers mean that 2.2 out of every 10,000 college students in 2011 reported an assault, Business Insider points out. Still, the spike doesn't necessarily mean that more assaults are occurring; it might just mean that victims have become more comfortable coming forward. "It's a good thing that more victims are reporting," one advocate told NPR in response to a similar finding in April. "For far too long, they've been left on their own. And now they're getting the help they need."

final_summary:
Oklahoma Governor Mary Fallin signed a bill into law on Monday that bars cities across the state from setting their own minimum wage, vacation, or sick-day requirements. The legislation, passed by the state's Republican-led legislature, comes as a response to an ongoing grassroots effort in Oklahoma City to raise the city's minimum wage to $10.10 per hour, a rate supported by President Barack Obama on the national level. Critics argue that the bill specifically targets Oklahoma City, where organizers have been collecting signatures on a petition to put the issue to a citywide vote. 

David Slane, the lawyer behind the petition, expressed disappointment that the state's decision to pass the bill undermines the right of citizens to decide on minimum wage. With a goal of collecting 4,000 signatures by the end of April, the organizers aim to reach the required 80,000 signatures to bring the issue to a statewide vote. 

Governor Fallin's office has defended the bill, stating that raising the minimum wage is not an effective way to combat poverty. However, this assertion contradicts findings from the Economic Policy Institute, which reports that nearly 90 percent of workers who would benefit from a minimum wage increase are over 20 years old, with an average age of 35, and more than a quarter have children to support. Furthermore, over half of these workers are employed full-time. 

Supporters of the ban claim that it will protect local businesses and maintain economic competitiveness. State Representative Randy Grau argued that an artificially raised minimum wage could harm local economies within months. Senator Dan Newberry echoed similar sentiments, suggesting that the bill creates a safer business environment that allows Oklahoma to remain economically competitive and foster job growth. 

This week, the bill's passage has sparked concerns about the impact on low-income workers, with some lawmakers labeling the current minimum wage of $7.25 per hour as "immoral" due to its inability to lift individuals above the poverty line despite working full-time. In contrast, Governor Fallin pointed to a Congressional Budget Office report indicating that a nationwide minimum wage hike could result in the loss of approximately half a million jobs. The governor also claimed that most minimum wage workers are young, single individuals working part-time or entry-level jobs, often living with their parents in middle-class families. However, this characterization has been disputed by experts, who argue that it perpetuates a common myth about low-wage workers.

Mary Fallin, who signed the bill into law, rejected the argument that allowing cities to raise the minimum wage would bring people out of poverty, saying having different minimum wages across the state could scare off businesses and a higher minimum wage would cost jobs. "Most minimum wage workers are young, single people working part-time or entry-level jobs," she said. "Many are high school or college students living with their parents in middle-class families." Numerous studies, however, have found this to be untrue, Al Jazeera notes. A scandal surrounding US Representative Anthony Weiner erupted when a young woman leaked screenshots of her post-resignation sexting conversations with him on the gossip website TheDirty.com. Initially, the woman wished to remain anonymous, but due to the nature of the internet, her identity was quickly revealed by BuzzFeed, which obtained images from her Facebook and Formspring profiles that matched the blurred shots posted on TheDirty.com. The woman, later identified as Sydney Elaine Leathers, was found to be an Indiana resident in her early twenties who worked as a field organizer for Obama for America and listed Weiner as one of her top heroes. On social media platforms, Leathers expressed her support for Weiner, stating that he "did nothing wrong" and could continue sending explicit photos "every single day" if he continued to serve as a legislator.

According to a high school friend, Leathers engaged in raunchy online conversations with Weiner last summer, during which he allegedly promised to relocate her to either Chicago or New York. This friend claimed that Leathers believed herself to be the most important person in the relationship, even surpassing Huma Abedin, Weiner's wife. Abedin initially defended her husband at a press conference, but in a subsequent statement, she explained her decision to stay with Weiner, describing him as a "smart, caring, and dedicated person."

The source, who says he or she has known Leathers since high school, says she "felt she was the important one" to Weiner, even more important than wife Huma Abedin. As for Abedin, Daily Intel notes that she came to a "prescient conclusion" in September's Harper's Bazaar: "People have said many things about my husband—some nice, some not so nice. And that will surely continue. Launching this campaign was not an easy decision for our family to make. Putting yourself out there comes with a cost." (Meanwhile, "cubicle guy" has become a viral sensation after Weiner's press conference last night.)

final_summary:
On Friday, President Donald Trump made a sudden and dramatic change in leadership within his administration, announcing that he had replaced his chief of staff, Reince Priebus, with Homeland Security Secretary John Kelly. According to Priebus, he had submitted his resignation to the President privately on Thursday, but the news was only confirmed publicly by Trump via a tweet shortly after he landed on Air Force One at Joint Base Andrews outside Washington. 

Priebus, who had held the position since July 2017, described the move as a "reset button" that the President had a right to hit, and expressed his continued loyalty to Trump, stating that he would still be a "Trump fan" and work towards achieving the President's goals. However, sources close to Priebus initially claimed that he had not resigned, sparking confusion and speculation about the circumstances surrounding his departure.

The move marked the end of a tumultuous six-month tenure for Priebus, who had faced intense scrutiny for his inability to stem the flow of leaks and impose order within the chaotic White House. Despite being brought on board by Trump due to his Washington connections, Priebus bore significant blame for the administration's legislative failures, including the collapse of efforts to pass a healthcare replacement bill.

Rumors of internal conflicts within the White House had been simmering for months, culminating in a public feud between Priebus and newly appointed Communications Director Anthony Scaramucci, who had launched a scathing attack on Priebus in a recent interview, calling him a "paranoid schizophrenic." CNN's Wolf Blitzer interviewed Priebus on "The Situation Room" Friday evening, where he declined to respond to the comments.

In contrast, Trump praised Kelly, describing him as a "great leader" and highlighting his impressive background as a nearly five-decade veteran of the US military, including stints as chief of Southern Command and senior assistant to the Secretary of Defense. Kelly will take over as chief of staff on Monday, participating in a meeting of Trump's cabinet, and will oversee a transition period.

The shake-up marks the shortest tenure of a White House chief of staff in modern history, with Priebus lasting just 189 days in the role. His departure follows a series of high-profile exits from the administration, including the departures of Press Secretary Sean Spicer and Deputy Chief of Staff Katie Walsh, both of whom were close allies of Priebus.

As news of Priebus' departure broke, Trump traveled to Long Island for an event on gang violence, where he was accompanied by Representative Peter King. CNN reported that there was reportedly no indication of the impending shake-up during the trip, with Priebus maintaining a "poker face" aboard Air Force One. Trump's family, including daughter Ivanka Trump, had reportedly lost confidence in Priebus, with one source claiming they urged the President to make a change.

Meanwhile, White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon is expected to remain in his position, despite rumors of his potential departure. The sudden shift in leadership comes as the White House continues to grapple with controversy and infighting, with Trump facing mounting pressure to address pressing policy issues and restore stability within his administration.

However, sources close to Priebus initially claimed that he had not resigned, sparking confusion and speculation about the circumstances surrounding his departure.

The move marked the end of a tumultuous six-month tenure for Priebus, who had faced intense scrutiny for his inability to stem the flow of leaks and impose order within the chaotic White House. Despite being brought on board by Trump due to his Washington connections, Priebus bore significant blame for the administration's legislative failures, including the collapse of efforts to pass a healthcare replacement bill.

Rumors of internal conflicts within the White House had been simmering for months, culminating in a public feud between Priebus and newly appointed Communications Director Anthony Scaramucci, who had launched a scathing attack on Priebus in a recent interview, calling him a "paranoid schizophrenic." CNN's Wolf Blitzer interviewed Priebus on "The Situation Room" Friday evening, where he declined to respond to the comments.

In contrast, Trump praised Kelly, describing him as a "great leader" and highlighting his impressive background as a nearly five-decade veteran of the US military, including stints as chief of Southern Command and senior assistant to the Secretary of Defense. Kelly will take over as chief of staff on Monday, participating in a meeting of Trump's cabinet, and will oversee a transition period.

The shake-up marks the shortest tenure of a White House chief of staff in modern history, with Priebus lasting just 189 days in the role. His departure follows a series of high-profile exits from the administration, including the departures of Press Secretary Sean Spicer and Deputy Chief of Staff Katie Walsh, both of whom were close allies of Priebus.

As news of Priebus' departure broke, Trump traveled to Long Island for an event on gang violence, where he was accompanied by Representative Peter King. CNN reported that there was reportedly no indication of the impending shake-up during the trip, with Priebus maintaining a "poker face" aboard Air Force One. Trump's family, including daughter Ivanka Trump, had reportedly lost confidence in Priebus, with one source claiming they urged the President to make a change.

Meanwhile, White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon is expected to remain in his position, despite rumors of his potential departure. The sudden shift in leadership comes as the White House continues to grapple with controversy and infighting, with Trump facing mounting pressure to address pressing policy issues and restore stability within his administration.

reference: – Reince Priebus is out as White House chief of staff, the AP reports, as President Trump announced in a tweet Friday: "I am pleased to inform you that I have just named General/Secretary John F Kelly as White House Chief of Staff. He is a Great American...." Trump continued, "...and a Great Leader. John has also done a spectacular job at Homeland Security. He has been a true star of my Administration." Kelly, a retired general and the current Homeland Security secretary, was named in a New York Times report last night as Trump's preferred replacement for Priebus, and CNN reports that Priebus resigned privately Thursday. Trump also spoke about the change Friday after Air Force One landed at Joint Base Andrews outside Washington, calling Priebus a "good man" and saying Kelly "will do a fantastic job."

final_summary:
On April 10, a remarkable incident unfolded at the Royal Burgers Zoo in Arnhem, Netherlands, showcasing the intelligence and resourcefulness of a chimpanzee named Tushi. A Dutch TV crew was filming a documentary using a camera-equipped drone, which caught the attention of the chimpanzees in their 1.7-acre enclosure. Two females, Tushi and Raime, climbed onto scaffolding, each grasping a 6-foot-long branch, while others nearby picked up smaller twigs. 

As the drone drew closer, Tushi, with a deliberate and determined expression, swiped at it twice with her branch, successfully knocking the device to the ground on the second attempt. The camera captured the moment, revealing Tushi's grimacing face and tense posture, indicating a calculated and forceful action rather than a fearful or reflexive response.

Researchers Jan van Hooff and Bas Lukkenaar, whose study was published in the journal Primates, analyzed the incident and concluded that Tushi's actions suggest forward planning and deliberation. The pair noted that Tushi had collected the branch before the drone approached and carried it to a location where it could be effectively used as a weapon, demonstrating a level of premeditation.

According to van Hooff and Lukkenaar, this behavior is consistent with previous observations of the chimpanzees at the Royal Burgers Zoo, who have shown a propensity for innovative tool use and problem-solving. The researchers pointed out that the chimps have not been explicitly taught how to use various tools but have learned through observation of humans handling different implements.

In an article published in the journal Primates, van Hooff and Lukkenaar explained that the incident occurred on April 10, when a Dutch television crew was filming at the zoo in Arnhem. The idea was to use a drone to film the chimpanzees in their compound from different close-up angles. The drone already caught the chimpanzees' attention during a practice run, prompting some to grab willow twigs off the ground, while four animals took these along when they climbed up scaffolding where the drone was hovering.

Filming started when the next drone flew over, zooming in on two chimpanzees, the females Tushi and Raimee. They were still seated on the scaffolding holding on to twigs that were about 180 cm (approximately six feet) long. Tushi made two long sweeps with hers – the second was successful in downing the drone and ultimately breaking it. Before and during the strike, she grimaced. Although her face was tense and her teeth were bared, she showed no signs of fear. This suggests that she quite deliberately and forcefully struck at the drone, rather than fearfully or reflexively.

Van Hooff commented on the uniqueness of Tushi's actions, noting that the use of the stick as a weapon in this context seemed deliberate, given the decision to collect and position it for optimal effect. Lukkenaar added that this incident adds to the indications that chimpanzees engage in forward planning of tool-use acts, highlighting their ability to think critically and adapt to new situations.

The study, published in the journal Primates, has significant implications for our understanding of chimpanzee cognition and behavior. The incident occurred in New York | Heidelberg, on 3 September 2015, and was documented in an article published by Springer.
