reference: – The best overall diet is the DASH diet, US News & World Report says in new rankings—but if weight loss is your specific goal, go with Weight Watchers. So what's DASH? It stands for Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension and it's generally meant for adults looking to curb high blood pressure. It's big on "whole grains, fruits, vegetables, low-fat and nonfat dairy foods, lean proteins, nuts and limited amounts of fats and sweets," explains the Los Angeles Times, which writes about it for a different reason: A new study suggests it slows weight gain in teen girls. Back on the US News & World rankings: The Mediterranean diet came in second for overall diet, while Jenny Craig and the Raw Food diet were close runners-up for weight loss, Reuters reports. “Our analysis put hard numbers on the common-sense belief that no diet is ideal for everybody,” says the magazine. It all depends on your aim. Click for details on the specific diets and complete rankings.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, Chicago emerged victorious in a fierce competition against Los Angeles and San Francisco to host the Lucas Museum of Narrative Art, a $1 billion interactive museum showcasing George Lucas' vast collection of artwork and movie memorabilia. The museum, fully funded by Lucas himself, will be situated on a 17-acre site between Soldier Field and McCormick Place, with plans to open in 2018. 

Mayor Rahm Emanuel played a crucial role in securing the deal, leveraging the city's central location, accessible public transportation, and rich artistic heritage to win over Lucas. Emanuel's swift action came after Lucas' initial choice for the museum's location, San Francisco's Crissy Field within the Presidio national park, was rejected. The museum's board cited these advantages, as well as the availability of a larger location, as key factors in their decision. 

Lucas, who is married to Chicago businesswoman Mellody Hobson, expressed his gratitude towards the city, stating that building in Chicago was "the right decision for the museum" despite his personal ties to the Bay Area, where he grew up and built his film career. The museum will feature a diverse range of exhibits, including Norman Rockwell paintings, special effects from Industrial Light & Magic, and iconic movie memorabilia such as the Darth Vader costume. With a focus on storytelling, the collection spans various mediums, including magazine illustrations that pre-date the advent of moving pictures, television, and radio. 

The museum's CEO, Laurie Norton Moffatt, described Lucas' collection as "still an emerging and growing collection," with a notable emphasis on movie posters, which she believes is the largest collection in the world. The museum will be built on a site that was previously used for parking, with the city planning to relocate the parking spaces underground at Lucas' expense. However, the impact on Chicago Bears tailgaters remains uncertain.

While the museum has garnered support from some quarters, it has also faced opposition from open-space advocates, including Friends of the Parks, who argue that the location violates the city's Lakefront Plan and Protection Ordinance. Despite this, Emanuel remains optimistic, stating that the museum will create hundreds of jobs and stimulate economic growth, while also partnering with local schools to provide educational opportunities. Lucas, who is worth an estimated $5.1 billion, has committed to accumulating a $400 million endowment for the museum over time. His philanthropic efforts extend beyond the museum, with he and Hobson having donated $25 million each to two local education-focused charities since their marriage.

As the museum moves forward, it is poised to become a significant cultural and economic asset for the city of Chicago, solidifying its position as a hub for arts and education.

reference: – Chicago will soon be home George Lucas' collection of art, film memorabilia, animation, and more—though the city wasn't necessarily Lucas' first choice for his private museum. The filmmaker proposed a $700 million plan earlier this year to build the museum in San Francisco, but his chosen bayside location was rejected, the New York Times reports. The city's Presidio Trust suggested he put the museum near his former film studio. Instead, he opted for Chicago, the Chicago Tribune reports. Chicago mayor Rahm Emanuel had battled to win the museum for the city, where Lucas lives part time; his wife is from Chicago. "I am humbled to be joining such an extraordinary museum community and to be creating the museum in a city that has a long tradition of embracing the arts and architecture,” Lucas says. “Choosing Chicago is the right decision for the museum, but a difficult decision for me personally because of my strong personal and professional roots in the Bay Area." The museum, set to open in 2018, will display items as varied as Norman Rockwell paintings, a Darth Vader suit, movie posters, and special-effects technology, the Tribune notes. The Chicago Sun-Times calls it a $1 billion project, noting that it will be built on the city's lakefront. (It's not the first time Lucas has had to retool a massive building project.)

final_summary:
Eric Hites, a 560-pound man from Danville, Indiana, embarked on a remarkable journey to bike across the United States, driven by a desire to lose weight, regain his health, and rekindle his marriage. According to The Daily, Hites weighed over 560 pounds and felt like a failure, unable to hold a job due to his weight and struggling to connect with his estranged wife. However, after calling his wife and vowing to change, he started hitting the gym, preparing for his epic adventure.

On June 10, Hites began his journey from Falmouth, Massachusetts, with a $170 bike and a trailer carrying 300 pounds of equipment. Despite initial struggles with a poorly equipped bike, he persevered, posting updates on his blog and Facebook page, Fatguyacrossamerica. As he rode through various towns, Hites encountered numerous challenges, including a bent rim that forced him to stop in Tiverton, Rhode Island, where he received support from the local community.

The Newport Daily News reported that Newport Bicycle, a local bike shop, generously donated a new bike with a suspension system suitable for Hites' frame, allowing him to continue his journey. The Tiverton Police Department also contributed supplies, and a growing online community rallied around him, offering encouragement and financial support through a GoFundMe account. The account's initial amount ballooned to nearly $3,500, demonstrating the widespread interest in Hites' inspiring story.

As news of his journey spread, Hites' wife took notice of his dedication and commitment to change. In an interview with The Newport Daily News, she acknowledged that his efforts were the most romantic gesture she'd ever seen, and their relationship began to heal. Hites' story resonated with people nationwide, particularly those struggling with similar issues, and he became an unlikely inspiration for others to pursue their goals.

The Beach Body network of personal trainers launched a national campaign to promote physical activity, featuring Hites as a symbol of perseverance and determination. With a newfound sense of purpose, Hites continued his journey, taking it one day at a time, and choosing routes based on his intuition. As he navigates the country, Hites remains focused on his mission to lose weight, rebuild his life, and become a beacon of hope for those facing similar challenges.

Having reached 560 pounds thanks to soda and a whole lot of sitting around, the DJ says both his health and his marriage are at stake, reports the Newport Daily News. Unfortunately, after getting off to a good start in Falmouth, Mass., (Tiverton-Little Compton Patch reports his dad dropped him and his $170 bike "on the side of the road") and losing 60 pounds in those first two weeks, the Indiana man hit a bump in the road, reports Fox 59: He bent the rim of one of his wheels in Tiverton, RI. "I thought it would take four months, but [having started in June] I’m only in Rhode Island," he says. Fortunately the owner of nearby Newport Bicycle, who says it "seems like a really good cause," built him a new bike by fitting a frame with mountain bike wheels that should support his weight all the way to California; he'll reportedly hit the road again today. Having first made a name for himself when he published the cookbook Everybody Loves Ramen, a compilation of 50 recipes from his college days (he studied graphic design but never graduated), Hites hopes to publish again, this time about his journey. He's traveling with his gear in tow, and writes on his website Fat Guy Across America that first and foremost he's got "to prove things to my wife and love." Patch reports the two were estranged when he set off, and that his wife, who was widowed once before, didn't want to repeat the experience. She has reportedly told him his quest is "the most romantic thing." Hites says he doesn't know his exact weight now (he dresses in size 70 pants), but he may weigh himself on the big scale at the junkyard up the road before getting back on the saddle. He says he hopes to drop down to a chubby but healthier 300 pounds. (When it comes to marriage, this is apparently the best age to wed.)

final_summary:
Today, Netflix announced a significant change to its pricing structure, effective starting in September, where it will no longer offer a combined streaming video and by-mail DVD subscription plan for $9.99 a month. Instead, the company will split each distribution mode into two separate subscription plans, priced at $7.99 per month, with a combined plan costing $15.98. This move sparked widespread criticism from customers, with thousands taking to the company's blog to express their discontent over the past couple of days.

A day before unveiling the price hike, Netflix managers informed customer-service personnel to expect an influx of angry calls, anticipating some customers would be unhappy with the decision. According to customer service representatives, hundreds of employees were manning the phones, but even with this increased staffing, many callers experienced lengthy wait times, often waiting between 9 minutes and 15 minutes to speak with a representative.

Wall Street analyst Rich Greenfield, from BTIG Research, conducted a test of Netflix's customer service department and found that the company was severely overwhelmed by calls. Greenfield noted that the representatives suggested to customers threatening to cancel their subscriptions that they should wait until September, when the new pricing takes effect, rather than offering any discounts or promotions to retain their business. This approach led Greenfield to question whether Netflix is truly concerned about losing subscribers.

In a statement to CNET, Netflix spokesperson Steve Swasey acknowledged that the company had anticipated the negative reaction to the price hike and had taken steps to prepare for it. Swasey stated that Netflix had conducted research and analysis prior to the announcement and was aware that some customers would be upset by the change. Despite the criticism, Swasey maintained that the new pricing structure is necessary to support the company's continued growth and expansion in Hollywood.

Meanwhile, Netflix has secured a new multi-year licensing agreement with NBC Universal, which will bring additional content to the platform, including new episodes of popular shows like "The Office" and "Law and Order: SVU", as well as films such as "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind" and "The Motorcycle Diaries". However, this deal may not be enough to appease subscribers who are upset about the price increase, particularly given the loss of Sony-owned content from the digital library and ongoing issues with other studios and networks.

reference: – Well, now we know what a portion of those new, higher Netflix fees could be used for: to pay call center reps to deal with angry customers. Customer service reps have been telling callers that the company not only told them to be ready for a backlash, it hired extra employees to take the calls, CNET reports. “We tested, we researched, we analyzed. We knew what the reaction was going to be,” a spokesman confirmed. “We are not surprised.” Despite the extra manpower, the phone lines have been overwhelmed. One analyst found that it took representatives nine to 15 minutes to get to the many callers on hold. When they did pick up, their suggestion was to just cancel in September, when the price increase hits. “There was simply no promo or 'save' technique to … retain our business,” he complained, indicating that “Netflix is simply not concerned with the prospect of losing customers.” But hey, it’s not all bad news, Netflix users: The company has renewed its deal with NBC Universal, so at least you can stream new episodes of 30 Rock.

final_summary:
With the start of 2013, thousands of new state laws took effect across the country, reflecting the diverse efforts of state governments to address pressing issues and concerns. In Illinois, residents may have bid farewell to their dreams of performing daring stunts on motorcycles, as the state outlawed wheelies effective immediately. Furthermore, strip clubs in Illinois are now required to charge a $3 entrance fee, with all proceeds going towards supporting local rape crisis centers.

In neighboring Kentucky, the release of domesticated pigs into the wild has become a punishable offense, while Californians can rejoice over the introduction of driverless cars on the roads, albeit with a human passenger seated in the vehicle at all times. Pet owners in Wellington, Kansas, face a new limit of four cats per household, effectively putting a cap on cat lovers' enthusiasm for adopting multiple felines.

Concord, Massachusetts, made a bold step towards environmental sustainability by banning plastic water bottles, joining the ranks of states taking proactive measures against pollution. North Carolinians, on the other hand, risk facing felony charges if they attempt to steal cooking oil valued at over $1,000.

California continued to push forward with progressive policies, mandating coaches and school administrators to report suspected child abuse, imposing stricter penalties for parents failing to report missing or deceased children within 24 hours, and expanding official authority to investigate mortgage fraud. The state also prohibited lenders from foreclosing on homeowners seeking loan modifications.

Maryland became the latest jurisdiction to legalize gay marriage, mirroring a similar move in Maine, where same-sex couples from other states are now recognized. New Hampshire enacted a ban on partial-birth abortions, while Montana introduced parental notification requirements for minors seeking abortions. Pennsylvania implemented the E-Verify system to verify employees' citizenship on public-works projects, and Montana began withholding state services from undocumented immigrants.

Illinois also took a stand against workplace privacy invasion by making it unlawful for employers to demand access to employees' social media passwords. According to experts, these new laws reflect the efforts of state governments to address pressing issues and concerns, despite voter dissatisfaction with their legislatures. Robert Stern, former president of the Center for Governmental Studies, noted that lawmakers pass laws that range from extremely important to ridiculous, often cleaning up past mistakes or eliminating outdated laws.

reference: – While everyone fretted about the fiscal cliff, thousands of new state laws quietly took effect at midnight. The Christian Science Monitor, the Washington Post, and Mediaite run down a few notable ones, from the weird (feral hogs?) to the more conventional: Caylee's Law: Legislation named after Caylee Anthony took effect in California and Illinois. Parents now face big penalties if they don't report the disappearance or death of a child within one day. Abortion: Partial-birth abortions are now banned in New Hampshire. In Montana, minors wanting an abortion must notify their parents. Illegal immigration: Employers in some states are now required to use the E-Verify system to confirm citizenship of their employees. Facebook: Employers can no longer ask job applicants for Facebook or Twitter passwords. Food safety: In Maryland, it's now illegal to use chicken feed that includes arsenic. Though it's the first state to pass such a ban, the practice is already illegal in Canada and the EU. Driverless cars: Are now legal on California roads, though a human must always be in the passenger seat. Weirder laws: No releasing feral hogs in Kentucky, and no using a dog to chase a bear or a bobcat in California. Strip clubs in Illinois must now charge $3 at the door and donate it to rape crisis centers. Also in Illinois, motorcycle wheelies are now banned. Also today, same-sex marriage became legal in Maryland.

final_summary:
Australian actor Chris Hemsworth, known for his role as Thor in the Marvel cinematic universe, had a fortunate turn of events when he lost his wallet while dining with his family in a "rough" area of Los Angeles on April 18, 2016. The wallet, containing a substantial amount of cash, was left unattended on the table, leading Hemsworth to believe it would never be recovered. 

Tristin Budzyn-Baker, a 17-year-old, stumbled upon the wallet and discovered its owner's identity after looking through it. He then wrote a letter to Hemsworth's management, mentioning that he found the wallet and requesting tickets to The Ellen DeGeneres Show for his mom, as Hemsworth was scheduled to appear on the show shortly.

Budzyn-Baker's honesty paid off when he received tickets to The Ellen DeGeneres Show as a reward for his actions. He and his mother attended the show, where Hemsworth shared the story of how he lost his wallet and commended Budzyn-Baker for his integrity. During the show, Hemsworth handed Budzyn-Baker a significant sum of money, stating, "I expected it was going to be empty, and all the cash was in there, so I want to give you the cash – there's a little extra in there."

Ellen DeGeneres surprised Budzyn-Baker with an additional reward, and Hemsworth penned a heartfelt letter of recommendation for Budzyn-Baker, a Boy Scout, to aid in his pursuit of the esteemed Eagle Scout award. This remarkable display of kindness and recognition serves as a testament to the power of honesty and the positive impact it can have on individuals.

The wallet, containing a substantial amount of cash, was left unattended on the table, leading Hemsworth to believe it would never be recovered. However, fate intervened when 17-year-old Tristin Budzyn-Baker stumbled upon the wallet and discovered its owner's identity. Recognizing the significance of the wallet's contents, Budzyn-Baker took the noble step of tracking down Hemsworth and returning the wallet intact, complete with its cash.

Budzyn-Baker's selfless act did not go unnoticed, as he soon received a call from Hemsworth's management team, expressing gratitude for his honesty. As a token of appreciation, Hemsworth invited Budzyn-Baker and his mother to appear on The Ellen DeGeneres Show, where they were seated in the audience on a Monday. During the show, Hemsworth shared the story of how he lost his wallet and praised Budzyn-Baker for his integrity. To further acknowledge Budzyn-Baker's good deed, Hemsworth handed him a significant sum of money, stating, "I expected it was going to be empty, and all the cash was in there, so I want to give you the cash – there's a little extra in there."

The generosity did not stop there; Ellen DeGeneres surprised Budzyn-Baker with an additional $10,000 reward, bringing the total compensation to a staggering $12,833. Furthermore, Hemsworth penned a heartfelt letter of recommendation for Budzyn-Baker, a Boy Scout, to aid in his pursuit of the esteemed Eagle Scout award. This remarkable display of kindness and recognition serves as a testament to the power of honesty and the positive impact it can have on individuals and their communities.

reference: – A 17-year-old kid who returned Thor's missing wallet is an even bigger Boy Scout than Captain America—literally. Mashable reports actor Chris Hemsworth was eating with his family in a "rough" area of Los Angeles when he forgot his wallet. "I left my wallet on the table and thought, 'I'm never going to get it back, I'm never going to see it again,'" Hemsworth said on Monday's episode of The Ellen DeGeneres Show. But he didn't count on Tristin Budzyn-Baker, who found the wallet. "I looked up at my mom and was like, 'Ma, do you know who this is?' We found Thor's wallet,'" Budzyn-Baker told Ellen. Budzyn-Baker wrote a letter to Hemsworth's management, asking only for tickets for his mom to see Hemsworth on Ellen's show. He got that and more, USA Today reports. "I expected it was going to be empty, and all the cash was in there, so I want to give you the cash—there's a little extra in there," Hemsworth told Budzyn-Baker during Monday's show, handing the teen a wad of cash. It didn't stop there. Budzyn-Baker got $10,000 from Ellen for being "such an honest guy," and Hemsworth wrote the Boy Scout a letter of recommendation for the Eagle Scout award. It seems it pays to be a hero's hero.

final_summary:
Beverly Hills, Calif. and Menlo Park, Calif., Dec. 18, 2013 - William Morris Endeavor Entertainment (WME), a leading talent, entertainment, and media company based in Beverly Hills, California, announced an agreement to acquire IMG Worldwide, a global market leader in college and professional sports, event management, client representation, fashion, and multi-media rights management. 

The deal brings together two prominent players in their respective fields, creating a unique global sports and entertainment platform that spans North America, Europe, Asia, South America, and Africa. WME, which was formed through the 2009 merger of the William Morris Agency and Endeavor, boasts an impressive client roster and a broad relationship base with sponsors, brands, and broadcasters. 

Patrick Whitesell and Ariel Emanuel, co-CEOs of WME, expressed enthusiasm for the deal, stating that IMG has incredible strategic value due to its global reach, outstanding management team, and leadership across sports, fashion, and media. They plan to build on the legacy of founder Mark McCormack and recent owner Ted Forstmann, supported by Silver Lake's continued partnership, to deliver a broad range of opportunities and resources to the companies and talent they collectively represent. 

Silver Lake, a global leader in technology investing, partnered with WME to facilitate the acquisition. As a minority investor, Mubadala Development Company will join forces with WME and Silver Lake to support the combined entity. Egon Durban, managing partner at Silver Lake, highlighted the significant and untapped potential for growth within IMG's existing markets and emphasized the company's commitment to accelerating its existing growth plans and expanding its digital platform. 

The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, with terms not disclosed. The combined company will operate across multiple locations, including New York, London, and other international hubs. This acquisition reflects the evolving nature of the entertainment industry, where talent agencies and entertainment companies are adapting to changing consumer habits and technological advancements.

history.draft:
On December 18, 2013, William Morris Endeavor Entertainment (WME), a leading talent, entertainment, and media company based in Beverly Hills, California, announced an agreement to acquire IMG Worldwide, a global market leader in college and professional sports, event management, client representation, fashion, and multi-media rights management. This acquisition marks a significant development in the rapidly shifting landscape of Hollywood, where the economics of the industry are undergoing a profound transformation.

The deal brings together two prominent players in their respective fields, creating a unique global sports and entertainment platform that spans North America, Europe, Asia, South America, and Africa. WME, which was formed through the 2009 merger of the William Morris Agency and Endeavor, boasts an impressive client roster and a broad relationship base with sponsors, brands, and broadcasters. With this acquisition, WME gains access to IMG's extensive network and expertise in sports, events, film, and television, further solidifying its position as a major player in the entertainment industry.

Patrick Whitesell and Ariel Emanuel, co-CEOs of WME, expressed enthusiasm for the deal, citing IMG's global reach, exceptional management team, and leadership across various sectors as key factors that make the acquisition an attractive opportunity. They plan to leverage these strengths to drive growth and expansion, particularly in the digital space.

Silver Lake, a global leader in technology investing, partnered with WME to facilitate the acquisition. As a minority investor, Mubadala Development Company will join forces with WME and Silver Lake to support the combined entity. Egon Durban, managing partner at Silver Lake, highlighted the vast potential for growth within IMG's existing markets and emphasized the company's commitment to accelerating its growth plans and expanding its digital platform.

The acquisition is expected to create a powerhouse in the entertainment industry, with a presence in key locations such as New York, London, and Los Angeles. The deal reflects the evolving nature of Hollywood, where talent agencies and entertainment companies are adapting to changing consumer habits and technological advancements. As the entertainment landscape continues to shift, this acquisition positions WME and IMG Worldwide for long-term success in the global market.

history.draft:
Beverly Hills, Calif. and Menlo Park, Calif., Dec. 18, 2013 - William Morris Endeavor Entertainment (WME), a leading talent, entertainment, and media company based in Beverly Hills, California, announced an agreement to acquire IMG Worldwide, a global market leader in college and professional sports, event management, client representation, fashion, and multi-media rights management. 

The deal brings together two prominent players in their respective fields, creating a unique global sports and entertainment platform that spans North America, Europe, Asia, South America, and Africa. WME, which was formed through the 2009 merger of the William Morris Agency and Endeavor, boasts an impressive client roster and a broad relationship base with sponsors, brands, and broadcasters. 

Patrick Whitesell and Ariel Emanuel, co-CEOs of WME, expressed enthusiasm for the deal, stating that IMG has incredible strategic value due to its global reach, outstanding management team, and leadership across sports, fashion, and media. They plan to build on the legacy of founder Mark McCormack and recent owner Ted Forstmann, supported by Silver Lake's continued partnership, to deliver a broad range of opportunities and resources to the companies and talent they collectively represent. 

Silver Lake, a global leader in technology investing, partnered with WME to facilitate the acquisition. As a minority investor, Mubadala Development Company will join forces with WME and Silver Lake to support the combined entity. Egon Durban, managing partner at Silver Lake, highlighted the significant and untapped potential for growth within IMG's existing markets and emphasized the company's commitment to accelerating its existing growth plans and expanding its digital platform. 

The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, with terms not disclosed. The combined company will operate across multiple locations, including New York, London, and other international hubs. This acquisition reflects the evolving nature of the entertainment industry, where talent agencies and entertainment companies are adapting to changing consumer habits and technological advancements.

reference: – What do Oprah Winfrey and Maria Sharapova have in common? Soon, they could both be represented by the same company. Hollywood talent titan William Morris Endeavor Entertainment has reached a deal to buy the massive sports agency IMG Worldwide, the companies announced today. "IMG has incredible strategic value," WME's co-CEOs said in a statement. IMG's "leadership across sports, fashion, and media are a strong complement to our business." WME, and its private equity partner Silver Lake, will pay about $2.4 billion, sources tell the Wall Street Journal, beating out a bid from another private equity firm, CVC Capital Partners, for about $2 billion. The deal is in part a sign that movie stars aren't what they used to be, the Journal explains; Hollywood agencies are diversifying in part because contracts for A-list stars have fallen off significantly. The fall of sitcoms and rise of original cable shows have also cut into once massive syndication revenues.

final_summary:
Scientists have long sought to understand the mysteries of the universe, particularly the nature of its smallest entities. Horizon, a documentary series, embarked on an ambitious journey to explore the realm of the infinitesimally small, where the laws of physics begin to break down and the fabric of reality becomes increasingly distorted. 

Professor Andy Parker, a physicist at Cambridge University in England, notes that the concept of the smallest thing in the universe has undergone significant transformations throughout human history. Historically, people believed that grains of sand were the fundamental building blocks of our surroundings, but later, atoms were considered the cornerstone of matter. However, the discovery of subatomic particles such as protons, neutrons, and electrons revealed that even these seemingly indivisible components are composed of quarks. Parker expresses skepticism regarding the notion that quarks and electrons might be the ultimate building blocks of matter, citing their point-like behavior in experiments and the resulting infinite forces when approached closely.

To address this paradox, scientists have proposed alternative theories, including superstring theory, which posits that particles are not point-like objects but rather tiny loops of string. Another contender for the smallest thing in the universe is the singularity at the center of a black hole, a region where gravity warps space-time. Most experts, including Parker, believe that black holes are not infinitely dense but rather a manifestation of the conflict between general relativity and quantum mechanics. When a unified theory of quantum gravity is developed, the true nature of black holes will be revealed, potentially showing them to be much smaller than previously thought, possibly even comparable to the size of superstrings.

The Planck length, measuring 1.6 x 10^-35 meters, serves as a theoretical boundary beyond which the universe becomes probabilistic and indeterminate. This scale marks the transition zone between general relativity and quantum mechanics, where the gravitational field becomes so strong that it can create black holes from energy. According to Parker, the Planck length represents the smallest measurable unit of length, and it is possible that all the universe's smallest things, including superstrings, singularities, and even space-time grains, are approximately this size.

As researchers continue to probe the mysteries of the universe, they may need to revise our understanding of time and space. Clara Moskowitz, reporting for LiveScience, highlights the ongoing efforts of scientists, such as those featured in the Horizon documentary, who are pushing the boundaries of human knowledge and shedding light on the enigmatic world of the infinitesimally small. This groundbreaking research has been reported by LiveScience, a leading online publication, and can be followed on social media platforms, including Twitter (@ClaraMoskowitz and @Livescience), Facebook, and Google+.

As researchers continue to probe the mysteries of the universe, they may need to revise our understanding of time and space. Clara Moskowitz, reporting for LiveScience, highlights the ongoing efforts of scientists, such as those featured in the Horizon documentary, who are pushing the boundaries of human knowledge and shedding light on the enigmatic world of the infinitesimally small. This groundbreaking research has been reported by LiveScience, a leading online publication, and can be followed on social media platforms, including Twitter (@ClaraMoskowitz and @Livescience), Facebook, and Google+.

reference: – The tiniest thing in the universe is not a grain of sand, as people once thought, nor a proton or neutron, or even a quark or electron—as small as they all are. In fact, experts today balk at naming any point-like object "the smallest" because of a theoretical conundrum: When you're infinitely near any point, the forces that act on it turn infinitely huge. Hoping to duck the whole "infinite" thing, scientists have invented novel theories around it, physicist Andy Parker tells Space.com. Among the work-arounds: Superstring theory, which posits that particles are like loopy strings rather than points. This would enable scientists to get infinitely close to something but always remain further from another part of it. "Space-time foam," the theory that space is made of pixels or grains. This would keeps particles from being infinitely close to each other, because the grain create a natural distance. One candidate for "smallest" that Parker discounts is black hole singularities, although he concedes that "they are maybe a million million times or even more than that smaller than the distances we've seen so far." See excerpts of Parker's BBC show on the subject.

final_summary:
Former Florida Republican Party Chairman Jim Greer has come forward with explosive allegations of voter suppression and internal party turmoil in a 630-page deposition spanning two days in late May. According to Greer, party officials were discussing ways to suppress the black vote during a December 2009 meeting, which included talks of "keeping blacks from voting." This claim directly contradicts the long-held stance of many Republicans that voter ID laws and similar measures are necessary to prevent voter fraud, rather than suppress minority votes.

Greer's testimony paints a picture of a deeply divided party, with officials at odds over issues such as the appointment of a liberal African-American judge to the Florida Supreme Court, Governor Charlie Crist's endorsement of John McCain for president in 2008, and Crist's infamous hug with President Barack Obama in 2009. The tension culminated in a power struggle, with Greer ultimately agreeing to resign in January 2010 for the "betterment of the party" as part of a severance agreement worth $130,000.

However, things took a turn for the worse when Greer claimed that party officials, including Senate President Mike Haridopolos and Senator John Thrasher, failed to uphold the severance agreement. Greer alleged that Thrasher, who succeeded him as party chairman, was aware of the agreement but chose to deny its existence, leading to a series of broken promises and ultimately, a statewide grand jury indictment on charges of money laundering and fraud.

Greer's deposition also shed light on the controversy surrounding his creation of Victory Strategies LLC, a company that collected nearly $200,000 from the House Republican Party while he was in charge. Greer claimed that party officials, including General Counsel Jason Gonzalez, were aware of his involvement in the company but chose to keep it hidden. Gonzalez, however, disputed Greer's account, stating that Greer never owned up to his involvement in the business.

As the deposition revealed, Greer's relationship with party officials deteriorated rapidly, with him labeling them as "whack-a-do, right-wing crazies" and accusing them of orchestrating a scheme to avoid paying him the promised severance package. The situation took a toll on Greer's personal life, with his family relying on food stamps and some of his possessions being repossessed.

Lucy Morgan, a senior correspondent for the Times, obtained copies of the deposition and other related documents, providing a glimpse into the inner workings of the Florida Republican Party. As the deposition suggests, the party's actions may have gone beyond mere infighting, with potentially far-reaching implications for the state's electoral landscape.

reference: – Liberals have been accusing recent GOP-led voter ID laws of aiming to disenfranchise minorities, who tend to vote Democrat. Now former Florida GOP chairman Jim Greer is saying on record that, yes, those laws are actually about stopping black people from voting, reports Salon, picking up on a story in the Tampa Bay Times. “I was upset because the political consultants and staff were talking about voter suppression and keeping blacks from voting,” Greer said, recalling a 2009 meeting with party officials. Greer also said the party's budget committee had been taken over by "whack-a-do, right-wing crazies." Greer is hardly without an axe to grind, though. His statements came from a 630-page deposition made in May as part of his trial on corruption charges. Greer paid a company he owned nearly $200,000 from GOP coffers while he was the chairman, although he contends it was authorized and made for services rendered. Florida state Sen. John Thrasher, who succeeded Greer as party chairman, called Greer's comments about voter suppression "baseless accusations on other people in an effort to divert attention from himself.''

final_summary:
On December 14, 2018, insurance company Pacific Life announced it would be pulling its advertisements from Fox News' "Tucker Carlson Tonight" after the host, Tucker Carlson, made inflammatory comments about the US immigration situation during his opening monologue on the evening of December 13, 2018. Specifically, Carlson stated that the "immigration crisis" in America makes the country "poorer and dirtier and more divided." This remark was followed immediately by an advertisement promoting Pacific Life, which highlighted its 150-year history of protecting generations of families. However, this juxtaposition sparked widespread criticism on social media, with many calling for the company to distance itself from the program due to the perceived inconsistency between the values represented by the company and those expressed by Carlson.

In response, Pacific Life released a statement on Twitter stating that they would be removing their ads from the show "in the coming weeks as we reevaluate our relationship with his program." The company emphasized that they "strongly disagree with Mr. Carlson's statements" and took pride in the diversity of their customer base and workforce, reflecting the diversity of the nation. Fox News responded to the controversy with a statement to The Hill, labeling the backlash as "unfortunate and unnecessary distractions." They claimed that left-wing advocacy groups were using social media to stifle free speech and intimidate companies into dropping their support for the program. In a separate incident, Carlson also made derogatory comments about immigrants, describing them as "cynical shake-down artists who have been watching too much CNN." This further fueled the public outcry, with critics on Twitter continuing to call for sponsors to boycott the program.

reference: – Comments about immigrants that Tucker Carlson made on his Fox News show Thursday have just caused a major advertiser to give pause. The Hill reports that insurance company Pacific Life is, at least for "the coming weeks," yanking its ads from Tucker Carlson Tonight as it "[reevaluates] our relationship with his program," per a statement posted on Twitter. What Carlson said that prompted Pacific Life's retreat: Per HuffPost, the Fox host was taking issue with "previous" US leaders who insisted that Americans "shut up and accept" immigrants. Then, in a continued mocking of those previous leaders, Carlson went on to say that "we have a moral obligation to admit the world's poor, they tell us, even if it makes our own country poorer, and dirtier and more divided." Pacific Life's ad aired immediately after the opening segment in which Carlson made those remarks. "As a company, we strongly disagree with Mr. Carlson's statements," Pacific Life says. "Our customer base and our workforce reflect the diversity of our great nation, something we take great pride in." In its own statement to the Hill, Fox News lamented the "unfortunate and unnecessary distractions" it says were spurred by "left wing advocacy groups [that] under the guise of being supposed 'media watchdogs' weaponize social media against companies in an effort to stifle free speech." Newsweek notes that, before this incident, Carlson has never been boycotted by advertisers, though other Fox News hosts like Laura Ingraham and Bill O'Reilly (who's no longer with the network) have.

final_summary:
A poignant plea for peace has emerged from a young voice that captured the world's attention through social media. Bana Alabed, a seven-year-old Syrian girl from Aleppo, has written an open letter to Donald Trump, the newly inaugurated President of America, urging him to take action to protect the children of Syria. Bana's letter, penned days before Trump's inauguration, reflects her personal experience of living through the brutal conflict in Aleppo, where she witnessed the destruction of her school and the loss of some of her friends due to bombings.

Bana's story began when she started tweeting from besieged East Aleppo, garnering hundreds of thousands of followers with her heart-wrenching accounts of life under siege. With the help of her mother, Fatemah, Bana's tweets provided a unique glimpse into the lives of those trapped in the war-torn city. After being evacuated to Turkey in December last year, Bana continued to use her platform to raise awareness about the plight of Syrian children, now focusing on the need for peace and protection.

In her letter to Trump, Bana appeals to the President's sense of compassion, stating that Syrian children are "like your children and deserve peace like you." She emphasizes the importance of peace, citing her own experiences in Turkey, where she can now attend school and enjoy a relatively peaceful life. However, Bana acknowledges that millions of Syrian children continue to suffer in various parts of the country, victimized by the actions of adults.

The BBC reported that Bana had written the letter in the days before Trump's inauguration. The letter serves as a powerful reminder of the human cost of the Syrian conflict, which has resulted in over 300,000 deaths, including at least 15,000 children, and thousands of displaced individuals. The Syrian Network For Human Rights reports that nearly 2,000 children lost their lives in 2016 alone. As the international community grapples with finding a solution to the crisis, Bana's words carry significant weight, particularly given the complex dynamics at play between major powers such as the United States, Russia, and Turkey.

Trump's stance on Syria remains unclear, with his administration signaling openness to working with Russia, which supports the regime of President Bashar al-Assad. The recent joint pledge by Iran, Russia, and Turkey to enforce a three-week ceasefire in Syria offers a glimmer of hope, but its success hinges on the cooperation of all parties involved. Amidst this uncertainty, Bana's letter stands as a testament to the resilience and determination of the Syrian people, particularly the children, who continue to bear the brunt of the conflict.

reference: – A 7-year-old Syrian girl whose tweets from Aleppo showcased the horrors of war has appealed to President Trump to "do something for the children of Syria." Now living in Turkey, Bana Alabed wrote a letter to the new president days before his inauguration, the BBC reports. "Dear Donald Trump," she writes, "I lived in Syria my whole life (and) ... I am part of the Syrian children who suffered from the Syrian war." Although she is now safe, "millions of Syrian children are not like me," she writes. "They are suffering. … I know you will be the president of America, so can you please save the children and people of Syria?" Bana's mother, Fatemah, sent the text to the BBC. (Read the full letter here.) Bana opted to make her plea in writing after seeing Trump "many times on the TV," Fatemah says. Like Trump, Bana has a penchant for tweeting—she has 362,000 followers—although hers drove home the terror in besieged Aleppo before her family was evacuated last month as government forces regained control of the city. More than 300,000 people, including 150,000 children, have been killed in the six-year civil war, reports the Guardian. Trump's position on Syria is not yet known. A White House spokesman said the president would "work with any country" committed to fighting ISIS, per the Guardian. The Pentagon, meanwhile, denied on Monday that US warplanes aided a Russian mission in Syria. Russia supports the government of President Bashar al-Assad. (Bana "befriended" JK Rowling.)

final_summary:
A violent crime spree unfolded in Kansas City on Wednesday evening, leaving a family shaken and a community searching for justice. The incidents began with a brazen armed robbery in the 8800 block of Crystal Avenue, where a man was held up while attempting to sell an Xbox he had advertised on Craigslist. The victim reported that two individuals, accompanied by a third person, approached him and demanded his property, fleeing into nearby woods with the stolen goods.

Just hours later, at approximately 7:30 pm, a similar scenario played out in the 5500 block of East 84th Street, where a Liberty couple and their 16-year-old son visited a residence to view a 2007 Hyundai Sonata listed on Craigslist. The trio met with a suspect who identified himself as "AJ" and took the vehicle for a test drive. However, upon returning to the apartment complex, two men emerged, brandishing black handguns and demanding money from the family. When the father attempted to shield his wife, the suspects opened fire, striking the 53-year-old man multiple times and shattering the 55-year-old woman's upper-right arm. Fortunately, the 16-year-old son escaped without injury.

The suspects, described as three black men in their late 20s or early 30s, fled the scene in the Hyundai, which was later recovered in the 4200 block of East 68th Street. The injured couple was rushed to the hospital, where the father underwent surgery for his severe wounds, including four gunshot wounds to his chest, stomach, and legs. The woman's arm was severely injured, but her son remained shaken but uninjured. 

According to KCTV, the family lost $3,000 during the robbery. Authorities are investigating the connection between these two incidents, and a reward has been established to aid in the capture of the perpetrators. Liberty-based Ferrellgas has donated $5,000 to Crime Stoppers, increasing the total reward to $7,000. The company's contribution was motivated by the personal connection to the injured victim, who works for Ferrellgas. Witnesses, including a couple who live in the Stone Haven Apartments, came to the aid of the injured couple, applying pressure to their wounds until medical help arrived.

Amber, a lifeguard, helped her boyfriend, J, apply pressure to the wounds, while others watched but did not assist. The public is urged to exercise caution when engaging in online transactions, particularly when meeting strangers, and to prioritize safety by choosing well-lit, populated areas or businesses. Those with information regarding the suspects are encouraged to contact the TIPS Hotline at 816-474-TIPS.

As the dad tried to keep his wife and son out of the barrage of bullets, he was shot four times in the chest, stomach, and legs, while his 55-year-old wife took a bullet to her upper right arm, which was “shattered,” reports the TV station. As the potential buyer was examining the wares, two other men reportedly arrived sporting a gun; the three men then lifted the Xbox and other items and ran into nearby woods, police say.

final_summary:
The nation's capital dominated English-language television ratings for the World Cup, with a high level of interest in the sport persisting throughout the tournament. On Monday, the Ghana-USA broadcast on ESPN garnered an 11.8 overnight rating in the Washington market, surpassing other major cities like New York (10.2), Hartford/New Haven (10.1), Boston (10.0), Columbus (8.9), and Baltimore (8.7), which rounded out the top six. This achievement marked the highest overnight rating ever for a World Cup group-round match in both Washington and Baltimore, across all networks. Notably, these ratings exclude Spanish-language broadcasts.

Through 14 matches, the region led the way for average World Cup ratings on the ESPN networks, with a rating of 5.0, trailed closely by San Francisco (4.4) and New York (4.1). The strong interest in soccer in the area was evident throughout the tournament, with the region having the highest local rating for several matches, including the Brazil-Croatia opener (5.1), Chile-Australia (3.8), and Spain-Netherlands (4.7). Additionally, the region tied for third for the Mexico-Cameroon match.

In contrast, during the 2010 World Cup final between Spain and the Netherlands, the region ranked fifth among English-language ratings, behind San Francisco, San Diego, New York, and Miami. A comparison can be made between the World Cup ratings and those of local sports teams in the area. The best-rated game in the Ovechkin era earned an 8.63 rating, while the best-rated playoff game this season earned an 8.51 rating. One game has achieved a rating above 10.0.

A tweet from John Ourand noted that despite the region's strong interest in soccer, it remains a football town, with the Washington Redskins averaging a 25.8 rating across three networks. The Ghana vs. USA telecast on ESPN averaged a 7.0 overnight rating, the highest for a men's World Cup match on either ESPN or ESPN2, according to Nielsen. The U.S. vs. Ghana match drew a record-breaking 15.9 million viewers between ESPN and Univision, setting new records for both networks. This milestone highlights the growing popularity of soccer in the country, particularly when the U.S. team is competing.

Washington, D.C., dominated the national English-language television ratings for the World Cup, with its high interest in the sport continuing through the tournament. On Monday, the Ghana-USA broadcast on ESPN earned an impressive 11.8 overnight rating in the Washington market, making it the highest number in the country. This achievement surpassed other major cities such as New York (10.2), Hartford/New Haven (10.1), Boston (10.0), Columbus (8.9), and Baltimore (8.7), which rounded out the top six.

This marked the highest overnight rating ever for a World Cup group-round match in both Washington and Baltimore, across all networks. Notably, these ratings do not account for the Spanish-language broadcasts. As of 14 matches into the tournament, D.C. led the way for average World Cup ratings on the ESPN networks, with a rating of 5.0, followed closely by San Francisco (4.4) and New York (4.1).

The strong interest in soccer in Washington, D.C., was evident throughout the tournament, with the city having the highest local rating for several matches, including the Brazil-Croatia opener (5.1), Chile-Australia (3.8), and Spain-Netherlands (4.7). D.C. also tied for third for the Mexico-Cameroon match. In contrast, during the 2010 World Cup final between Spain and the Netherlands, Washington ranked fifth among English-language ratings, behind San Francisco, San Diego, New York, and Miami.

A notable comparison can be made between the World Cup ratings and those of local sports teams in the area. For instance, the best-rated Capitals game in the Ovechkin era earned an 8.63 rating, while the best-rated Wizards playoff game this season earned an 8.51 rating. However, only one Nationals game has achieved a rating above 10.0.

On a related note, a tweet from John Ourand highlighted that despite D.C.'s strong interest in soccer, it remains a football town, with the Washington Redskins averaging a 25.8 rating across three networks. Meanwhile, the Ghana vs. USA telecast on ESPN averaged a 7.0 overnight rating, the highest for a men's World Cup match on either ESPN or ESPN2, according to Nielsen.

In terms of overall viewership, the U.S. vs. Ghana match drew a record-breaking 15.9 million viewers between ESPN and Univision, setting new records for both networks. This significant viewership milestone underscores the growing popularity of soccer in the United States, particularly when the U.S. team is competing.

The nation's capital dominated English-language television ratings for the World Cup, with a high level of interest in the sport persisting throughout the tournament. On Monday, the Ghana-USA broadcast on ESPN garnered an 11.8 overnight rating in the Washington market, surpassing other major cities like New York (10.2), Hartford/New Haven (10.1), Boston (10.0), Columbus (8.9), and Baltimore (8.7), which rounded out the top six. This achievement marked the highest overnight rating ever for a World Cup group-round match in both Washington and Baltimore, across all networks. Notably, these ratings exclude Spanish-language broadcasts.

Through 14 matches, the region led the way for average World Cup ratings on the ESPN networks, with a rating of 5.0, trailed closely by San Francisco (4.4) and New York (4.1). The strong interest in soccer in the area was evident throughout the tournament, with the region having the highest local rating for several matches, including the Brazil-Croatia opener (5.1), Chile-Australia (3.8), and Spain-Netherlands (4.7). Additionally, the region tied for third for the Mexico-Cameroon match.

In contrast, during the 2010 World Cup final between Spain and the Netherlands, the region ranked fifth among English-language ratings, behind San Francisco, San Diego, New York, and Miami. A comparison can be made between the World Cup ratings and those of local sports teams in the area. The best-rated game in the Ovechkin era earned an 8.63 rating, while the best-rated playoff game this season earned an 8.51 rating. One game has achieved a rating above 10.0.

A tweet from John Ourand noted that despite the region's strong interest in soccer, it remains a football town, with the Washington Redskins averaging a 25.8 rating across three networks. The Ghana vs. USA telecast on ESPN averaged a 7.0 overnight rating, the highest for a men's World Cup match on either ESPN or ESPN2, according to Nielsen. The U.S. vs. Ghana match drew a record-breaking 15.9 million viewers between ESPN and Univision, setting new records for both networks. This milestone highlights the growing popularity of soccer in the country, particularly when the U.S. team is competing.

reference: – ESPN and Univision have scored with their World Cup coverage: The US victory over Ghana Monday night set World Cup ratings records for both broadcasters, with a total audience of 15.9 million viewers. For ESPN, it was the highest-rated men's soccer game ever. The ratings were way ahead of the 6 million that watched hockey's Stanley Cup final on Friday night, and weren't that far behind the NBA final between the San Antonio Spurs and the Miami Heat, which pulled in 17.9 million viewers, the Wall Street Journal finds. Among English-language viewers, the World Cup so far has the highest average ratings in the Washington, DC, area, with San Francisco, Boston, New York, and Orlando rounding out the top five, the Washington Post reports.

final_summary:
A Reuters report from Seoul states that North Korea has detained a U.S. university student, Otto Frederick Warmbier, 21, from the University of Virginia, for allegedly committing a "hostile act" against the state. According to the North's official KCNA news agency, Warmbier entered the country as a tourist on a five-day New Year's trip organized by China-based Young Pioneer Tours, with the intention of "destroying the country's unity." However, KCNA claimed that the U.S. government had "tolerated and manipulated" Warmbier's actions.

Warmbier was detained at Pyongyang airport on January 2, just four days before North Korea conducted its fourth nuclear test in violation of UN sanctions. This incident marks the third time a Westerner has been detained in North Korea. The U.S. State Department acknowledged the detention but declined to provide further details, citing privacy concerns.

Young Pioneer Tours, the tour operator responsible for arranging Warmbier's trip, stated that they are in contact with the Swedish Embassy in Pyongyang, which represents U.S. interests in North Korea, and are working to secure Warmbier's release. The company also expressed concern for Warmbier's family's privacy and requested respect for their wishes during this difficult time.

Warmbier, an Echols Scholar at the University of Virginia, studying economics with a minor in global sustainability, has previously traveled to Cuba, Ireland, and Israel. Friends and family describe him as a "wonderful, warm person" who should not have to endure such treatment. North Korea has a history of detaining foreigners, often using them as leverage to extract concessions from the international community. The country has previously detained several U.S. citizens, including three Americans who were released in 2014.

The U.S. government strongly advises against traveling to North Korea, and the State Department has issued warnings about the risks associated with visiting the country. As tensions continue to escalate between North Korea and its neighbors, particularly the United States and South Korea, the international community remains concerned about the fate of Warmbier and other foreign nationals detained in the country.

The U.S. State Department acknowledged the detention but declined to provide further details, citing privacy concerns.

The company also expressed concern for Warmbier's family's privacy and requested respect for their wishes during this difficult time.

Warmbier, who is an Echols Scholar at the University of Virginia, studying economics with a minor in global sustainability, has previously traveled to Cuba, Ireland, and Israel. His detention has sparked concern among friends and family, with a friend describing him as a "wonderful, warm person" who should not have to endure such treatment.

North Korea has a history of detaining foreigners, often using them as leverage to extract concessions from the international community. The country has previously detained several U.S. citizens, including three Americans who were released in 2014. The U.S. government strongly advises against traveling to North Korea, and the State Department has issued warnings about the risks associated with visiting the country.

As tensions continue to escalate between North Korea and its neighbors, the international community remains concerned about the fate of Warmbier and other foreign nationals detained in the country.

reference: – North Korea says it has detained an American student from the University of Virginia who visited the country as part of a "New Year's Party Tour." A rep for Chinese travel company Young Pioneer Tours says undergraduate commerce student Otto Frederick Warmbier, 21, was arrested at Pyongyang airport on Jan. 2 after a five-day tourist trip. North Korea's official news agency says he visited with the "aim to destroy the country's unity" and "was caught committing a hostile act against the state," which was "tolerated and manipulated by the US government," per Reuters. The US Embassy in Seoul says it's aware of Warmbier's arrest, per CNN. The tour group says it's working with the State Department and the Swedish Embassy in Pyongyang, which represents US interests there. "We would appreciate Otto's and his family's privacy being respected and we hope his release can be secured as soon as possible," a rep tells the BBC. Reuters notes about 6,000 westerners travel to North Korea each year; the "New Year's Party Tour" Warmbier was participating in promised a fireworks display in Kim Il Sung Square. One other American citizen is being held in North Korea. Kim Dong Chul, who says he's a naturalized US citizen who used to live in Virginia, was arrested in October.

final_summary:
On Monday, a dramatic scene unfolded at a Chase Bank location on Detroit's west side when a 15-year-old boy allegedly attempted to rob the institution. According to Detroit Police Officer Dan Donakowski, the teenager walked into the bank in the 15000 block of West 7 Mile around 2 p.m., brandishing a note and threatening to detonate a bomb unless the teller handed over cash. The quick-thinking teller remained calm and pressed the button to activate the automatic doors, trapping the suspect between the inner and outer doors.

In a separate incident on the city's east side, a 16-year-old boy entered a Chase Bank location on Seven Mile Road around 2:30 p.m. and claimed to be armed with a bomb, demanding that the teller hand over all the money. The teller complied, but the suspect soon found himself stuck when he attempted to exit the building. As he reached the outermost set of doors, he discovered they were locked, and upon trying to return through the previous door, he became trapped. The teller had activated the "man-trap" security system, designed to keep potential intruders confined until law enforcement arrives. In both incidents, the suspects were eventually taken into custody by the police, with one being transported to a juvenile facility. Detroit Police Officer Jennifer Moreno reported that it's unclear if the teens had any weapons, with one possibly having only a note in their possession. Details regarding the charges against the teens remain unclear, and investigations are ongoing.

reference: – A teenage would-be bank robber in Detroit found himself imprisoned—and presumably regretting one or two life choices—within seconds of being handed money at a bank on Monday, police say. A police spokesman tells the Detroit Free Press that the 15-year-old gave a teller a note claiming he had a bomb and demanding money. As he left with the cash, the teller hit a button that trapped the youth between two sets of doors. The security system, known as a "man-trap," keeps intruders caged until police arrive, MLive reports. Police say the teen, who didn't have any explosives on him, was taken to a juvenile detention facility. (A man who stole a restaurant's tip jar ended up losing money.)

final_summary:
In the aftermath of a classmate's October suicide in Lebanon, Tenn., 16-year-old Emily Gipson, a student at Lebanon High School in Wilson County, created a powerful anti-bullying video titled "Welcome to Lebanon High School," which she posted on YouTube on January 22. The video, a free-verse poem, garnered over 700,000 views and sparked heated debate within the school community. However, instead of receiving praise for her efforts to raise awareness about bullying and the need for kindness, Gipson was met with disciplinary action – a two-day in-school suspension.

According to Principal Scott Walters, the suspension was not due to the content of the video itself, but rather because Gipson had allegedly recorded the video in a classroom without obtaining permission from a teacher. Gipson disputes this claim, stating that she had obtained permission from two coaches. Walters also expressed that the video hurt his feelings and those of the teachers, suggesting that it was the manner in which the message was conveyed that was the issue, rather than the message itself.

Gipson's grandfather and guardian, Kenneth Gipson, acknowledged that his granddaughter may have broken school rules by recording the video on school grounds, but emphasized that he is proud of her for speaking out against bullying, a pressing concern in today's society, particularly in schools. He believes that the video's message is valuable and necessary, even if it was delivered in a way that may have been perceived as confrontational.

The incident highlights the complexities surrounding freedom of expression and the challenges faced by educators in balancing the need to protect students' rights with the responsibility to maintain order and discipline within the school environment. As Gipson herself noted in a subsequent video, "This is not my school, this is everyone's school, this is a national problem." Her words serve as a poignant reminder of the need for ongoing dialogue and reform in addressing the pervasive issue of bullying in American schools.

In the aftermath of a classmate's October suicide in Lebanon, Tenn., 16-year-old Emily Gipson, a student at Lebanon High School in Wilson County, created a powerful anti-bullying video titled "Welcome to Lebanon High School," which she posted on YouTube on January 22. The video, a free-verse poem, garnered over 700,000 views and sparked heated debate within the school community. However, instead of receiving praise for her efforts to raise awareness about bullying and the need for kindness, Gipson was met with disciplinary action – a two-day in-school suspension.

According to Principal Scott Walters, the suspension was not due to the content of the video itself, but rather because Gipson had allegedly recorded the video in a classroom without obtaining permission from a teacher. Gipson disputes this claim, stating that she had obtained permission from two coaches. Walters also expressed that the video hurt his feelings and those of the teachers, suggesting that it was the manner in which the message was conveyed that was the issue, rather than the message itself.

Gipson's grandfather and guardian, Kenneth Gipson, acknowledged that his granddaughter may have broken school rules by recording the video on school grounds, but emphasized that he is proud of her for speaking out against bullying, a pressing concern in today's society, particularly in schools. He believes that the video's message is valuable and necessary, even if it was delivered in a way that may have been perceived as confrontational.

The incident highlights the complexities surrounding freedom of expression and the challenges faced by educators in balancing the need to protect students' rights with the responsibility to maintain order and discipline within the school environment. reference: – "We're expected to forgive the bullies because the authorities are sure they didn't mean it," Emily Gipson says in a YouTube video posted Jan. 22. "Sometimes I wonder how many kids it takes dying to make a difference." The 16-year-old sophomore at Tennessee's Lebanon High School made the video after a classmate's apparent suicide, the AP reports. The video went viral—surpassing 800,000 views—and Gipson has been hit with a two-day suspension. In the video, she says her school is an "emotional prison" and a place "where creativity is put down, where the people who make fun of others never get punished because 'There's no proof,' or 'There's nothing we can do about it,' or, my favorite, 'Kids will be kids.'" Principal Scott Walters says his feelings and those of teachers were hurt by the video, but that's not why Gipson was suspended. Gipson says the school administration accused her of "trying to incite violence," but Walters says she was suspended for filming in a classroom after school without the teacher's permission or knowledge, the Tennessean reports. Gipson says she had permission from two coaches, and in a statement quoted by CNN, Wilson County Schools spokesperson Jennifer Johnson confirms that Gipson asked a coach if she could use the classroom only to have the coach later be "mortified by the nature of her message." Johnson adds that it's "patently false" to say Gipson was punished for inciting violence and there's "no evidence whatsoever" that the student who died was bullied, despite the claims of other students. Gipson says she wanted "to be a voice for as many people as possible" and ends the video by imploring others "do not be the bully ... do not be the one that takes their own life."

final_summary:
On June 2, 2017, the Rock am Ring music festival in Nuerburg, Germany, was abruptly evacuated due to concerns of a potential terrorist threat. The decision to evacuate the venue, which was hosting approximately 90,000 attendees, was made by the organizers in collaboration with local authorities in Berlin after receiving concrete indications from the Koblenz police department. According to reports from AFP, the police had informed the organizers that a possible terrorist threat could not be ruled out, prompting them to shut down the festival for the day.

The evacuation came just hours before the scheduled performance of the German band Rammstein, which was set to headline the event on Friday evening. As a precautionary measure, the police had already increased security personnel to 1,200 officers, including an additional 1,200 staff, in response to the recent Manchester bombing that occurred on May 22, which claimed the lives of 22 people. 

The suspected terrorist threat was linked to three individuals who were reportedly hired to set up security barriers at the venue. These individuals, believed to be members of a Salafist group from the neighboring state of Hesse, were later detained by the police but released without charges on Saturday. One of the suspects, described as a non-German individual with ties to the Islamist terror network, had allegedly gained access to the backstage areas of the festival. 

Photographer Thomas Frey captured images of festival-goers leaving the venue following the evacuation alert. Police spokesman Wolfgang Fromm stated that one person who is not of German origin and is known to the police as having links to the Islamist terror network had access to the backstage areas.

The three-day Rock am Ring festival, which was initially scheduled to conclude on Sunday, was temporarily suspended due to the security concerns. However, after a series of intensive searches across the site, the fears of an imminent danger were not confirmed. The police gave the go-ahead for the festival to resume, allowing live music to start again at 1:30 pm (1130 GMT) on Saturday. 

Regional Interior Minister Roger Lewentz defended the decision to evacuate the festival, stating, "We cannot take risks." The incident comes less than two weeks after a terrorist attack on a Christmas market in Berlin on December 19, where 11 people lost their lives and dozens were wounded. In a similar vein, the region remains on high alert following the Manchester bombing. 

In a statement, the festival organizers expressed gratitude for the cooperation between the police and the public, ensuring a safe and controlled exit process for the attendees.

On June 2, 2017, the Rock am Ring music festival in Nuerburg, Germany, was abruptly evacuated due to concerns of a potential terrorist threat. The decision to evacuate the venue, which was hosting approximately 90,000 attendees, was made by the organizers in collaboration with local authorities in Berlin after receiving concrete indications from the Koblenz police department. According to reports from AFP, the police had informed the organizers that a possible terrorist threat could not be ruled out, prompting them to shut down the festival for the day.

The evacuation came just hours before the scheduled performance of the German band Rammstein, which was set to headline the event on Friday evening. As a precautionary measure, the police had already increased security personnel to 1,200 officers, including an additional 1,200 staff, in response to the recent Manchester bombing that occurred on May 22, which claimed the lives of 22 people. 

The suspected terrorist threat was linked to three individuals who were reportedly hired to set up security barriers at the venue. These individuals, believed to be members of a Salafist group from the neighboring state of Hesse, were later detained by the police but released without charges on Saturday. One of the suspects, described as a non-German individual with ties to the Islamist terror network, had allegedly gained access to the backstage areas of the festival. 

Photographer Thomas Frey captured images of festival-goers leaving the venue following the evacuation alert. Police spokesman Wolfgang Fromm stated that one person who is not of German origin and is known to the police as having links to the Islamist terror network had access to the backstage areas.

The three-day Rock am Ring festival, which was initially scheduled to conclude on Sunday, was temporarily suspended due to the security concerns. However, after a series of intensive searches across the site, the fears of an imminent danger were not confirmed. The police gave the go-ahead for the festival to resume, allowing live music to start again at 1:30 pm (1130 GMT) on Saturday. 

Regional Interior Minister Roger Lewentz defended the decision to evacuate the festival, stating, "We cannot take risks." The incident comes less than two weeks after a terrorist attack on a Christmas market in Berlin on December 19, where 11 people lost their lives and dozens were wounded. In a similar vein, the region remains on high alert following the Manchester bombing. 

In a statement, the festival organizers expressed gratitude for the cooperation between the police and the public, ensuring a safe and controlled exit process for the attendees.

reference: – A "possible terrorist threat" shut down Rock am Ring, one of Germany's biggest music festivals, Friday near Nuerburg, AFP reports. Police say they have "concrete elements, in the light of which a possible terrorist threat cannot be ruled out." But no other details were released. According to the Guardian, thousands of people were asked to leave the festival calmly "in order to help police investigations." It's unclear how many people were in attendance Friday, but about 90,000 were expected over the three-day festival. Organizers hoped the festival would resume Saturday. Rock am Ring had already increased security, adding 1,200 additional staff, after the recent Manchester bombing at an Ariana Grande concert. (Rock am Ring was also suspended last year after dozens were injured by lightning.)

final_summary:
Following the tragic mass shootings that occurred on Friday in Pike County, Ohio, leaving eight members of the Rhoden family dead, officials are continuing to investigate the motives behind the brutal killings. According to sources, a Facebook threat targeting one of the victims, Christopher Rhoden Jr., is among the evidence being examined by investigators. 

Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine confirmed that his office is aware of the threat and stated that analyzing social media accounts is essential in any murder investigation. DeWine emphasized that his team is working closely with local authorities to gather information. Pike County Sheriff Charles Reader echoed DeWine's sentiments, noting that the family was targeted and warning the surviving members to take precautions and arm themselves. Reader expressed gratitude to fellow Ohio sheriffs for their support and assistance in the investigation.

The investigation has revealed that marijuana grow operations were discovered at three of the four crime scene locations, with estimates suggesting approximately 200 plants were found, indicating a commercial-scale operation. Although it remains unclear whether the grow operations are connected to the shootings, they will be a significant focus of the investigation. Law enforcement is exploring various possibilities, including a Mexican drug cartel connection or a family feud, as potential motives behind the killings.

On Sunday, DeWine and Reader held a joint press conference, during which they highlighted the sophistication of the operation and the need for caution. The victims, including 40-year-old Christopher Rhoden Sr., his 16-year-old son Christopher Rhoden Jr., 44-year-old Kenneth Rhoden, 38-year-old Gary Rhoden, 37-year-old Dana Rhoden, 20-year-old Clarence "Frankie" Rhoden, 20-year-old Hannah Gilley, and 19-year-old Hanna Rhoden, were found shot in the head at four separate locations near Piketon, Ohio, on Friday. Autopsies were completed over the weekend, and authorities have collected over 30 statements from individuals in an effort to identify the perpetrator or perpetrators.

Since the tragic events unfolded on Friday, residents have expressed fear and concern for their safety. A $25,000 reward has been offered for information leading to the capture and conviction of the killer or killers. On Saturday, Pastor Phil Fulton described the Rhoden family as close-knit and hardworking, and expressed the community's desire to provide support and comfort to the grieving family. As the community mourns the loss of the Rhoden family, residents are coming together to offer condolences and support. Those with information regarding the case are urged to contact the Pike County Sheriff's Office or the BCI tip line at 1-855-BCI-OHIO (224-6446).

As the community grapples with the tragedy, residents expressed fear and concern for their safety.
