reference: – A new development in Ohio: One of the eight family members killed in a horrific mass slaying had already been threatened on Facebook, CBS News reports. The threat was directed at Christopher Rhoden Jr., who at 16 was the youngest of the victims. "I'm aware of the Facebook threat," says Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine. "Every piece of information is valuable and our investigators are certainly taking that into consideration." Now Pike County Sheriff Charles Reader has told surviving Rhoden family members to be careful. "I cautioned them, told them we will be there," Reader says. "I told [them] to be armed." One surviving family member says the Rhodens are "torn up" about the killings. "And you know what I mean, kinda freaked out because we don't know," says the relative, who asked not to be identified. "There's still killers on the loose, or killer." The slayings happened in four rural homes near Piketon, Ohio, a town of about 2,200 where the Dogwood Festival on Sunday had a smaller-then-usual turnout. "People are scared to come out," says a resident. "What if that person who shot all those people is running around here." Now autopsies of all eight victims have been completed, News Net 5 reports, and a chart of the Rhoden family relationships can be seen here. (Marijuana-growing was found at three of the four murder sites.)

final_summary:
In the United States, the 2012 presidential election marked the beginning of a new cycle of speculation about the country's future leadership. With the outcome of the election settled, attention shifted towards the 2016 presidential election, where both major parties faced significant decisions regarding their future leadership. 

For the Democratic Party, a crucial question arose: what would Hillary Clinton do? If she chose not to run, several prominent figures emerged as potential candidates, including Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley, a relatively unknown figure within the party, and Vice President Joe Biden, whose status quo candidacy raised concerns about continuity. Other notable Democrats considered for the nomination included Antonio Villaraigosa, the mayor of Los Angeles, who enjoyed strong support within the Latino community, and Senator Kirsten Gillibrand of New York, a formidable figure due to her popularity in a large state and her importance as a woman candidate. Additionally, New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, who came from a key state with access to party loyalists and financial resources, was also mentioned as a possible contender.

On the Republican side, the path forward appeared more treacherous, with the party set to undergo a period of internal conflict between various factions, including conservatives, establishment figures, and populists. Several prominent Republicans were mentioned as potential candidates, including Jeb Bush, the former Florida governor, who boasted a strong name and financial backing. Other notable Republicans included Paul Ryan, a favorite among deficit hawks and a standout performer on the Romney campaign, Florida Senator Marco Rubio, a rising star who could help the party tap into the Latino vote, and New Jersey Governor Chris Christie, who impressed with his leadership during Hurricane Sandy and his tough-talking style.

The possibility of an unknown candidate emerging from the 2012 election also remained a factor, as it had in previous cycles. Meanwhile, speculation about the 2016 election generated little interest abroad, except perhaps among bookmakers such as Paddy Power, which offered early odds on the outcome, favoring Hillary Clinton and Paul Ryan, while listing Donald Trump as a long shot at 100 to 1.

In the immediate aftermath of the 2012 election, several individuals stood out as potential candidates for the 2016 presidency. On the Democratic side, Vice President Joe Biden, a seasoned politician with decades of experience in the Senate and as the nation's second-in-command, hinted at a possible run, although his tendency to make gaffes raised concerns about his electability. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton, a uniquely accomplished figure who had navigated a successful transition from First Lady to Senator to Secretary of State, was widely regarded as the top Democratic contender, despite her repeated denials of interest in seeking the presidency. New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, a low-key figure with a high-profile job, was believed by insiders to harbor ambitions for the White House, and would likely step up to the plate if Hillary Clinton declined to run.

On the Republican side, Wisconsin Congressman Paul Ryan, who had performed impressively on the 2012 campaign trail, was seen as a strong contender, along with New Jersey Governor Chris Christie, who had burnished his bipartisan credentials during the Hurricane Sandy crisis, and Florida Senator Marco Rubio, a charismatic young leader who embodied the party's desire to broaden its appeal to a more diverse electorate.

reference: – And so it begins: Speculation about Election 2016 is already running rampant, mere hours after President Obama was re-elected. Some of the frontrunners for a presidential run next time around, courtesy of ABC News and US News & World Report: Hillary Clinton: Not surprisingly, she's at the forefront of many minds right now ("Hillary2016" was trending on Twitter early today, the Hill notes), and is widely considered the No. 1 Democrat contender. Of course, she also keeps insisting she won't run. Joe Biden: The world took notice when he said yesterday that he'd probably vote for himself again someday—but he later joked that he just meant he'd be running for county councilman in the future. Andrew Cuomo: The New York governor has access to lots of money, and his liberal stance on social issues combined with a more fiscally conservative stance could make him a formidable contender. But don't expect him to run if Hillary does; he served under Bill Clinton and is loyal to the family. Other possible Democrats: Maryland Gov. Martin O'Malley, Los Angeles Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa, and Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand. Paul Ryan: He fared well as Romney's running mate and got himself re-elected to boot, so clearly he's a top contender. Marco Rubio: The charismatic Florida senator could help the GOP increase its appeal to Latino voters. Chris Christie: His tough talk has made him quite popular, and he's seen as more of a bipartisan figure now that he's praised the federal response to Hurricane Sandy. Other possible Republicans: Jeb Bush, Mike Huckabee, plus pretty much anyone who ran in the primary this time around. But in the International Herald Tribune, Harvey Morris has just one piece of advice: "Take a breath, America! Give yourselves time to recover from what seemed the longest and certainly was the most expensive campaign in US political history before starting on the next one."

final_summary:
Richard Bootzin, a psychologist, introduced the concept of a "stimulus-control" approach to improve sleep quality in 1972. This method emphasized training the brain to associate the bed with sleep and sex alone, utilizing operant conditioning principles. However, the inclusion of sex as a permissible activity in the bed seemed counterintuitive to the goal of creating a sleep sanctuary.

Last year, Charles Czeisler, a professor of sleep medicine at Harvard Medical School, discovered that around 90 percent of Americans use electronic devices within an hour before bedtime. His initial theory was that overstimulation caused by these devices impeded the natural withdrawal of sympathetic nervous system activity necessary for sleep onset. Czeisler and colleagues published their findings in the Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine in December 2013, suggesting that the detrimental effects of screens on sleep might be attributed to both cognitive stimulation and light exposure from technological devices.

A recent study led by Czeisler and Anne-Marie Chang, an associate neuroscientist at Harvard Medical School, aimed to determine the extent to which light from devices affects sleep patterns. The researchers recruited 12 participants who stayed in a monitoring unit at Boston's Brigham and Women's Hospital for two weeks. Participants were divided into two groups: one read on an iPad for four hours before bedtime, while the other read printed books in dim light. The groups then switched roles after a week.

The results showed that individuals who read on the iPad had lower melatonin levels, took longer to fall asleep, and spent less time in the REM phase of sleep compared to those who read printed books. Blood tests revealed that the brain's melatonin secretion was delayed by an hour and a half on iPad nights. Similar light-emission patterns were observed in other devices, including tablets, phones, and laptops, leading the researchers to conclude that these devices can have profound biological effects on sleep.

Czeisler emphasized that the study's findings are not limited to iPads and that other devices emitting similar blue light can have similar effects on sleep. He expressed concern that the widespread use of electronic devices before bedtime could lead to long-term health consequences, such as increased cancer risk, obesity, and diabetes. The study's results highlight the need for reevaluating the impact of electronic devices on human health and potentially implementing stricter regulations for their development and use.
anchors: The Journal Of Clinical Sleep Medicine

history.draft:
Richard Bootzin, a psychologist, introduced the concept of a "stimulus-control" approach to improve sleep quality in 1972. This method emphasized training the brain to associate the bed with sleep and sex alone, utilizing operant conditioning principles. However, the inclusion of sex as a permissible activity in the bed seemed counterintuitive to the goal of creating a sleep sanctuary.

Last year, Charles Czeisler, a professor of sleep medicine at Harvard Medical School, discovered that around 90 percent of Americans use electronic devices within an hour before bedtime. His initial theory was that overstimulation caused by these devices impeded the natural withdrawal of sympathetic nervous system activity necessary for sleep onset. Czeisler and colleagues published their findings in the Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine in December 2013, suggesting that the detrimental effects of screens on sleep might be attributed to both cognitive stimulation and light exposure from technological devices.

A recent study led by Czeisler and Anne-Marie Chang, an associate neuroscientist at Harvard Medical School, aimed to determine the extent to which light from devices affects sleep patterns. The researchers recruited 12 participants who stayed in a monitoring unit at Boston's Brigham and Women's Hospital for two weeks. Participants were divided into two groups: one read on an iPad for four hours before bedtime, while the other read printed books in dim light. The groups then switched roles after a week.

The results showed that individuals who read on the iPad had lower melatonin levels, took longer to fall asleep, and spent less time in the REM phase of sleep compared to those who read printed books. Blood tests revealed that the brain's melatonin secretion was delayed by an hour and a half on iPad nights. Similar light-emission patterns were observed in other devices, including tablets, phones, and laptops, leading the researchers to conclude that these devices can have profound biological effects on sleep.

Czeisler emphasized that the study's findings are not limited to iPads and that other devices emitting similar blue light can have similar effects on sleep. He expressed concern that the widespread use of electronic devices before bedtime could lead to long-term health consequences, such as increased cancer risk, obesity, and diabetes. The study's results highlight the need for reevaluating the impact of electronic devices on human health and potentially implementing stricter regulations for their development and use.

reference: – Your iPad or smartphone could well be hurting your sleep patterns, even if you're getting in a full eight hours of shuteye, researchers say—and the effects could be long-term. Over a five-night period, researchers in Boston had some subjects read for four hours a night on an iPad; others did their reading on printed books, with the lights low, the Washington Post reports. Among members of the iPad group, levels of melatonin, a chemical behind sleepiness, decreased. It took them longer to drift off by an average of 10 minutes, the Wall Street Journal reports. And once they did, their periods of rapid eye-movement sleep were shorter. The effects weren't limited to the nighttime. Their circadian rhythms were affected, and the next day—even if they'd slept eight hours—they reported being more tired. And in the long term, reduced melatonin may boost the risk of various forms of cancer (prompting the rather frightening headline "Reading an iPad in Bed May Increase Cancer Risk," the Atlantic notes). At issue is what's known as "blue light," the kind often given off by backlit gadgets, the Journal adds. "We introduce these devices that have medical and biological effects without requiring any health studies on their impact ... They don't have to go through any evaluation like a drug would, for safety and efficacy," says a researcher. "I think it's time to rethink that." (As far as sleep goes, here's your "optimal" amount.)

final_summary:
Jonas Bevacqua, co-founder and creative director of the Irvine-based clothing company LRG, was found dead in his home on Bern Drive in Laguna Beach on Monday morning. The Orange County Sheriff's Office confirmed the death, stating that the cause remains undetermined pending further investigation. The Laguna Beach Police Department reported that the death appears to be non-suspicious.

LRG, short for Lifted Research Group, was a prominent urban-style clothing brand that supported and was supported by a community of musicians, artists, and athletes who wore its clothing. Celebrities such as Kobe Bryant, Reggie Bush, Kanye West, John Legend, Quentin Tarantino, and Tom Arnold were among those who donned LRG apparel. The company's success was marked by significant growth, with Entrepreneur magazine reporting sales of $5 million in 2002 and $150 million in 2006, ranking it fifth on the publication's list of the 500 fastest-growing companies.

Bevacqua's vision for LRG was rooted in his diverse upbringing, having grown up in a multicultural household in Long Beach before moving to Laguna Beach in high school. This exposure to various cultures and lifestyles influenced his approach to fashion, which aimed to bridge the gaps between different communities. As he explained in a 2009 interview with the Orange County Register, "I grew up in a pretty unique environment and was exposed to a lot of different things... I didn't feel there was a clothing company to bridge the gap among all these different things that we were into – that spoke for that melting pot of what was going on. That's what LRG was all about."

Bevacqua's entrepreneurial journey began after dropping out of college, where he spent two semesters before drifting through California, eventually settling in Laguna Beach to launch LRG in 1999. With the help of his adoptive father, Robert Wright, a college-educated individual with experience in the surfwear industry, and investors Charlie Moothart and Ronnie Ghenender, Bevacqua brought his designs to life. The company's early days saw Bevacqua working as a valet and DJ, using his earnings to fund his passion project. LRG's growth was fueled by innovative designs that conveyed messages of wit, humor, and social awareness, including the iconic phrase "Adopt Children, Not Style," which held personal significance for Bevacqua.

On Tuesday, a statement on LRG's website described Bevacqua as a "friend, partner, father, and mentor" who would be deeply missed. As LRG expanded, it branched out beyond clothing to include electronics, music, and other products, solidifying its position as a "creative lifestyle" company. Bevacqua's leadership style was characterized by a relaxed, casual atmosphere, where employees were encouraged to wear whatever they felt comfortable in, rather than adhering to traditional corporate attire. The company's headquarters featured a basketball court and skateboard facilities, reflecting Bevacqua's love for action sports.

In addition to his professional achievements, Bevacqua was a devoted family man, leaving behind his fiancée, son, seven siblings, and his parents. The news of his passing sent shockwaves through the fashion world, with fans and colleagues paying tribute to his legacy on social media.

reference: – The co-founder of the hugely popular hip-hop and skateboarding clothing brand LRG has been found dead in his Laguna Beach home. Jonas Bevacqua was 34. There was no obvious cause of death, and an autopsy will be conducted, AP reports. LRG, which the designer founded when he was barely out of his teens, branched out from clothing into music and other items, which supported up-and-coming recording artists. Bevacqua, who grew up in a multi-racial California family, said he founded LRG because no other company was creating clothing that reflected his and co-founder Robert Wright's interest in surfing, skateboarding, and hip-hop. "I grew up in a pretty unique environment and was exposed to a lot of different things," Bevacqua told the Orange County Register in 2009. "I didn't feel there was a clothing company to bridge the gap among all these different things that we were into—that spoke for that melting pot of what was going on. That's what LRG was all about."

final_summary:
Donald Trump has nominated Rick Perry, the former Texas governor, to lead the Department of Energy, an agency he once vowed to abolish and famously forgot the name of during a 2011 presidential debate. Perry, a climate skeptic and former 2016 presidential primary rival, is viewed by conservatives as having management experience and a willingness to question the agency's status quo. 

Perry's nomination comes at a time of heightened scrutiny for the Department of Energy, which has a $29.6 billion budget and over 100,000 federal employees and contractors spread across the country. The agency plays a crucial role in the nation's nuclear security, energy research, and climate initiatives, including the Obama administration's nuclear deal with Iran. 

Conservatives have long criticized the Department of Energy's programs, including its fossil and efficiency offices, which they see as unnecessary and market-distorting schemes. Perry, who served as governor of Texas for 14 years, has a history of advocating for energy independence and reducing regulations on the oil industry. 

In 2011, Perry called for eliminating the Department of Energy as part of his plan to shrink the federal government, but later clarified that the agency he meant to abolish was the Department of Energy. During his presidential campaigns, Perry repeatedly called for "energy independence" and a rollback of regulations from the Interior Department and the EPA. 

Perry's nomination has sparked alarm from environmental groups, who fear he will shift the department away from renewable energy and toward fossil fuels. As governor, Perry questioned the scientific consensus on climate change, comparing the minority of scientists who challenge this assumption to 17th-century astronomer Galileo, who was persecuted by the Catholic Church. 

Perry has also been a vocal supporter of the Dakota Access Pipeline, which has faced intense opposition from activists who claim it would disturb Native American burial grounds and pollute the drinking water of the nearby Indian reservation. Perry sits on the board of Energy Transfer Partners, the firm behind the pipeline, and has received nearly $237,000 in cash and shares from the company last year. 

If confirmed, Perry would oversee the Department of Energy, which has undergone significant changes since its creation in 1977 under President Jimmy Carter. The agency has shifted from focusing on weapons maintenance and regulations to prioritizing climate change mitigation and renewable energy. Perry's lack of experience in these areas has raised concerns among experts, who note that he would be walking into an agency where many career civil servants are wary of him due to his past pledge to abolish the department. 

The Department of Energy has also been at the forefront of the Obama administration's climate agenda, including the Iran nuclear deal and the promotion of renewable energy. Trump's transition team has requested the names of employees who worked on these initiatives, sparking fears of retaliation against career civil servants. 

Perry's nomination has been met with skepticism by lawmakers, including Rep. Frank Pallone, the top Democrat on the House Energy and Commerce Committee, who called it "defies all logic" given Perry's previous interest in dismantling the agency. The nomination has also raised questions about potential conflicts of interest, given Perry's ties to the fossil fuel industry. 

The Senate confirmation process for Perry's nomination is expected to be contentious, with many Democrats expressing concerns about his views on climate change and his potential impact on the Department of Energy.

final_summary:
Prime Minister David Cameron is facing a high-stakes battle for his political survival as the General Election looms, with just 18 weeks to convince voters that he is the right leader to steer Britain through uncertain times. In an exclusive interview with The Mail on Sunday, Cameron outlined his vision for the country's future, emphasizing the need for economic stability and warning that a Labour government would lead to "chaos." He vowed to introduce reforming legislation on education and welfare within the first 50 days of a new term, if re-elected, and promised to "hit the ground running and hard."

During the Christmas break, Cameron spent time at his Oxfordshire home, where he indulged in DIY activities, including unblocking drains and cooking meals for his family. His wife, Samantha, has been a constant source of support throughout his tenure as Prime Minister. The couple has been married since 2000 and has three children together. Cameron acknowledged that the upcoming election will be a defining moment for Britain's future, warning that a Labour government would lead to economic disaster, citing the party's plans to increase borrowing and spending, which would result in £13.5 billion in extra interest payments.

Cameron's relationship with US President Barack Obama has been a highlight of his premiership, with the two leaders developing a close bond that extends beyond formal diplomatic channels. Obama has taken to calling Cameron "bro," a term that has raised eyebrows among some observers, although the Prime Minister sees it as a sign of their strong friendship. The two leaders have found common ground on several issues, including their shared commitment to tackling terrorism and promoting global trade.

In the run-up to the election, Cameron has been crisscrossing the country, visiting key battlegrounds such as Cornwall and Yorkshire, where he has been touting his party's message of economic competence and stability. His Chancellor, George Osborne, has been campaigning in Twickenham, where he faced off against Liberal Democrat leader Vince Cable. Meanwhile, Labour leader Ed Miliband has launched his party's campaign, focusing on the NHS and accusing the Tories of being reckless with the country's finances.

As the election heats up, Cameron's leadership style has come under scrutiny, with some critics questioning his ability to connect with working-class voters. However, the Prime Minister remains confident in his abilities, pointing to his record on issues such as education and welfare reform. He has also been praised for his willingness to listen to opposing views, including those of his closest allies, such as Boris Johnson, whom he has authorized to return to the Cabinet after the election.

Despite the challenges ahead, Cameron remains resolute, insisting that he is committed to serving a full five-year term as Prime Minister. His wife, Samantha, has been a rock of support throughout his career, offering sage advice on matters ranging from hostage-rescue operations to domestic policy. Together, the couple has navigated the complexities of high office, and their partnership has become a hallmark of the Prime Minister's tenure.

As the election approaches, Cameron's relationship with Europe will come under intense scrutiny, particularly in light of his pledge to renegotiate the UK's membership terms if he fails to secure concessions from EU leaders. German Chancellor Angela Merkel has signaled her opposition to any attempts to restrict freedom of movement, a key demand of the Prime Minister. The outcome of these negotiations will have significant implications for Britain's future, and Cameron's determination to secure a better deal has sparked debate among his own party members.

Throughout the campaign, Cameron has been keen to emphasize his commitment to social justice, including his support for gay marriage, which he sees as a fundamental human right. He has also spoken out against the idea of a wealth tax, which he believes would be counterproductive and unfair to hardworking individuals. In London, Cameron has been touting his party's message of economic competence and stability, highlighting the importance of reducing the national debt and promoting economic growth. As the election enters its final stretch, Cameron's message will face stiff competition from Labour's promises of greater equality and social justice.

reference: – The "special relationship" between the US and the UK is still back-slappingly good, according to David Cameron. President Obama has said "the special relationship is stronger than it has ever been privately and in public and I agree," the British prime minister tells the Daily Mail, revealing that Obama sometimes calls him "bro" when they talk on the phone. The term is more commonly associated with frat boys than world leaders, but the Independent has decided it's still "a step forward" from George W. Bush's "condescending" greeting for Tony Blair: "Yo, Blair!" In the Mail interview, Cameron, who faces a tough election battle in May, also discusses gay marriage—he's proud of introducing the legislation and says he will probably attend a same-sex wedding soon—and his own marriage. He says Samantha Cameron is the first person he turns to for advice on difficult decisions like sending in special forces to rescue hostages. "If she'd organized Napoleon’s march on Moscow, they would have reached Vladivostok in good time," he says.

final_summary:
Susan Schneider Williams, artist and wife of the late Robin Williams, shares a poignant account of her husband's struggle with Lewy Body Disease (LBD), a lesser-known yet debilitating neurodegenerative disorder affecting approximately 1.5 million Americans. In a heartfelt letter, she recounts the harrowing experience of watching her beloved partner succumb to the disease, which ravaged his brain, causing a cascade of symptoms that ultimately led to his untimely death in 2014.

Robin's journey with LBD began in late October 2013, when he started experiencing uncharacteristic symptoms, including constipation, urinary difficulties, heartburn, sleeplessness, and a poor sense of smell. Initially, these issues were dismissed as unrelated, but as the months progressed, his anxiety and fear spiked, culminating in a panic attack during a film shoot in Vancouver. Despite undergoing various tests and treatments, including psychotherapy and medication, Robin's condition worsened, with symptoms such as paranoia, delusions, insomnia, and memory loss becoming increasingly pronounced.

As the disease progressed, Robin's behavior became erratic, and he struggled to remember lines for his scenes, a stark contrast to his impressive performance in the Broadway production "Bengal Tiger at the Baghdad Zoo." His medication regimen was adjusted multiple times, but the effects were short-lived, and he continued to deteriorate. The couple's sleep deficit grew, exacerbating the situation, and they were advised to sleep apart to mitigate the impact.

For seven years, Robin and Susan had shared a deep connection, built on trust, love, and open communication. They would regularly review their days, discussing their joys, fears, and concerns, creating a strong foundation that helped them navigate life's challenges. However, as LBD took hold, this bond was put to the test, and Susan found herself powerless to ease her husband's suffering.

In the months leading up to his death, Robin's condition accelerated, with his left-hand tremor becoming continuous, and he began to exhibit a parkinsonian mask, a slow gait, and difficulty finding words. His cognitive functions declined, and he became trapped in a cycle of fear and anxiety, which he desperately sought to escape. In a poignant moment, he expressed his desire to "reboot his brain," highlighting the desperation and frustration he felt as the disease ravaged his mind.

After Robin's passing, an autopsy revealed that he suffered from diffuse Lewy Body Disease, a pathology characterized by the widespread presence of Lewy bodies – abnormal protein clumps within brain neurons. This diagnosis shed light on the complexity of the disease, which is often misdiagnosed or mistaken for other conditions. Susan Schneider Williams' tireless efforts to understand LBD led her to join the American Brain Foundation, where she currently serves on the Board of Directors.

Throughout her journey, Susan has come to realize that LBD is a disease that affects not just the individual, but also their loved ones. She has dedicated herself to raising awareness about the disease and advocating for greater understanding and support. Through her experiences, Susan implores researchers and scientists to continue their pursuit of a cure for LBD and other brain diseases, emphasizing that every step forward brings hope for those affected by these conditions. Her story serves as a testament to the importance of awareness, education, and support for individuals and families navigating the complexities of neurodegenerative disorders.

About 40% of dopamine-producing neurons were lost in Robin's brain, and nearly every neuron was affected by Lewy bodies. The sheer extent of the damage was staggering, leaving Susan and the medical team stunned. In the end, it was not the lack of a diagnosis that contributed to Robin's demise, but rather the absence of a cure for the disease. Susan's story highlights the urgent need for further research and development of effective treatments for LBD and other brain diseases.

Three months after Robin's death, the autopsy report confirmed the extent of the damage, revealing a diffuse Lewy Body Disease pathology that had ravaged his brain. Susan's journey has taken her to the forefront of the fight against LBD, and she remains committed to ensuring that her husband's legacy lives on through her advocacy and support for those affected by this devastating disease.

reference: – In what Discover calls a "unique and touching paper" published in the journal Neurology Thursday, Robin Williams' widow shares the heartbreaking story of her husband's final 10 months and asks neurologists not to give up in their quest toward brain disease cures. Susan Schneider Williams refers to Lewy Body disease, the neurodegenerative disorder that ultimately led to Williams' suicide in August 2014, as "the terrorist inside my husband's brain." She describes how, toward the end of his life, "Robin was losing his mind and he was aware of it." He was struggling with paranoia, anxiety, delusions, insomnia, and other symptoms both physical and mental, and for months, he and his wife could get no answers about what was happening to him. He was ultimately diagnosed with Parkinson's disease, but as his symptoms continued to worsen, he grew "weary" and it felt like they were both "drowning." Then, ultimately, he took his own life. It was only after his death that an autopsy revealed Williams actually had Lewy Body disease—his clinical symptoms mirrored Parkinson's, but his brain pathology showed that almost all of the neurons throughout his brain and brainstem had been besieged by Lewy bodies, Schneider Williams writes. When she found out, she wasn't surprised: "The mere fact that something had invaded nearly every region of my husband's brain made perfect sense to me," she writes. She has spent the time since Williams' death learning all she can about Lewy Body disease, for which there is no cure, and she now serves on the Board of Directors for the American Brain Foundation. Her full piece is worth a read.

final_summary:
Frank Romano, a distraught pet owner, is desperately searching for his 6-year-old brindle and white Bull Terrier, Ty, after the dog mysteriously disappeared during a flight from Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) to Tampa on Friday night. According to Romano, Ty was traveling in a specially designed kennel purchased from PetCo, which was placed in the cargo area of the Delta Airlines flight. However, when Romano boarded the plane with his family, a Delta employee informed him that Ty had escaped from the kennel approximately an hour prior, claiming the dog had chewed through the metal door and hard plastic walls.

Romano expressed skepticism about the explanation, citing the durability of the kennel and questioning why the employee did not notify him immediately about the incident. Furthermore, he was assured that the empty kennel would be available for collection at baggage claim, but it never materialized. The next day, Romano's family flew to Tampa as planned, but he continued to search for Ty, contacting Delta multiple times without receiving any concrete answers or updates on the dog's whereabouts.

This incident is not an isolated case; Delta Airlines has a history of mishandling pets in their care. A Change.org petition with over 2,000 signatures demands accountability from the airline due to numerous instances of lost or injured animals. One such case involved a New York man who attempted to transport a puppy from Alabama to New York via Delta in 2011, only to have the dog temporarily misplaced in Atlanta. Another instance in 2011 saw a German Shepherd, destined for Germany, escape in Atlanta and ultimately die after being struck by a vehicle.

In 2010, Delta experienced a series of blunders, including misrouting two children between Boston and Cleveland, losing a dog en route from Mexico to Canada, and failing to provide adequate care for a puppy that arrived in California instead of Maine. The airline's handling of these incidents has sparked widespread criticism, with some owners receiving inadequate compensation or assistance. Delta's response to these incidents has been inconsistent, with the airline initially providing vague explanations and eventually offering refunds or vouchers as a form of restitution.

A report by LAist highlighted the airline's struggles with pet transportation, citing multiple cases of lost or injured animals. In 2010, a German Shepherd mix named Nala was lost during a flight from LAX to Germany, with the airline claiming she had tried to escape from her crate. history.draft:
Frank Romano, a distraught pet owner, is desperately searching for his six-year-old brindle and white Bull Terrier, Ty, after the dog mysteriously disappeared during a flight from Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) to Tampa on Friday night. According to Romano, Ty was traveling in a specially designed kennel purchased from PetCo, which was placed in the cargo area of the Delta Airlines flight. However, when Romano boarded the plane with his family, a Delta employee informed him that Ty had escaped from the kennel approximately an hour prior, claiming the dog had chewed through the metal door and hard plastic walls.

Romano expressed skepticism about the explanation, citing the durability of the kennel and questioning why the employee did not notify him immediately about the incident. Furthermore, he was assured that the empty kennel would be available for collection at baggage claim, but it never materialized. Despite searching the airport and contacting Delta multiple times, Romano has yet to receive any concrete answers or updates on Ty's whereabouts.

This incident is not an isolated case; Delta Airlines has a history of mishandling pets in their care. A Change.org petition with over 2,000 signatures demands accountability from the airline due to numerous instances of lost or injured animals. One such case involved a New York man who attempted to transport a puppy from Alabama to New York via Delta in 2011, only to have the dog temporarily misplaced in Atlanta. Another instance in 2011 saw a German Shepherd, destined for Germany, escape in Atlanta and ultimately die after being struck by a vehicle.

In 2010, Delta experienced a series of blunders, including misrouting two children between Boston and Cleveland, losing a dog en route from Mexico to Canada, and failing to provide adequate care for a puppy that arrived in California instead of Maine. The airline's handling of these incidents has been met with widespread criticism, with some owners receiving inadequate compensation or assistance.

Delta's response to these incidents has been inconsistent, with the airline initially providing vague explanations and eventually offering refunds or vouchers as a form of restitution. However, the emotional toll of losing a beloved pet cannot be measured by monetary compensation alone. As Romano's desperate search for Ty continues, the lack of transparency and accountability from Delta Airlines raises serious concerns about the safety and well-being of animals in their care.

In recent years, Delta has faced further scrutiny for their pet transportation practices. In 2010, a German Shepherd mix named Nala was lost during a flight from the United States to Germany, with the airline claiming she had tried to escape from her crate. Her body was later discovered on a highway, sparking outrage among animal welfare advocates. Additionally, a family's two dogs were misrouted during a flight from Germany to Washington state, resulting in the death of one dog and the prolonged separation of the other.

The cumulative effect of these incidents highlights the need for Delta to conduct a thorough internal investigation and revamp their policies and procedures regarding animal transport. Until then, pet owners like Romano will continue to face uncertainty and heartbreak when entrusting their beloved companions to the airline.

reference: – A man is trying to find his best friend after Delta Airlines lost track of the 6-year-old bull terrier Friday night. "I keep praying and wishing for him to come back," Frank Romano tells ABC News. Delta has told him they're looking for the dog, Ty, who seems to have gone missing at LAX when Romano and his family were headed to Tampa. After Romano boarded the plane, Delta staff told him Ty was missing, having bitten through the door of his kennel. Romano finds that confusing "when it had a metal door and was made of hard plastic," he tells the LAist. He searched the airport without finding the dog. The next day, he and his family headed to Tampa, where he is now. Meanwhile, he says he's received mixed reports from Delta, who at first told him they'd found the dog; then, "a few minutes later," said they hadn't. "They give us the run-around," he says. "Even gave us a number to the cargo place at Tampa airport saying he was there. We called and (they) said they had no dogs." Delta has lost enough dogs in the past to prompt a Change.org petition, LAist notes, pointing to three different incidents. "If I could I would go back to LA in a heartbeat," Romano says. "I bet I could find Ty faster than Delta can."

final_summary:
At 19 years old, Demi Lovato reached rock bottom after checking into a rehab facility in Illinois for emotional and physical issues in 2010. Despite receiving treatment and support, she continued to struggle with addiction, revealing that she would often sneak cocaine onto airplanes and use it in the lavatory. Lovato confessed that she was addicted to cocaine and would experience withdrawal symptoms within 30 minutes to an hour without it. She would go to great lengths to obtain the substance, including smuggling it onto planes while traveling with a sober companion who was tasked with monitoring her 24/7. This level of manipulation and deception was a hallmark of her addiction, which she described as a disease that made her a skilled liar and manipulator.

In addition to her struggles with cocaine, Lovato also spoke about her history of alcohol abuse, recalling a particularly disturbing incident where she filled a Sprite bottle with vodka and drank it on the way to a flight, only to vomit in the car. This behavior, she acknowledged, was indicative of a deeper problem - she was exhibiting classic signs of alcoholic behavior, and it was no longer just about rebellion or fun. Lovato's mother, Dianna, also shared her own experiences with addiction and mental health issues, revealing that she had struggled with an eating disorder for many years and had been in denial about her daughter's struggles with addiction. Dianna recalled feeling torn between believing her daughter's denials and recognizing the warning signs of substance abuse.

On Monday, Lovato opened up about her struggles in an exclusive interview with Access Hollywood's Kit Hoover from the set of "The X Factor." Lovato's mother, Dianna, also sat down with Kit, recounting her own journey towards recovery. Dianna had struggled with an eating disorder for many, many years and had been in denial about her daughter's struggles with addiction. She credited an intervention by loved ones with prompting her to seek treatment, and she eventually became closer to her daughter as they both worked through their respective struggles. Following Demi's 2010 inpatient rehab stint, the young star left with a greater understanding of the challenges her mom was also facing. As a child star who rose to fame on Disney, Lovato has since become a vocal advocate for mental health awareness and addiction recovery.

In a candid interview with Access Hollywood's Kit Hoover, Demi Lovato, a 21-year-old singer and "X Factor" coach, has spoken publicly about her struggles with substance abuse and addiction. At the age of 19, Lovato reached rock bottom after checking into a rehab facility in Illinois for emotional and physical issues in 2010. However, despite receiving treatment and support, she continued to struggle with addiction, revealing that she would often sneak cocaine onto airplanes and use it in the lavatory.

Lovato confessed that she was addicted to cocaine and would experience withdrawal symptoms within 30 minutes to an hour without it. She would go to great lengths to obtain the substance, including smuggling it onto planes while traveling with a sober companion who was tasked with monitoring her 24/7. This level of manipulation and deception was a hallmark of her addiction, which she described as a "disease" that made her a skilled liar and manipulator.

In addition to her struggles with cocaine, Lovato also spoke about her history of alcohol abuse, recalling a particularly disturbing incident where she filled a Sprite bottle with vodka and drank it on the way to a flight, only to vomit in the car. This behavior, she acknowledged, was indicative of a deeper problem - she was exhibiting classic signs of alcoholic behavior, and it was no longer just about rebellion or fun.

Lovato's mother, Dianna, also shared her own experiences with addiction and mental health issues, revealing that she had struggled with an eating disorder for many years and had been in denial about her daughter's struggles with addiction. Dianna recalled feeling torn between believing her daughter's denials and recognizing the warning signs of substance abuse. Ultimately, both Demi and her mother sought help and support, and their relationship grew stronger as a result.

Dianna's own journey towards recovery involved seeking help for her eating disorder and depression, which she had been struggling with for many years. She credited an intervention by loved ones with prompting her to seek treatment, and she eventually became closer to her daughter as they both worked through their respective struggles. Demi expressed her gratitude and admiration for her mother's strength and resilience, stating that she loves and is proud of her.
